Coffee is an agricultural product, and like wine it carries the signature of a plant, a place, and a process. The chain runs roughly like this: 1. The plant — which species and Coffee Varietals of Coffea was grown. See The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta and Coffee Varietals. 2. Terroir — the origin, altitude, soil, and climate that fed it. See Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir. 3. Processing — how the fruit was turned into a dry, storable green seed. See Coffee Processing Methods, Washed Process, Natural Process, and Honey and Experimental Processing. 4. Roasting — the heat that develops aroma, acidity, and body from raw green coffee. See Coffee Roasting Explained, Roast Levels for Pour Over, and Light Roast and Specialty Coffee. 5. Freshness — how the roasted bean ages, degasses, and is best stored. See Coffee Freshness and Degassing.

sections.h: Why It All Matters for Pour Over
sections.t: Pour over is a transparent brewing method — it hides nothing. A clean Washed Process Kenyan and a wild Natural Process Ethiopian will taste like different beverages through the same Hario V60, and a Roast Levels for Pour Over demands more from your Grind Size for Pour Over and Water Temperature for Brewing than a dark one. Understanding the bean lets you read a cup backward to its source and forward to a better Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes. [!tip] Where to start If you only read two notes here, make them Roast Levels for Pour Over (because roast most directly affects how you brew) and Reading a Coffee Bag Label (because it teaches you to decode everything else at the point of purchase).

Stage Note What it covers Plant The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta The two commercial species Variety Coffee Varietals Bourbon, Geisha, SL28 and cup character Place Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir Origin and regional flavor Process Coffee Processing Methods How cherry becomes green bean Roast Coffee Roasting Explained The chemistry of roasting Buying Single Origin vs Blends What kind of coffee to choose The most important myth to retire early: darker does not mean stronger, and "100% Arabica" is not a guarantee of quality — both claims are unpacked in The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta and Roast Levels for Pour Over.

title: The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta

sections.t: Coffee comes from the seeds of Coffea , a genus of tropical evergreen shrubs that produce small fruits called cherries . Inside each cherry sit two green seeds — the beans we eventually roast. Of the 120 plus known Coffea species, only two matter commercially: Coffea arabica (Arabica) and Coffea canephora (Robusta). Understanding their differences explains why specialty Coffee Beans and Roast is almost always Arabica.

sections.h: Arabica vs Robusta at a Glance
sections.t: Trait Arabica Robusta Share of world crop 60% 40% Caffeine 1.2–1.5% 2.2–2.7% Chlorogenic acids Lower Higher (more bitter) Sugars / lipids Higher Lower Typical flavor Sweet, complex, bright Acidity in Coffee\ Earthy, woody, harsh, "rubbery" Growing altitude High (1,000–2,200 m) Low (0–800 m) Disease resistance Low High Chromosomes 44 (tetraploid, self pollinating) 22 (diploid)

Arabica's higher sugar and lipid content and gentler acidity give it the floral, fruity, and sweet character that pour over is designed to showcase. Grown slowly at altitude — see Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir — its beans develop dense, complex precursors that survive into a clean cup. The vast world of Coffee Varietals (Bourbon, Typica, Geisha and more) exists almost entirely within Arabica. Robusta's appeal is agronomic, not sensory: it resists the leaf rust and borers that plague Arabica, tolerates heat and lowland conditions, and yields more per plant. Its extra caffeine and chlorogenic acids make it bitter, which is why it dominates instant coffee and traditional Single Origin vs Blends (for crema and punch) far more than filter brewing. [!warning] The "100% Arabica" myth A label reading "100% Arabica" is a statement about species, not quality. Plenty of cheap, poorly grown, stale, or Identifying Defects and Off Flavors Arabica exists. Meanwhile, a growing movement of fine Robusta is producing genuinely clean, sweet lots. Species is one signal among many on a Reading a Coffee Bag Label — not a verdict.

Because Arabica is self pollinating with low genetic diversity, it is fragile in the face of disease and climate change — a central concern for Sustainability in Coffee. Breeders increasingly cross Arabica with Robusta to create hardy hybrids (like Castillo or the Sarchimor group) that aim to keep Arabica's cup while borrowing Robusta's resilience. These appear in Coffee Varietals and matter for Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing.

If The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta is the broad category, a varietal (more precisely a cultivar ) is the specific genetic line within it — coffee's equivalent of a grape variety like Pinot Noir or Chardonnay. Variety helps determine a coffee's potential flavor, yield, and disease resistance, though it always works in concert with Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir, Coffee Processing Methods, and Coffee Roasting Explained.

Almost every prized variety descends from two ancestral Ethiopian derived lines, Typica and Bourbon , which spread through colonial trade and then mutated and crossbred across the coffee world. Variety Lineage Cup character Typica Ancestral Clean, sweet, classic; low yield Bourbon Ancestral mutation Sweet, balanced, syrupy body Caturra Bourbon dwarf mutation Bright, citric, high yielding Geisha / Gesha Ethiopian landrace Floral, jasmine, bergamot, tea like SL28 / SL34 Kenyan selections Intense blackcurrant, juicy Acidity in Coffee\ Pacamara Pacas × Maragogipe Big bodied, complex, savory sweet Castillo / Sarchimor Arabica × The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta\ hybrids Disease resistant; cup varies

Geisha (Gesha) is the variety that rewired specialty coffee. When Panama's Hacienda La Esmeralda showcased it in 2004, its astonishing floral, tea like clarity shattered auction records and proved variety could be a headline act. It remains the benchmark for The World Brewers Cup pour over. SL28 and SL34 , selected in 1930s Kenya, deliver the electric blackcurrant acidity that defines great Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir coffee. Bourbon and its mutations (Caturra, Pacas, Villa Sarchi) form the sweet backbone of Latin American specialty. [!note] Landraces vs cultivars In Ethiopia — coffee's genetic homeland — thousands of wild and semi wild types grow side by side. These are often labeled "heirloom" or landrace rather than a single named cultivar, because the genetics are a diverse mix rather than one clean line. [!warning] Variety is potential, not destiny A Geisha grown at low altitude and over cropped can taste ordinary, while a humble Caturra grown with care can sing. Treat variety as one input to flavor — see how it interacts with Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes and How to Taste Coffee before crediting genetics alone.

sections.h: Why It Matters for the Cup
sections.t: For a pour over drinker, variety is a flavor forecast. A bag listing SL28 primes you to expect bright, juicy acidity (and perhaps a slightly finer Grind Size for Pour Over to tame it); a Geisha invites a gentle, low Agitation and Turbulence brew to preserve its delicate florals. Learning to connect names on a Reading a Coffee Bag Label to expectations in the cup is a core palate skill — see Building Your Palate.

Terroir — borrowed from wine — is the idea that place leaves a fingerprint on flavor. A coffee's origin, altitude, soil, rainfall, and climate all shape the chemistry of the bean before it is ever Coffee Processing Methods or Coffee Roasting Explained. Combined with Coffee Varietals, terroir is why a Kenyan and a Colombian can taste like different fruits entirely.

Coffee grows in a band roughly between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn, where the climate is warm but tempered by altitude. Higher elevation is the single most important terroir factor for The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta: cooler temperatures slow the cherry's maturation, letting sugars and acids concentrate into denser, more complex beans. This is why bags proudly print altitude (often as MASL , metres above sea level) — see Reading a Coffee Bag Label. [!note] What "hard bean" means Terms like SHB (Strictly Hard Bean) or SHG (Strictly High Grown) on Central American coffees simply mean the beans grew above 1,350 m. The cooler, slower growth makes them physically denser — which also means they often need a hotter Water Temperature for Brewing and a touch finer Grind Size for Pour Over.

These are useful generalizations, not laws — processing and variety can override them. Region Origin examples Typical character East Africa Ethiopia, Kenya Floral, citrus, berry, bright Acidity in Coffee\ Central America Guatemala, Costa Rica Balanced, chocolate, caramel, clean South America Colombia, Brazil Nutty, cocoa, soft acidity, full Sweetness and Body\ Asia / Pacific Sumatra, Java, PNG Earthy, herbal, syrupy, low acidity Ethiopia , coffee's birthplace, gives delicate jasmine and stone fruit cups when Washed Process and explosive blueberry when Natural Process. Kenya delivers blackcurrant intensity thanks to Coffee Varietals and meticulous washing. Brazil , the world's largest producer, leans nutty and chocolatey with low acidity — a backbone for Single Origin vs Blends. Sumatra's distinctive earthy body comes partly from its unusual giling basah (wet hulled) processing, not terroir alone. [!warning] Terroir is collaborative, not deterministic It is a myth that origin alone decides flavor. A famously "fruity" region produces clean, tea like lots when Washed Process and grown high; a "balanced" region can taste wild when Natural Process processed. Always read terroir alongside Coffee Processing Methods and Coffee Varietals.

sections.h: Why It Matters for Pour Over
sections.t: Because pour over is so transparent, terroir comes through vividly. Knowing a coffee's origin helps you anticipate its acidity and body and dial a Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes accordingly — a bright Kenyan loves a higher The Brew Ratio to keep it juicy, while an earthy Sumatran can take a heavier hand. This is also where Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing and Sustainability in Coffee meet the cup, since terroir is also a community and a livelihood.

Between a ripe coffee cherry picked off a tree and a green bean ready to Coffee Roasting Explained lies processing — the post harvest work of removing the fruit and drying the seed. It sounds like mere logistics, but it is one of the most powerful flavor decisions in coffee, often shaping the cup as much as Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir or Coffee Varietals. This note maps the landscape; the detailed methods each have their own page.

To understand processing you have to know what is being removed. From the outside in: 1. Skin (exocarp) — the cherry's outer fruit skin. 2. Pulp (mesocarp) — sweet, sticky fruit flesh, including the mucilage . 3. Parchment (endocarp) — a papery hull around the seed. 4. Silverskin — a thin membrane (becomes Coffee Roasting Explained chaff). 5. The seed — the green bean itself. Processing decides how much fruit stays in contact with the seed, and for how long, while it dries. More fruit contact generally means more sweetness, body, and fermentation driven flavor; less means more clarity and cleaner Acidity in Coffee.

Method What happens Cup tendency Washed Process\ Fruit removed before drying Clean, bright, transparent Natural Process\ Cherry dried whole , fruit on Fruity, heavy, wild Honey and Experimental Processing\ Some mucilage left on to dry Between the two; rounded sweetness These three sit on a spectrum of fruit contact, from least (washed) to most (natural). The modern frontier — anaerobic and carbonic maceration ferments — pushes that spectrum further and is covered in Honey and Experimental Processing. [!note] Fermentation is the hidden hero Nearly every method involves controlled fermentation , where microbes break down sugars and create new flavor compounds. Done well it adds complexity and sweetness; done poorly it creates Identifying Defects and Off Flavors like ferment y, boozy, or vinegary off notes. Process is a balance of intention and risk.

sections.h: Why It Matters for Pour Over
sections.t: Because pour over is a high clarity brewing method, it reveals process character with unusual honesty. A Washed Process coffee gives a crisp, see through cup that flatters a delicate Coffee Varietals; a Natural Process brings jammy fruit and heavier Sweetness and Body. Knowing the process on a Reading a Coffee Bag Label tells you what to expect and how to brew — naturals and experimentals often benefit from gentler Agitation and Turbulence to keep their intensity from tipping into muddiness. [!warning] Don't assume "natural = better" Process is a stylistic choice, not a quality ranking. Some of the world's cleanest, most expensive coffees are washed; some are natural. Match the process to the flavors you enjoy.

The washed (or wet ) process is the clarity benchmark of coffee. By removing all the fruit before the bean dries, it strips away the variables of Natural Process fruit drying and lets the underlying Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir and Coffee Varietals speak for themselves. It is the dominant method in Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir, much of Central America, and washed Ethiopia — and the style most associated with crisp, transparent pour over.

1. Pulping — ripe cherries pass through a machine that removes skin and most pulp, leaving sticky mucilage on the parchment. 2. Fermentation — the beans sit in tanks (often 12–72 hours) while microbes and enzymes break down the remaining mucilage. This is a controlled, watchful step. 3. Washing — beans are rinsed in channels or tanks until the parchment is clean and slick. (Sorting often happens here by density.) 4. Drying — the clean parchment coffee dries on raised beds or patios down to 10–12% moisture before being hulled into green beans. [!note] Why it tastes "clean" Because no fruit dries in contact with the seed, washed coffees carry less fermentation driven body and more of the bean's intrinsic Acidity in Coffee\. The result reads as transparent, bright, and well defined — a window onto origin rather than a fruit cocktail.

Attribute Washed tendency Acidity High, crisp, structured Body Lighter, cleaner Sweetness Refined rather than jammy Clarity Very high Typical notes Citrus, floral, stone fruit, tea A washed Ethiopian floats jasmine and bergamot; a washed Kenyan delivers laser cut blackcurrant from Coffee Varietals. These are coffees that reward a careful, even Pouring Technique and reveal small changes in your Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes.

Their bright Acidity in Coffee and lighter Sweetness and Body make washed lots a natural fit for pour over, but the same clarity that flatters them also exposes mistakes. Aim for even extraction: a uniform Grind Size for Pour Over, adequate Water Temperature for Brewing, and controlled Agitation and Turbulence keep the cup sweet rather than sharp. Because washed coffees are often Light Roast and Specialty Coffee to preserve their delicacy, Under Extraction and Over Extraction is the most common failure — pull the Grind Size for Pour Over finer if it tastes sour and thin. [!warning] Water intensive Traditional washing uses large volumes of fresh water, raising real Sustainability in Coffee concerns. Many producers now use eco pulpers and water recycling, and some skip the fermentation tank entirely (mechanical demucilaging). Process names on a Reading a Coffee Bag Label sometimes hide these variations.

The natural (or dry ) process is the oldest method of all — coffee was processed this way for centuries before water washing existed — and it produces the wildest, fruitiest cups in coffee. Instead of stripping the fruit away as in the Washed Process, the whole cherry is dried intact, so the seed marinates in its own pulp. The result is heavy, sweet, and unmistakably fruity , which is why naturals have become specialty pour over's most polarizing crowd pleasers.

1. Sorting — ripe cherries are floated and sorted; underripe or damaged ones are removed (crucial, since defects concentrate in this method). 2. Drying whole — cherries are spread on raised beds or patios and dried slowly for two to four weeks , raked and turned constantly to prevent mold and uneven drying. 3. Fermentation in the fruit — as the cherry dries, sugars in the pulp ferment and migrate into the seed, building intense fruit and winey notes. 4. Hulling — once dry, the brittle husk is removed in one step to reveal the green bean. [!note] Where naturals shine Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir\ (especially Yirgacheffe and Sidamo) and Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir\ are the classic homes of natural processing — Ethiopia for sunny, dry harvest weather and a tradition of bold fruit, Brazil because its lower acid coffees gain sweetness and body from the method.

Attribute Natural tendency Acidity Lower, rounder Body Heavy, syrupy Sweetness Big, jammy, fruit forward Clarity Lower than washed Typical notes Blueberry, strawberry, wine, rum, tropical fruit A great natural Ethiopian can taste like blueberry jam; a Brazilian natural leans toward chocolate, nuts, and ripe red fruit. These coffees feel weightier on the palate — see Sweetness and Body.

[!warning] Higher reward, higher risk Because the fruit ferments directly against the seed for weeks, naturals walk a knife edge. Poorly managed drying produces ferment y, boozy, vinegary, or "rotten fruit" off flavors and inconsistency from bean to bean. What one drinker calls "vibrant and fruity" another calls "fermented and unclean." Trust your own palate — see Identifying Defects and Off Flavors.

Naturals are intense, so the goal is to keep that intensity from turning muddy. Use gentle Agitation and Turbulence and a slightly coarser Grind Size for Pour Over to avoid over extracting their already heavy compounds, and consider a marginally lower Water Temperature for Brewing. A clean Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth helps preserve clarity. Because their fruit forward sweetness is the whole point, dial your Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes toward highlighting it rather than chasing acidity.

Between the clean Washed Process and the wild Natural Process sits a growing world of honey and experimental methods that deliberately tune fermentation and fruit contact to engineer specific flavors. This is the modern frontier of coffee — exciting, sometimes spectacular, and genuinely controversial.

In honey processing the cherry skin is removed (as in washed) but some or all of the sticky mucilage is left on the bean to dry (as in natural). That mucilage — never actual honey — gives the method its name and a sweetness and body that splits the difference between the two extremes. Producers grade it by how much mucilage remains and how much sun the beans get: Honey type Mucilage left Cup tendency White / Yellow Least Closer to washed; clean, light sweetness Red Medium Balanced, syrupy, rounded Black Most Closer to natural; heavy, fruity, intense Common in Costa Rica and El Salvador, honey coffees offer the rounded Sweetness and Body many pour over drinkers love without going as far as a full natural.

The last decade has seen producers borrow tools from winemaking and brewing to control fermentation precisely: Anaerobic fermentation — cherries ferment in sealed, oxygen free tanks , often pressurized, producing intense, sometimes funky tropical and boozy notes. Carbonic maceration — borrowed from Beaujolais wine, whole cherries ferment in CO2 saturated tanks, yielding vivid, candy like fruit. Thermal shock, inoculated yeasts, co fermentation — adding specific microbes or even fruit/spices to steer flavor. [!example] Why competitors love them Many recent The World Brewers Cup\ routines feature anaerobic or carbonic lots precisely because they deliver loud, memorable, easily described flavors that score well — see Competition Recipes.

[!warning] Process flavor vs coffee flavor Critics argue that aggressive ferments can mask terroir and variety , making a Colombian and an Ethiopian taste like the same "tutti frutti" novelty. Some co fermented lots blur the line between coffee and a flavored beverage, raising questions of transparency on the Reading a Coffee Bag Label\. Inconsistency and runaway fermentation also risk Identifying Defects and Off Flavors\. Whether these coffees represent the future or a fad is a live debate in The Specialty Coffee Industry\.

Experimental lots are usually bold and aromatic , so restraint pays. Favor gentle Agitation and Turbulence and a clean Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth to keep the cup articulate, and treat them like intense Natural Process when dialing your Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes. They are best enjoyed Light Roast and Specialty Coffee, which preserves the very ferment driven aromatics they were made for.

Green coffee is nearly flavorless — grassy, dense, and inert. Roasting is the application of heat that transforms it, through a cascade of chemical reactions, into the aromatic brown bean we grind and brew. It is the bridge between the Coffee Processing Methods green seed and a finished cup, and understanding it demystifies why Roast Levels for Pour Over so strongly affects your brewing.

A roast is a controlled journey from around room temperature to roughly 195–230°C over 8–15 minutes. It moves through distinct phases: 1. Drying phase — the bean sheds moisture (from 10–12% down toward 2–4%). It turns yellow and smells of toast and grass. Endothermic — it absorbs heat. 2. Maillard reaction — amino acids and sugars react to create hundreds of new aroma compounds and brown the bean. This is the same browning that crusts bread and sears steak, and it builds much of coffee's complexity, body, and savory depth. 3. Caramelization — sugars break down, developing sweetness, caramel, and color while reducing perceived Acidity in Coffee. 4. First crack — around 196°C the bean audibly pops as built up steam and CO2 escape and cell walls fracture. This marks the start of drinkable, developed coffee. 5. Development — the stretch after first crack where the roaster balances acidity, sweetness, and body. Longer development = darker, rounder, less acidic. 6. Second crack — a quieter, oilier crackle deep into dark roast territory, where oils migrate to the surface and roast (smoky, bitter) character dominates over origin. [!note] First crack is the pivot Almost every flavor decision in roasting happens around first crack. Drop the roast soon after and you preserve bright, complex origin character (a Light Roast and Specialty Coffee\); carry it well past and you trade origin for body and roast flavor.

As roast progresses... Acidity Sweetness Body Origin character Lighter High Building Lighter Preserved Medium Balanced Peak Fuller Balanced Darker Low Caramelizes then burns Heavy then thin Lost to roast Roasting also makes the bean brittle and porous , which is what lets you grind it and lets water The Science of Extraction flavor. And it generates CO2 , which slowly escapes over the following days — the basis of Coffee Freshness and Degassing and the The Bloom. [!warning] Roast defects are real Heat can go wrong. Baking (too slow) flattens flavor; scorching or tipping (too hot, too fast) adds ashy, burnt notes; underdevelopment leaves grassy, sour, astringent cups. These are distinct from green coffee faults — see Identifying Defects and Off Flavors.

sections.h: Why It Matters for Pour Over
sections.t: Roast sets the table for everything you do at the brewer. A lighter roast is denser and less soluble, so it generally needs hotter Water Temperature for Brewing, a finer Grind Size for Pour Over, and more Agitation and Turbulence to extract well; a darker roast is the opposite. Roast is the single bean variable that most changes your Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes — which is why it gets its own deep dive in Roast Levels for Pour Over.

Roast level is the bean variable that most directly changes how you brew . It runs along a continuous spectrum from light to dark, set by how far past Coffee Roasting Explained the roaster takes the bean. For pour over and filter brewing in general, the specialty world leans light to medium — and this note explains why, and how roast reshapes The Science of Extraction.

Roast Color Acidity Body Best showcases Common use Light Light brown, dry High, bright Lighter Origin, Coffee Varietals\, Coffee Processing Methods\ Specialty filter Medium Mid brown, dry Balanced Fuller Balance, sweetness Versatile, blends Medium dark Dark brown, faint sheen Lower Heavy Chocolate, caramel Espresso, diner drip Dark Very dark, oily Minimal Roast dominated Roast flavor itself Espresso, dark roast fans

sections.h: Why Filter Brewing Favors Lighter Roasts ✨
sections.t: Pour over is a high clarity, origin revealing method, and its whole purpose is to showcase what makes a coffee distinctive — the floral Coffee Varietals, the blackcurrant Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir, the blueberry Natural Process. Lighter roasts preserve those delicate, Aroma and Flavor Compounds and acidic compounds that darker roasting burns away. Conversely, dark roasts mute origin in favor of generic "roasty" flavor, which is better suited to milk drinks and AeroPress than to a transparent filter cup. This is why most The Specialty Coffee Industry sell their single origins at a filter friendly light to medium level.

[!tip] Lighter roast = harder to extract Lighter roasts are denser, less porous, and less soluble because they spent less time developing. To get enough flavor out, they generally want hotter Water Temperature for Brewing\ (often 93–96°C), a finer Grind Size for Pour Over\, and more Agitation and Turbulence\ . Darker roasts are brittle and very soluble, so they extract fast and easily — back off with cooler water, a coarser grind, and gentler pours to avoid bitterness. This single relationship — roast level driving temperature, grind, and agitation — is one of the most useful mental models in all of brewing. When you switch beans and your Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes suddenly tastes off, roast level is the first thing to check. [!warning] Two persistent myths 1. "Dark roast = strong / high caffeine." False. Extraction Yield and Strength\ is set by your The Brew Ratio\, not roast color, and dark roasts actually have slightly less caffeine by mass than light. 2. "Oily beans are fresh." Surface oil signals a dark roast (and often advancing staleness), not freshness — see Coffee Freshness and Degassing.

For pour over, start with a light to medium single origin from a roaster who prints a Reading a Coffee Bag Label. If your cups come out thin and sour, you may be under extracting a light roast — go finer and hotter. If they come out flat and bitter, you may be over roasted or over extracting — go coarser and cooler. See Under Extraction and Over Extraction and Dialing In Grind Size.

Over the last two decades, light roasting went from a curiosity to the defining house style of The Specialty Coffee Industry. Championed by Scandinavian roasters and spread by the The Specialty Coffee Movement, the light roast philosophy treats coffee like fine wine: the roaster's job is to reveal the bean, not to stamp it with roast flavor. For pour over drinkers this is both a flavor revolution and a technical challenge.

sections.h: What Light Roasting Is Trying to Do 🎯
sections.t: The goal is transparency — showcasing the Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir, Coffee Varietals, and Coffee Processing Methods that make a specific lot unique. By stopping the roast not long after Coffee Roasting Explained, the roaster preserves: High, complex Acidity in Coffee — the bright, juicy quality specialty prizes. Delicate Aroma and Flavor Compounds — florals, fruit, tea like notes that dark roasting destroys. Origin clarity — so a Yirgacheffe tastes like Yirgacheffe, not "coffee." [!note] "Nordic roast" The very light end of the spectrum is often called a Nordic or Scandinavian roast, associated with roasters like Tim Wendelboe. These coffees are dropped barely past first crack — maximally origin forward, minimally roast forward.

[!warning] Light roasts are harder to brew well Because they are denser and less soluble than darker roasts (see Roast Levels for Pour Over), light roasts are prone to Under Extraction and Over Extraction\ — they come out sour, thin, grassy, and astringent when you don't pull enough flavor out of them. This is the single most common frustration for newcomers to specialty coffee. The fixes all push extraction up : Lever Direction for light roasts Water Temperature for Brewing\ Hotter — often 93–96°C, sometimes full boil Grind Size for Pour Over\ Finer than you'd use for a dark roast Agitation and Turbulence\ More — stir, swirl, or pour with energy Brew Time and Total Contact Time\ Longer drawdowns help The Brew Ratio\ A touch higher ratio keeps acidity bright and clean Techniques built for these beans — the Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method and James Hoffmann V60 Technique among them — emphasize even, complete extraction. A refractometer (see Refractometers and Measuring Extraction) helps confirm you are hitting a healthy Extraction Yield and Strength.

[!warning] A matter of taste, not virtue The specialty world sometimes implies light roast is objectively "correct," but that is a stylistic stance, not a fact. Many excellent coffees are roasted to a balanced medium for sweetness and body, and plenty of drinkers genuinely prefer that. Light roasting also exposes every flaw — green coffee Identifying Defects and Off Flavors and brewing mistakes have nowhere to hide. Brew to the cup you enjoy.

A perfectly grown, processed, and roasted coffee can still brew badly if it is too young, too old, or poorly stored. Freshness is a moving target: roasted coffee needs a few days to settle before it brews well, then a few weeks of peak, then a slow slide into staleness. Understanding degassing — the release of CO2 created during Coffee Roasting Explained — is the key to timing it.

Coffee Roasting Explained traps large amounts of carbon dioxide inside the porous bean. Over the days after roasting, that CO2 slowly escapes — this is degassing . It matters for brewing because excess CO2: creates the vigorous The Bloom when hot water first hits the grounds, and if there is too much of it, repels water and causes uneven, Under Extraction and Over Extraction cups. That is why fresh off the roaster coffee tastes oddly thin and gassy, and why resting improves it. See CO2, Degassing, and the Bloom Science for the deeper mechanism. [!tip] Optimal rest after roast A good rule of thumb for filter roasts : rest whole beans 4–14 days after the roast date before brewing pour over. Lighter roasts often peak a little later (a week or more); darker roasts degas faster and peak sooner. Trust your palate — if the bloom is explosive and the cup tastes hollow, give it a few more days.

Age after roast State What to expect 0–3 days Too gassy Erratic bloom, thin, "green" cup 4–14 days Peak Sweet, aromatic, balanced 2–5 weeks Good Slowly fading; still enjoyable 6+ weeks Staling Flat, papery, dull; lost aromatics

sections.h: Staling and How to Slow It
sections.t: Three enemies degrade roasted coffee: oxygen (oxidation, the main cause of stale, cardboard flavors), moisture , and heat/light . Aromatics escape and lipids go rancid over time, leaving a flat, lifeless cup no Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes can fix. [!tip] Storage best practice Buy whole bean and grind just before brewing — ground coffee stales in minutes to hours because of the huge surface area (see Grind Size and Surface Area). Keep beans in an airtight, opaque container at room temperature, away from heat and light. One way valve bags let CO2 out while keeping O2 out — store coffee in its bag, sealed, if it has a valve. Buy in quantities you'll finish in 3–4 weeks . [!warning] Two freshness myths 1. "Freeze your daily coffee." Long term freezing of unopened, well sealed coffee can preserve it, but repeatedly taking a bag in and out of the freezer invites condensation and moisture damage. 2. "Oily beans are fresh." Surface oil indicates a dark roast or advancing staleness, not freshness — see Roast Levels for Pour Over.

sections.h: Why It Matters for Pour Over
sections.t: Freshness shows up most visibly in the The Bloom: a lively dome of foam means active degassing and a recently roasted bean, while a flat, lifeless bloom suggests stale coffee. Always check the roast date on the bag — see Reading a Coffee Bag Label — and prefer roasters who print it over those who hide behind a distant "best before" date.

A specialty coffee bag is a dense little dossier. Learning to read it lets you predict how a coffee will taste and how to brew it before you ever open the bag — and it teaches you to spot the roasters who value transparency over marketing. This note ties together everything in the Coffee Beans and Roast domain at the point of purchase.

Label field What it tells you See Origin Country, region, often farm/washing station Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir Altitude (MASL) Density and likely Acidity in Coffee\ Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir Variety Genetic flavor potential Coffee Varietals Process Washed / natural / honey / experimental Coffee Processing Methods Roast level How they roasted it (filter vs espresso) Roast Levels for Pour Over Roast date Freshness — the most important date Coffee Freshness and Degassing Tasting notes The roaster's flavor forecast The Coffee Flavor Wheel

sections.h: Reading the Story the Label Tells
sections.t: The best labels let you reconstruct the Coffee Beans and Roast. A label reading "Ethiopia, Guji, 2,050 m, Heirloom, Natural, filter roast, roasted 12 May, notes of blueberry and jasmine" tells you to expect a fruit forward, Sweetness and Body, floral cup from a Natural Process Light Roast and Specialty Coffee — and to brew it gently. By contrast, a vague "Premium Dark Roast Blend, 100% Arabica" tells you almost nothing. [!tip] Roast date over "best before" The roast date is the single most useful thing on the bag. A roaster who prints it respects freshness; one who only prints a far off "best before" is hiding age. Aim to buy coffee roasted within the last couple of weeks — see Coffee Freshness and Degassing. [!warning] Marketing red flags "100% Arabica" is about species, not quality — see The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta. "Italian / French / Espresso roast" are roast style names, not origins, and usually mean dark. No roast date = treat as old until proven otherwise. "Strong / bold" describes Roast Levels for Pour Over\ marketing, not Extraction Yield and Strength\ (which your The Brew Ratio\ controls).

sections.h: Putting It to Use at the Brewer
sections.t: Once you've decoded the label, it should shape your Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes: a high altitude, lightly roasted washed coffee wants hotter Water Temperature for Brewing and a finer Grind Size for Pour Over; a natural or experimental lot wants gentler Agitation and Turbulence. The label is the bridge between buying well and brewing well — and a good Single Origin vs Blends label gives you far more of this information than a typical blend.

When you buy coffee, you face a basic fork: a single origin that showcases one place, or a blend that combines several for a designed effect. Pour over culture overwhelmingly favors single origin — but understanding both, and the marketing around them, makes you a sharper buyer.

[!note] What the terms actually mean Single origin means the coffee comes from one defined source — sometimes a whole country, sometimes a single farm, lot, or Coffee Varietals\. The tighter the definition (single farm or micro lot), the more traceable it is. Single estate or micro lot are the most specific. A blend is a deliberate mix of two or more coffees — different origins, Coffee Processing Methods\, or Coffee Varietals\ — combined by a Notable Roasters\ to hit a target flavor.

Single Origin Blend Goal Express one place / Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir\ Achieve a consistent, designed profile Character Distinctive, sometimes "spiky" Balanced, rounded, reliable Consistency Varies by harvest and lot Stable year round Traceability High Often lower Best for Exploration, pour over clarity Espresso, milk drinks, everyday Single origins are about discovery — the blackcurrant of a Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir, the jasmine of a washed Ethiopian, the blueberry of a Natural Process. Because they express one place, they shine through the transparent pour over method and reward the palate building work in How to Taste Coffee and Building Your Palate. Blends are about design and consistency . A roaster can balance a bright coffee against a heavy one to build sweetness, Sweetness and Body, and a flavor that stays the same all year — invaluable for espresso, where consistency and crema matter, and for crowd pleasing house coffees.

sections.h: Why Pour Over Culture Favors Single Origin
sections.t: The whole point of pour over is clarity and transparency — revealing what makes a coffee unique. That aligns naturally with single origin, where there is a single, traceable story to reveal. The The Specialty Coffee Movement and Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing also champion single origin because traceability supports better prices for farmers and honest Reading a Coffee Bag Label. For learning, single origins are simply more instructive : each one teaches you something specific about Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir, Coffee Varietals, or Coffee Processing Methods. [!warning] Neither is "better" It is a myth that blends are inherently inferior. A thoughtfully built blend can be more delicious, sweet, and consistent than a one dimensional single origin — and many legendary espresso coffees are blends. Conversely, "single origin" guarantees nothing about quality: a poorly grown, Identifying Defects and Off Flavors single origin is still bad coffee. Judge by the cup, not the category.

Grinding is the act of reducing roasted whole beans to particles ready for brewing — and it is arguably the single most important variable a pour over brewer controls. Grinding determines the surface area the water meets, and surface area governs the speed of The Science of Extraction. Change nothing but your grind and you can swing a cup from sour and thin to bitter and muddy. This is the home of everything about particle size, the machines that produce it, and how to wield it.

sections.h: Why Grind Sits at the Center 🎯
sections.t: Of all the levers in a pour over — The Brew Ratio, Water Temperature for Brewing, Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity, Pouring Technique — grind is the one most directly tied to extraction rate. A finer grind exposes more Grind Size and Surface Area and slows the water's path, raising Extraction Yield and Strength; a coarser grind does the reverse. Just as crucial is consistency : a grinder that produces evenly sized particles extracts evenly, while one that produces a chaotic mix over and under extracts at the same time. For that reason a good grinder usually does more for cup quality than an expensive dripper. [!tip] Grind first, then everything else When dialing in a new coffee, hold ratio, water, and technique steady and adjust grind alone. It is the cleanest single variable lever you have. See Dialing In Grind Size.

Topic Note The master lever Why Grind Size Matters Why burrs win Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders Burr geometry Conical vs Flat Burrs The pour over target Grind Size for Pour Over Even particles Particle Distribution and Uniformity The two extremes Fines and Boulders Practical method Dialing In Grind Size Manual vs electric Hand Grinders vs Electric Grinders

Grind never acts alone. It interacts with Roast Levels for Pour Over (darker roasts are more brittle and shatter into more fines), with the The Dripper Explained you choose, and with the whole Brewing Technique that follows. When a cup goes wrong, the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide will almost always send you back here first.

Grind size is the master lever of pour over because it sets the pace of The Science of Extraction more directly than any other single variable. When you grind a bean, you do two things at once: you create vast new surface area , and you shorten the distance water must travel to reach the soluble compounds locked inside. Both effects push flavor out of the coffee and into the cup faster. Understanding this one relationship is the key to dialing in almost any brew.

Extraction happens at the boundary between water and coffee. A whole bean has very little exposed surface, so it gives up flavor slowly. Crack that bean into hundreds of small particles and the total exposed surface area multiplies dramatically — even though the mass is unchanged. More surface means more contact, and more contact means faster dissolving of acids, sugars, and the heavier compounds that bring Sweetness and Body and bitterness. This is the core idea explored in depth in Grind Size and Surface Area. [!note] Smaller particles, bigger surface Halving particle diameter roughly doubles the surface area to volume ratio. That is why a small grinder adjustment can have an outsized effect on the cup.

Grind Surface area Flow rate Extraction Risk Finer Higher Slower Higher Under Extraction and Over Extraction\, bitterness Coarser Lower Faster Lower Under extraction, sourness Grind affects extraction through two mechanisms that reinforce each other. First, more surface area dissolves flavor faster. Second, finer particles pack the bed more tightly and slow the water down, increasing Brew Time and Total Contact Time. So going finer both speeds the chemistry and lengthens the brew — a double push toward higher Extraction Yield and Strength.

sections.h: The Master Lever for Dialing In
sections.t: Because grind moves extraction so reliably, it is the first thing to adjust when a cup tastes wrong. Sour, thin, and weak usually means under extracted — grind finer. Harsh, dry, and bitter usually means over extracted — grind coarser. Hold your The Brew Ratio and Water Temperature for Brewing constant and let grind do the work, as laid out in Dialing In Grind Size. [!warning] Myth: "finer always means stronger" Finer grind raises extraction , not necessarily strength . Strength (concentration / TDS) is set mainly by the The Brew Ratio. A very fine grind can even choke the bed, stall flow, and produce a weak, over extracted cup at the same time. Strength and extraction are distinct — see Extraction Yield and Strength.

Not all grinders are created equal — and the gap between a burr grinder and a blade grinder is the largest single equipment upgrade most home brewers can make. The difference comes down to one word: uniformity . Burrs produce particles of a controlled, consistent size; blades produce chaos. For pour over, where Particle Distribution and Uniformity is everything, that difference decides the cup.

A burr grinder crushes beans between two abrasive surfaces — either Conical vs Flat Burrs — set a fixed distance apart. A bean cannot fall through until it has been reduced to a size smaller than that gap, so every particle exits at roughly the same target size. You set the gap; the gap sets the grind. A blade grinder is really a tiny propeller. A spinning blade smashes beans by brute impact, flinging fragments in every direction. Some particles get pulverized to dust on the first hit; others bounce away nearly whole. The result is a wildly inconsistent mix, and the only "setting" is how long you hold the button. Burr grinder Blade grinder Mechanism Crushing at a fixed gap Random impact Size control Precise, repeatable None (timing only) Distribution Tight, mostly Particle Distribution and Uniformity\ Wide, Fines and Boulders\ Repeatability High — set and forget Low — never the same twice Pour over suitability Excellent Poor

The blade's core failure is that it produces an extreme spread of sizes simultaneously. The cloud of Fines and Boulders over extracts into bitterness while the Fines and Boulders sit barely touched and under extract into sourness — in the same cup . You cannot dial in what you cannot control, so Dialing In Grind Size becomes guesswork. Blades also heat the coffee through friction and offer no way to repeat a result, since "ten seconds" never yields the same grind twice. [!warning] The common workaround doesn't fix it Pulsing a blade grinder or shaking it helps a little but cannot create true uniformity — the impact mechanism is the problem, not the technique. Even a modest hand burr grinder will out brew the best blade grinder. [!tip] Where blades are tolerable For spices, or for very forgiving French Press brewing, a blade grinder is passable. For pour over, it is the weakest link in the chain.
If you upgrade one thing, upgrade your grinder to a burr — even an entry level Hand Grinders vs Electric Grinders transforms the cup. From there, the next question is what kind of burr.

Once you have committed to a Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders, the next fork is geometry : conical or flat. Both crush beans between abrasive surfaces, but their shapes route coffee through the grinding zone differently, and that shapes the Particle Distribution and Uniformity — and, many argue, the flavor. This is one of the more contested topics in coffee, so treat the broad strokes as reliable and the flavor claims as debated.

A conical burr set is a cone shaped inner burr nested inside a ringed outer burr. Coffee enters at the top, spirals down the widening gap, and exits at the base. Conical burrs are common in Hand Grinders vs Electric Grinders because the cone is easy to turn and forgiving to manufacture. A flat burr set is two parallel rings of teeth facing each other, one spinning. Beans are flung outward across the flat grinding surface by centrifugal force and exit at the rim. Flat burrs are typical in higher end electric grinders and commercial machines. Conical Flat Shape Cone in a ring Two facing rings Common in Hand grinders, many electrics High end electrics, commercial Distribution Often slightly wider; some say Particle Distribution and Uniformity\ tendency Often tighter, more Particle Distribution and Uniformity\ Cost / power Cheaper, lower power Pricier, higher power, more heat Reputation Body, sweetness, forgiveness Clarity, separation, complexity

The popular framing is that flat burrs deliver clarity — bright, well separated, distinct flavors — while conical burrs deliver body — rounder, sweeter, heavier cups. The proposed mechanism is that flats produce a tighter, more uniform grind that extracts more evenly, whereas conicals produce a slightly wider spread (sometimes a mild Fines and Boulders heavy second peak) that adds texture and complexity. [!warning] This is contested, not settled Blind tastings and particle size studies give mixed results, and burr quality, alignment, model, and RPM often matter more than geometry alone. Plenty of conical grinders out clarify cheap flats, and vice versa. Treat "flat = clarity, conical = body" as a useful starting heuristic, not a law. The individual grinder beats the category. [!tip] What actually moves the cup Burr alignment and sharpness , the Roast Levels for Pour Over, and how well you Dialing In Grind Size will affect your pour over more than the conical vs flat choice for most home setups.

For filter brewing, a well made grinder of either geometry can produce an excellent, clean cup. If you chase ultimate How to Taste Coffee and complexity and have the budget, a quality flat burr electric is the enthusiast's classic pick; if you value sweetness, portability, and value, a good conical — including most Hand Grinders vs Electric Grinders — serves pour over beautifully.

So where exactly should you set the grinder? Pour over lives in a medium grind window — coarser than espresso, finer than French Press — but the precise target shifts with your The Dripper Explained, your Roast Levels for Pour Over, and your taste. There is no universal number; there is a starting zone and a method for moving within it.

A useful mental reference is table salt to coarse sand — distinctly granular, not powdery, not chunky. In practical terms most pour over sits in the medium to medium fine range, roughly 600–900 microns, though brewers describe it relative to their own grinder rather than in microns. The goal is a grind that lets a full brew run in a sensible Brew Time and Total Contact Time (commonly 2:30–3:30 for a single cup) without stalling or gushing. Reference point Relative grind French Press Coarse Chemex Medium coarse Kalita Wave Medium Hario V60 Medium / medium fine Espresso Very fine

The brewer's geometry and Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth change how fast water drains, so the grind must compensate to keep contact time in range. [!example] Dripper by dripper Hario V60 — a fast, open cone with a big single hole. Water races through, so it wants a finer grind (medium / medium fine) to slow it and reach full Extraction Yield and Strength. Kalita Wave — a flat bed with three small holes that restricts flow. A medium grind suits it; go too fine and it clogs. Chemex — uses a thick, restrictive paper filter. A medium coarse grind keeps the bed from choking against that dense paper.

Roast Levels for Pour Over changes both bean structure and how readily it gives up flavor. Lighter roasts are dense and harder to extract, so they generally want a finer grind (and hotter water) to coax out enough. Darker roasts are brittle and extract easily — and shatter into more Fines and Boulders — so they want a coarser grind to avoid bitterness and clogging. [!warning] Don't copy microns blindly Grinder "click" and micron numbers from a recipe rarely transfer between machines. Burr design, alignment, and wear mean the same setting grinds differently on different grinders. Use any published number as a rough start, then Dialing In Grind Size.

sections.h: The Real Answer: Dial It In
sections.t: The window above gets you in the neighborhood; your tongue finds the address. Brew, taste, and adjust grind toward the cup you want — sour pushes finer, bitter pushes coarser. That iterative loop is the whole craft.

A grind is never a single size — it is a distribution of sizes around a target. Plot how many particles fall at each diameter and you get the grinder's signature curve. The shape of that curve, not just its center point, decides how evenly your coffee extracts. This is why two grinders set to the "same" medium can brew very differently: their distributions differ.

The key feature is the number of peaks. A unimodal distribution has one clear hump — most particles cluster near the target size, with few strays. A bimodal distribution has two humps — a main peak at the target plus a second, smaller peak of very fine dust (Fines and Boulders). Almost every real grinder produces some fines, so distributions are rarely perfectly unimodal; the question is how tight the main peak is and how large the fines tail. Distribution Shape Extraction behavior Unimodal (tight) One narrow peak Even — particles extract in step Bimodal Main peak + fines peak Uneven — fines and boulders diverge Wide / flat Spread out Chaotic — see Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders\

Extraction is a race against time, and every particle extracts at a rate set by its size. In a brew, all particles get the same contact time — so if they differ wildly in size, they cannot all finish in the right place. The Fines and Boulders over extract into bitterness while the Fines and Boulders under extract into sourness, and the cup is the muddled average of both. A uniform grind lets you choose one contact time that suits nearly every particle, so the whole bed lands in the sweet spot of Extraction Yield and Strength together. That is the mechanism behind clean, sweet, well defined pour over. [!note] Uniformity is why grinders cost money Much of the price difference between grinders buys a tighter distribution — sharper, better aligned burrs that produce fewer strays. It is the main reason a good grinder out brews a good dripper. [!warning] Some fines are useful — and unavoidable A modest population of fines is not pure evil. Fines add body and help build the bed's resistance to flow. The goal is controlled distribution, not zero fines — which no grinder achieves. Chasing perfection here has diminishing returns past a quality burr set.

Burr geometry nudges the curve: many Conical vs Flat Burrs aim for a tighter unimodal peak, while conicals are sometimes described as slightly broader — though, as that note stresses, this is contested and grinder quality dominates the category.

At the two ends of every grind's Particle Distribution and Uniformity sit the troublemakers: fines and boulders . Fines are the smallest particles — near dust; boulders are the largest — oversized chunks. A little of each is inevitable, but too many of either pull the cup apart, because each extracts at a completely different pace from the particles in the middle.

Fines are particles far smaller than your target — fragments and powder produced as burrs fracture beans, especially brittle Roast Levels for Pour Over ones. Boulders are particles far larger than target, often beans that slipped through a Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders only partly crushed, or the inevitable output of an inconsistent burr or a Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders. Fines Boulders Size Much smaller than target Much larger than target Extraction Too fast → over extracted, bitter Too slow → under extracted, sour Main problem Clog the bed, slow drawdown Waste flavor, leave it locked in Worsened by Dark roasts, dull burrs Inconsistent / blade grinders

The trouble is that fines and boulders fail in opposite directions at the same time. Fines, with their huge surface area, dump flavor almost instantly and tip into Under Extraction and Over Extraction — bitter, drying, astringent. Boulders barely give anything up in a normal brew and stay Under Extraction and Over Extraction — sour, weak, hollow. Because a single brew uses one contact time, you cannot satisfy both. The cup becomes a confused blend of bitter and sour rather than a clean, sweet whole.

Fines do extra damage by migrating downward during the brew and packing into the base of the coffee bed and the pores of the Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth. This restricts flow, slows The Drawdown, and stalls the brew — which then lengthens contact time and deepens over extraction. Stalls, long brew times, and a soupy bed are classic fines symptoms, especially in a fast brewer like the Hario V60. This dynamic also feeds Channeling and Uneven Extraction as water seeks easier paths around the clog. [!tip] Reducing the damage Use a quality Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders\ for a tighter Particle Distribution and Uniformity\. Grind a touch coarser if brews are stalling. Some brewers sift out fines, or gently pour off the dust, for extra clarity — useful but fussy. Match grind to Roast Levels for Pour Over\: dark roasts make more fines, so grind them coarser. [!warning] Don't aim for zero fines No grinder eliminates fines, and a small amount adds welcome body and helps regulate flow. The goal is controlling the extremes, not erasing them.

Every coffee has a grind setting that makes it sing — and dialing in is the simple, disciplined loop you use to find it: brew, taste, adjust grind, re brew. Because grind is the Why Grind Size Matters of The Science of Extraction, it is also your primary troubleshooting tool. Master this loop and most "bad cups" become solvable in two or three iterations.

Dialing in only works if you change one thing at a time . Lock everything else — The Brew Ratio, Water Temperature for Brewing, dose, and Pouring Technique — and move grind alone. If you change grind and temperature together, you learn nothing about either. Keep a consistent The Anatomy of a Pour Over so the only variable in play is the one you are studying.

Taste tells you which way to turn the dial. The two signposts are sourness and bitterness , the tells of Under Extraction and Over Extraction. Cup tastes... Likely cause Move Sour, thin, salty, weak Under extracted Grind finer Bitter, dry, harsh, hollow Over extracted Grind coarser Sweet, balanced, clear Dialed in 🎯 Stop — record it [!example] The loop in practice 1. Brew a reference cup with fixed The Brew Ratio and water. 2. Note the Brew Time and Total Contact Time and taste. 3. Sour? Grind finer (also lengthens brew). Bitter? Grind coarser (also shortens brew). 4. Change grind by a small step, re brew, taste again. 5. Repeat until the cup is sweet and balanced — then write down the setting .

When the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide asks "what went wrong?", grind is almost always the first answer because it moves extraction more cleanly than anything else. Brew stalling or going long? Likely too fine, or too many Fines and Boulders — go coarser. Brew gushing through in under two minutes and tasting weak? Too coarse — go finer. Only once grind is settled should you reach for temperature, ratio, or Agitation and Turbulence as fine tuning. [!tip] Move in small steps and keep notes Big grind jumps overshoot the sweet spot. Adjust a click or two at a time, and log the grinder setting, ratio, and time for each coffee. A simple brew journal turns guesswork into a repeatable recipe. [!warning] When grind isn't the culprit If finer makes the cup more sour rather than less, suspect a stall or Channeling and Uneven Extraction — the bed is mis extracting, not under extracting. Fix the pour or Particle Distribution and Uniformity before chasing grind further.

Once you've chosen a Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders and thought about Conical vs Flat Burrs, one practical question remains: do you turn it by hand or plug it in? Both hand (manual) and electric grinders can deliver excellent, uniform pour over grinds. The choice is mostly about budget, convenience, volume, and how much you enjoy the ritual — not about whether good coffee is possible.

A hand grinder uses a crank and your arm; an electric grinder uses a motor. For a single pour over, a modern hand grinder can match or beat an electric costing several times more, because more of your money goes into the burrs rather than the motor and housing. But grinding by hand takes effort and time, and that adds up fast for multiple cups. Hand grinder Electric grinder Burr quality per dollar High Lower at entry level Effort Manual cranking Push a button Speed / volume Slow; tiring for batches Fast; great for Single Cup vs Batch Pour Over\ Noise Quiet Often loud Portability Excellent (travel) Tied to a socket Consistency Excellent (good models) Excellent (good models) Best for Single cups, value, travel Daily volume, convenience

[!tip] Pick a hand grinder if... You brew mostly one or two cups at a time, want the best Particle Distribution and Uniformity\ for your money, travel with your coffee, or value a quiet morning ritual. The modern wave of stainless burr hand grinders has made this the value conscious enthusiast's default for Hario V60\ style brewing. [!tip] Pick an electric grinder if... You grind frequently or in volume , brew for a household, value speed and convenience over the last few dollars of value, or want a single dose machine that just works each morning. High end electrics also unlock large Conical vs Flat Burrs\ sets that are impractical to crank by hand.

sections.h: A Brand Neutral Map of the Market 🗺️
sections.t: Rather than name a single "best," it helps to think in tiers — and you can dig into makers in Coffee Gear Brands. Entry hand grinders — affordable stainless conical burrs; a huge upgrade over any blade grinder. Enthusiast hand grinders — precision machined burrs and adjustments that rival mid electrics for filter. Entry / mid electrics — convenient flat or conical burrs aimed at home filter brewing. High end / prosumer electrics — large flat burrs, single dosing, and tight Particle Distribution and Uniformity\ for those chasing maximum How to Taste Coffee\. [!warning] Don't confuse motor for quality An expensive electric grinder is not automatically better than a cheaper hand grinder — burr quality and alignment decide the grind, not whether a motor spins it. Many cheap electrics ship with mediocre burrs; judge the burrs, not the badge.

A finished cup of pour over is roughly 98–99% water by mass — the coffee solubles you worked so hard to extract make up barely one or two percent of what reaches your lips. That arithmetic alone explains why water is the most underrated ingredient in brewing: the liquid you pour through the bed is not a neutral solvent but an active reagent whose mineral content, buffering, and temperature shape every flavor you taste. This domain is the home for everything about that water — its chemistry, its measurement, the standards that define "good," and how to build or choose it.

You can buy a world class grinder, a Hario V60, and a single origin from the best roaster in town, and still brew a flat, dull cup if your water is wrong. Hard, over buffered tap water mutes Acidity in Coffee and flattens sweetness; near pure distilled water extracts poorly and tastes hollow. The water sits between your coffee and your palate, and getting it into a sensible window is one of the highest leverage, least glamorous upgrades a home brewer can make. See Why Water Matters for the full case. [!tip] The two thirds rule of thumb Coffee that tastes good usually has water with moderate mineral content to dissolve flavor and modest alkalinity to keep acidity bright but not harsh. Everything in this domain is, in some sense, a refinement of those two ideas.

Topic Note The overlooked variable Why Water Matters GH vs KH Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity Minerals in the water TDS and Mineral Content The official target The SCA Water Standard Heat and the brew window Water Temperature for Brewing Make your own water Building Water — Recipes and Remineralization Everyday sources Filtered, Bottled, and Tap Water

Water never works alone. Its temperature is one of the strongest levers over The Role of Temperature in Extraction, its minerals govern what Solubility and What Dissolves and how Extraction Yield and Strength, and its alkalinity decides how much of a coffee's perceived Acidity in Coffee survives into the cup. Brewers who chase a competition clean result through The Brew Ratio and Pouring Technique eventually arrive here, because water is the substrate all of that acts upon. When a cup is mysteriously dull across every coffee you own, the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide will often point back to this domain.

Because a brewed coffee is about 98% water , the water you start with is not a backdrop to flavor — it is most of the flavor's physical home. Two brewers can use identical beans, the same Grind Size for Pour Over, the same The Brew Ratio, and the same Pouring Technique, and produce cups that taste like different coffees, purely because one drew from a softened tap and the other from a remineralized bottle. Water is the single most overlooked variable in pour over, and once a brewer fixes it, every other variable starts behaving predictably.

sections.h: Water Is an Active Solvent, Not a Blank One 🧫
sections.t: Pure H₂O is a surprisingly aggressive and unselective solvent — and that is a problem, not a virtue. The dissolved minerals in water, chiefly magnesium and calcium , latch onto specific flavor compounds and help pull them out of the coffee bed. Magnesium tends to favor bright, fruity, Acidity in Coffee notes; calcium favors heavier, creamier body. Strip those minerals out entirely, as in distilled or fresh reverse osmosis water, and extraction becomes inefficient and the cup tastes thin and hollow even at textbook Extraction Yield and Strength. The minerals are part of how coffee dissolves — see Solubility and What Dissolves.

sections.h: The Two Levers: What Dissolves, and What Survives
sections.t: Water shapes the cup through two largely independent mechanisms, and confusing them is the root of most water mistakes. [!note] Extraction vs. perception Hardness (GH) — the magnesium and calcium content — mostly governs how much and what kind of flavor is extracted. Alkalinity (KH) — the buffering capacity — governs how much of the coffee's natural acidity survives into the cup by neutralizing acids. High alkalinity can leave a technically well extracted coffee tasting chalky and flat. Both are unpacked in Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity.

sections.h: Why It Hides in Plain Sight
sections.t: Water is invisible and seemingly "the same everywhere," which is exactly why its effects get misattributed. A brewer whose cups are consistently dull will re grind, re ratio, and buy new beans long before suspecting the tap. Worse, water problems are systematic: they degrade every coffee the same way, so there is no good cup to compare against. If you have dialed in Dialing In Grind Size and Brewing Technique and your results are still muted across the board, the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide will steer you here.

[!warning] "Bottled water is always better for coffee" Not so. Many popular bottled mineral waters are very high in bicarbonate (alkalinity) and brew flatter coffee than decent filtered tap. Some are excellent; some are awful. The label matters far more than the word "spring." See Filtered, Bottled, and Tap Water. The practical payoff is enormous and cheap. Getting your water into a sensible window — explored in The SCA Water Standard and built deliberately in Building Water — Recipes and Remineralization — is often the largest single improvement available to a home brewer, dollar for dollar.

The single most useful idea in coffee water chemistry is that hardness and alkalinity are two different things , even though everyday language tangles them together. Hardness — measured as GH , general hardness — is the amount of calcium and magnesium dissolved in the water. Alkalinity — measured as KH , carbonate hardness — is the water's buffering capacity , almost entirely from bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) . They are measured in similar units, they often rise together in tap water, and they do completely different jobs in your cup. Untangling them is the key to understanding water, as Why Water Matters argues.

General hardness is the mineral content that actively drives Solubility and What Dissolves. Magnesium is the more aggressive, flavor binding ion — it tends to lift bright, juicy, Acidity in Coffee and fruity character. Calcium is gentler and associated with body, weight, and a rounder mouthfeel. Together they set how efficiently water pulls flavor from the bed: too little GH and the water extracts poorly, leaving a thin, hollow cup; too much and you risk over extraction plus limescale on your Gooseneck Kettles and equipment.

Alkalinity is where most "good beans, dull cup" mysteries hide. Bicarbonate is a buffer : it neutralizes acids. Coffee is full of desirable organic acids — citric, malic, and others that read as brightness and fruit. The higher your water's KH, the more of those acids get chemically mopped up before they reach your palate, so a high alkalinity brew tastes flat, chalky, or muted even when extraction is otherwise fine. Low KH lets acidity sing, but go too low and the brew can taste sharp, sour, or harsh, and the water becomes corrosive to equipment. [!note] One sentence to remember GH controls how much flavor comes out; KH controls how much of the coffee's acidity survives. Bright, fruity coffees want lower alkalinity to shine; balanced or chocolatey roasts tolerate, and sometimes prefer, a little more.

Hardness and alkalinity are usually quoted in ppm as CaCO₃ (or in German degrees, °dH; 1 °dH ≈ 17.8 ppm). A rough orientation: Measure What it is Drives A workable target GH (general hardness) Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ Extraction, body, brightness 50–175 ppm; 68 ppm ideal KH (alkalinity) Mostly HCO₃⁻ Acid buffering, scale 40–75 ppm; 40 ppm ideal Ratio of the two GH relative to KH Overall balance GH at or above KH These align with The SCA Water Standard and feed directly into TDS and Mineral Content. The headline ratio matters: many specialty recipes aim for GH at least equal to KH , so the extracting minerals outweigh the buffering ones.

[!warning] The famous 68/40 "ideal" is a target, not a law The widely cited "magic" figure of 68 ppm GH and 40 ppm KH comes from the SCAA/SCA work and the book Water for Coffee . It is an excellent starting point, not a universal optimum — the best water depends on the coffee, the roast, and your taste. Treat published numbers as a launchpad, then taste and adjust. To act on all this — softening, hardening, or rebuilding water to hit a chosen GH and KH — see Building Water — Recipes and Remineralization and the everyday source guidance in Filtered, Bottled, and Tap Water.

Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) is the total mass of dissolved substances in water, usually expressed in parts per million (ppm) or milligrams per litre (mg/L — the same number). For brewing water, that means everything dissolved before the coffee touches it: calcium, magnesium, bicarbonate, sodium, chloride, and trace minerals. A higher TDS means more mineral content available to pull flavor from the bed; a near zero TDS — distilled or fresh reverse osmosis water — means almost nothing is there to extract with, and the cup suffers. This note is about the mineral content of your brewing water , the front end of the whole Why Water Matters. [!warning] Two very different "TDS" numbers — don't confuse them Water TDS is the mineral content of the water before brewing (this note). Brew TDS is the strength of the finished coffee — the percentage of dissolved coffee solids a refractometer reads, typically 1.2–1.5%, covered in Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) and Extraction Yield and Strength. Same acronym, opposite ends of the brew.

The cheap, common tool is a TDS meter (EC/conductivity pen) . It does not actually count dissolved solids — it measures electrical conductivity and multiplies by a conversion factor to estimate ppm. That makes it a fast, rough gauge of "how mineral" your water is, but it is blind to the GH/KH split that actually matters for flavor: two waters can read the same TDS yet brew completely differently if one is mostly extracting magnesium and the other mostly buffering bicarbonate. For that distinction, use GH/KH drop tests, not the pen — see Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity.

sections.h: What a Good Number Looks Like
sections.t: Water TDS (ppm) Character Verdict for brewing 0–30 Distilled / fresh RO Too low — extracts poorly, hollow cup 50–100 Lightly to moderately mineral Sweet spot for most pour over 150 The SCA reference point Solid target; see The SCA Water Standard 175–250 Hard Often over buffered; mutes acidity 250+ Very hard Flat cups and scale on the Gooseneck Kettles\ The widely used reference target is around 150 ppm , with a workable band of roughly 75–175 ppm . But TDS is only a headline figure: what makes up that number — the balance of extracting minerals versus buffering bicarbonate — decides the cup far more than the total.

Because a TDS pen cannot see composition, chasing a target ppm without knowing your GH and KH is like setting The Brew Ratio without weighing the coffee. A 150 ppm water that is nearly all bicarbonate will brew flat and chalky; a 150 ppm water weighted toward magnesium will brew bright and extractive. Use TDS as a quick sanity check and a way to confirm a Building Water — Recipes and Remineralization landed where you intended, then trust GH/KH for the flavor decisions. The interaction of mineral content with Solubility and What Dissolves and The Role of Temperature in Extraction is what ultimately sets your Extraction Yield and Strength.

The Specialty Coffee Association (SCA) — and before it the SCAA — publishes a recommended water profile for brewing, the closest thing the industry has to an agreed definition of "good water." It exists because water is the great hidden variable (see Why Water Matters), and a shared target lets roasters, cafés, and competitors compare results on common ground. The standard is best understood as a target box with an acceptable range and an "ideal" centre point, not a single magic recipe.

[!example] SCA recommended brewing water profile Attribute Target / ideal Acceptable range Total Dissolved Solids 150 ppm 75–250 ppm Calcium hardness (GH) 68 ppm (as CaCO₃) 17–85 ppm Total alkalinity (KH) 40 ppm (as CaCO₃) at/near 40 ppm pH 7.0 (neutral) 6.5–7.5 Sodium (Na⁺) 10 mg/L low Odor clean / odor free — Chlorine 0 0 Those map directly onto the TDS and Mineral Content and Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity ideas: moderate total minerals, hardness comfortably above alkalinity, neutral pH, and no chlorine (which causes off flavors and is the first thing a Filtered, Bottled, and Tap Water should remove).

The famous " 68/40 " pairing — 68 ppm calcium hardness and 40 ppm alkalinity — is the ideal centre, while the wider TDS band of 75–250 ppm marks where water is still considered acceptable. The intent is a water that extracts efficiently (enough GH) without over buffering acidity (modest KH) , stays kind to equipment, and adds no flavors of its own. Many Building Water — Recipes and Remineralization are explicitly engineered to land on or near this profile.

The standard is a useful launchpad, but it is not gospel , and serious brewers treat it with nuance. [!warning] Known shortcomings of the SCA profile The acceptable range is very wide. A 75 ppm water and a 250 ppm water both "pass" yet brew dramatically different cups, so passing the standard does not guarantee a great result. It specifies total hardness, not the magnesium to calcium ratio — yet that ratio strongly shapes flavor (magnesium for brightness, calcium for body). Two compliant waters can taste very different. It targets a general optimum, not your coffee. A juicy washed Ethiopian and a dark espresso blend do not want identical water; the ideal shifts with roast and bean. Measurement is fuzzy — cheap TDS and Mineral Content\ read conductivity, not composition, so "150 ppm" on a pen tells you little about GH/KH balance. The honest takeaway: hit the SCA box to rule water out as a problem, then use taste — your developing Building Your Palate — to fine tune within it. When dialing a new coffee, the standard tells you your water is sane ; Dialing In Grind Size and Brewing Technique do the rest.

After mineral content, temperature is the strongest dial water gives you over the cup, because heat governs how fast coffee solubles dissolve. The widely accepted pour over window is roughly 90–96°C (195–205°F) , with much of the specialty world parking near 92–94°C for filter brewing. Hotter water extracts faster and harder; cooler water extracts slower and gentler. Temperature is one of the cleanest single variable levers in brewing — see how it feeds the bigger picture in The Role of Temperature in Extraction.

Higher temperatures speed up Solubility and What Dissolves and push Extraction Yield and Strength up. The catch is that the compounds dissolve in a rough order: pleasant acids and sugars first, then heavier, more bitter and astringent compounds. Brew too cool for the grind and you stop short — a sour, Under Extraction and Over Extraction cup. Brew too hot for the grind and you drag out the bitter tail — a harsh, over extracted one. Water temp Tendency Use when 96–100°C Fast, aggressive extraction Coarse grind, Light Roast and Specialty Coffee\, hard to extract beans 92–94°C Balanced, the common default Most pour over, most roasts 88–91°C Gentle, forgiving Roast Levels for Pour Over\, to tame bitterness Below 85°C Slow, prone to sourness Rarely ideal for hot pour over [!tip] Temperature and grind are partners Temperature and Grind Size for Pour Over both move extraction in the same direction, so adjust one at a time . Many brewers fix temperature near 93°C and dial flavor with grind, the cleaner lever per Dialing In Grind Size.

Roast level shifts the ideal window. Lighter, denser roasts are harder to extract and generally reward hotter water (95–100°C) to coax out enough flavor. Darker, more soluble and brittle roasts give up their solubles easily and can turn bitter, so they often brew better cooler (88–92°C). This is why a single fixed temperature is a starting point, not a rule.

Water boils at lower temperatures as elevation rises, because atmospheric pressure drops. At sea level water boils at 100°C ; near 1,500 m (≈5,000 ft) it boils around 95°C ; at 2,500 m (≈8,000 ft) closer to 91–92°C . [!warning] Boiling water is not a fixed temperature At high altitude, "fresh off the boil" may already sit below your target window, so you may need to brew with near boiling water and not let it cool — and very high elevation brewers may struggle to reach ideal temperatures at all. Conversely, at sea level, water straight off the boil (100°C) is usually a touch hot for filter; a 30–45 second rest brings it into range. A variable temperature Gooseneck Kettles removes the guesswork.

[!warning] "Never use boiling water" This common rule is an oversimplification. Off the boil water is too hot for some darker roasts, but plenty of light roast specialty recipes — including competition routines — deliberately use water at or very near 100°C. The right answer depends on roast, grind, and altitude, not a blanket prohibition.

Once you understand that water is most of the cup, the logical next step is to stop hoping your tap is good and build your own to a known target. The recipe is always the same shape: start from near zero water — distilled or reverse osmosis (RO) — which is a blank slate with almost no TDS and Mineral Content, then add back a controlled amount of minerals to hit a chosen Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity. This gives perfectly repeatable, competition grade water anywhere in the world, independent of local supply.

Remineralization needs a source of hardness (calcium and/or magnesium) and, optionally, a source of alkalinity (bicarbonate) to buffer. The two most common DIY salts: Salt Adds Role Epsom salt (magnesium sulfate, MgSO₄·7H₂O) Magnesium → GH Brightness, extraction, fruit Baking soda (sodium bicarbonate, NaHCO₃) Bicarbonate → KH Buffering, balance, tames sharpness Some recipes add calcium (e.g. calcium chloride or food grade calcium sulfate/gypsum) for body. The headline rule from Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity holds: keep GH at or above KH so extracting minerals outweigh buffering ones.

[!note] Concentrates vs. commercial sachets DIY concentrate method: dissolve your salts into a small, strong "stock" solution, then dose a few millilitres of each concentrate into a litre of RO/distilled. Cheap, precise, infinitely tweakable. Commercial sachets: products like Third Wave Water , Lotus , Aquacode , or Perfect Coffee Water are pre measured minerals you tip into a fixed volume — zero math, less control. Both start from the same blank RO/distilled base.

Here is a classic, widely used Barista Hustle–style two concentrate recipe targeting a balanced profile near the The SCA Water Standard ( 68 ppm GH, 40 ppm KH). All weights are food grade salts into distilled or RO water.
