[!example] Build sheet — balanced "everyday" water Step 1 — make two concentrates (per 1 litre distilled water each): Concentrate Salt Amount Hardness Epsom salt (MgSO₄·7H₂O) 2.45 g Buffer Baking soda (NaHCO₃) 1.68 g Step 2 — build a brewing litre: Into 1 L distilled/RO, add Volume of concentrate Hardness concentrate 10 mL Buffer concentrate 10 mL Resulting water (approx): GH ≈ 68 ppm, KH ≈ 40 ppm, TDS ≈ 110–150 ppm — squarely in the target box. Adjust to taste: more hardness concentrate for a brighter, more extractive water that flatters Light Roast and Specialty Coffee and juicy Acidity in Coffee; more buffer to soften a sharp, sour cup or to suit Roast Levels for Pour Over. Always weigh salts on a precise scale and store concentrates refrigerated.

[!warning] Reverse osmosis and distilled water are not ready to brew Never brew with bare RO or distilled water — its near zero mineral content extracts poorly and tastes hollow, and some sources note it can leach metals from equipment. It is a base , to be remineralized first. The payoff is total control and perfect repeatability: the same water, every brew, so when you change Dialing In Grind Size or The Brew Ratio you know the water held constant. For brewers who don't want to mix salts, the practical middle ground — good filters and the right bottled water — lives in Filtered, Bottled, and Tap Water.

Building water from scratch is the precise route, but most people brew with whatever comes out of a tap, a filter jug, or a bottle. The good news is that sensible everyday water can brew excellent coffee — you just need to know which sources help, which hurt, and what to avoid. This is the pragmatic counterpart to Building Water — Recipes and Remineralization: how to get into the The SCA Water Standard without a scale and a bag of Epsom salt.

Tap water is free and often fine — but it varies wildly by region, and two things commonly spoil it: chlorine/chloramine (added for safety, it causes off flavors) and high alkalinity (hard, over buffered water that flattens Acidity in Coffee). If your area has soft to moderate, low chlorine water, a simple carbon filter may be all you need. If it is very hard, tap alone will mute most coffees. [!warning] Avoid water softener output Household ion exchange softeners swap calcium and magnesium for sodium . This stops limescale but is bad for coffee: it strips the Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity that drives extraction while leaving the alkalinity (KH) untouched , so you get flat, salty tasting, poorly extracting water — often the worst of both worlds. Brew from an unsoftened line or a different source.

Filtration is the best value upgrade for most homes, but not all filters do the same job : Filter type Removes Affects minerals? Activated carbon (Brita style jug, faucet) Chlorine, odors, some organics Largely leaves GH/KH intact Ion exchange / "softening" cartridges Hardness Lowers GH (can over soften) Reverse osmosis (RO) Nearly everything Strips to near zero — must remineralize For most tap water, a carbon filter is the sweet spot: it removes the chlorine that ruins flavor while keeping the minerals you want. RO is excellent only as a blank base for Building Water — Recipes and Remineralization — never brew with bare RO, which extracts poorly and tastes hollow, as TDS and Mineral Content explains.

Bottled water is convenient but a minefield, because "spring" and "mineral" tell you nothing about the brew . Check the label for bicarbonate / total dissolved solids : [!tip] Read the bottle's mineral table Favor low to moderate TDS, low bicarbonate waters. Many famous mineral waters are extremely high in bicarbonate and brew flat, chalky coffee. Some low mineral bottled waters (e.g. certain "pure"/low TDS labels) are popular precisely because they sit near the SCA target out of the bottle. [!warning] Myth — "bottled water is always best for coffee" False, and a costly habit. A high alkalinity bottled water brews worse than decent carbon filtered tap. The word on the label matters far less than the numbers on the back, per Why Water Matters.

Soft, low chlorine tap → carbon filter, brew, done. Hard, high alkalinity tap → low bicarbonate bottled water, or go to Building Water — Recipes and Remineralization. Chasing competition level consistency → RO base + remineralization. Never → softener output, bare RO/distilled, or unlabeled "mineral" water. If your cups are dull across every coffee despite good Dialing In Grind Size and Brewing Technique, suspect the water first — the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide agrees.

Brewing technique is everything the brewer actually does at the dripper — the controllable levers of a manual brew. If Grinding, Water for Coffee, and Coffee Beans and Roast are the ingredients, technique is the cooking: how you wet the bed, when and where you pour, how much energy you add, and how you read the brew as it drains. This domain is about the principles behind those moves. The specific, timed pour schedules — Hoffmann's V60, Kasuya's Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method, the Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method — live in the Recipes and Methods and apply these principles to a cup.

Every pour over comes down to a handful of variables you can move independently. Master each one and you can steer a cup deliberately rather than by luck. Lever What it sets Note Ratio Strength / concentration The Brew Ratio Bloom Degassing & even wetting The Bloom Pour pattern Evenness & flow Pouring Technique Pour style Pulse vs continuous Pulse vs Continuous Pouring Agitation Extraction Yield and Strength\ energy Agitation and Turbulence Drawdown Final drain & contact The Drawdown Total time Overall extraction Brew Time and Total Contact Time

[!tip] Reading order If you are new, walk these in sequence. Each builds on the last, and together they describe one complete brew. The Anatomy of a Pour Over — the whole brew, start to finish, stage by stage. The Brew Ratio — how much coffee to how much water, and why it sets strength. The Bloom — the degassing pre infusion that primes the bed. Pouring Technique — center vs spiral, pour height, flow, kettle control. Pulse vs Continuous Pouring — the two great pouring philosophies. Agitation and Turbulence — adding energy to lift extraction (and its risks). The Drawdown — the final drain, bed compaction, and what its timing tells you. Bypass and Channeling — water taking the easy path, and how to stop it. Pre Wetting the Filter — rinsing paper, and the debate over whether it matters. Brew Time and Total Contact Time — target times and what running long or short signals. Single Cup vs Batch Pour Over — how technique scales with bed depth and volume. Common Pour Over Mistakes — the frequent errors and their quick fixes.

Technique never works alone. The same pour gives a different cup depending on your Grind Size for Pour Over, your Water Temperature for Brewing, and your Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers. When a brew goes wrong, the cause is usually one lever out of balance — diagnose it with the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide and confirm with the Under Extraction and Over Extraction lens. Technique is where theory meets the cup, so keep The Science of Extraction nearby.

title: The Anatomy of a Pour Over

sections.t: Every manual brew, from a humble Hario V60 to a four cup Chemex, follows the same arc. Knowing the stages turns a recipe from a list of numbers into a story you can read and adjust. This note maps the brew start to finish; the deeper craft of each stage lives in its own note.

[!example] One brew, five acts Rinse → Dose → Bloom → Pours → Drawdown. Each stage hands off to the next, and a fault in one shows up later as an off cup. Stage What happens Roughly Deep dive 1. Rinse Wet the paper, preheat the brewer 0:00 Pre Wetting the Filter 2. Dose Add ground coffee, level the bed — The Brew Ratio 3. Bloom Wet all grounds, let CO₂ escape 0:00–0:45 The Bloom 4. Pours Add the remaining water in stages 0:45–2:30 Pouring Technique 5. Drawdown Last water drains through the bed until 3:00 The Drawdown

Pour hot water through the empty paper Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth to rinse away papery taste and preheat the The Dripper Explained and server. Discard that water before you add coffee. Whether it changes the cup is debated — see Pre Wetting the Filter.

Weigh your coffee on a Scales, Timers, and Servers and add the Grind Size for Pour Over. A quick tap settles the bed flat so water meets every particle evenly — an uneven bed is the first seed of Bypass and Channeling.

The first small pour — typically two to three times the coffee weight — saturates the grounds and releases trapped CO2, Degassing, and the Bloom Science. Fresh coffee swells and bubbles. The bloom sets up an even extraction for everything that follows.

Now you add the bulk of the water, either as discrete Pulse vs Continuous Pouring or one Pulse vs Continuous Pouring stream, controlling Agitation and Turbulence with your Gooseneck Kettles. Pour pattern, height, and flow rate all live here — see Pouring Technique.

Once your final pour is in, the remaining water drains through the bed and the The Drawdown begins. Its speed reveals whether your grind and pour were balanced. When the dripper stops dripping, the brew is done — total Brew Time and Total Contact Time is your headline number. [!tip] Read the bed afterward A flat, level bed of spent grounds signals an even brew. A crater, a high sided "donut," or coffee stranded up the walls hints at Bypass and Channeling or sloppy pouring.

The brew ratio is the proportion of coffee to water — the single number that most directly sets how strong your cup will be. Written as (e.g. ), it tells you how many grams of water you pour per gram of coffee. Get this right and everything else — Pouring Technique, The Bloom, Grind Size for Pour Over — fine tunes around it.

[!note] Two ways, one idea means 16 g of water per 1 g of coffee . Some people flip it and say "16:1" or even "60 g/L." They all describe the same concentration. For a single cup: 15 g coffee × 16 = 240 g water. A Scales, Timers, and Servers makes this trivial and repeatable — eyeballing scoops is the fastest way to an inconsistent cup.

The The Specialty Coffee Industry world centers on a band rather than a single magic number: Ratio Roughly Cup character 1:14–1:15 Strong Bold, heavy Sweetness and Body\, intense 1:16 Balanced The common all rounder default 1:17–1:18 Light Delicate, tea like, more Acidity in Coffee\ [!warning] "Golden ratio" is a guideline, not a law The oft quoted 1:18 "golden ratio" comes from drip machine standards. Modern pour over brewers commonly run stronger — 1:15 to 1:16 — and there is no single correct value. Pick what tastes good to you .

A crucial distinction: ratio controls strength (how concentrated the brew is, its Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)), while Grind Size for Pour Over, Water Temperature for Brewing, and Agitation and Turbulence control Extraction Yield and Strength (how much you pull from the grounds). You can have a strong but underextracted cup or a weak but overextracted one. The The Coffee Brewing Control Chart plots these two axes against each other.

Too weak / watery? Use more coffee (lower the water number, e.g. 1:16 → 1:15) before you reach for a finer grind. Too intense / muddy? Add water (1:15 → 1:17) for a lighter, cleaner cup. Changing dose? Keep the ratio fixed and scale both numbers together so the strength stays put — see Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes. Lighter roasts often reward a slightly higher coffee dose; see Roast Levels for Pour Over. When you scale up to a Single Cup vs Batch Pour Over, the ratio holds even as bed depth and time change.

The bloom is the first small pour of a brew — a brief pre infusion that wets all the grounds and lets trapped gas escape before the main pours begin. Watch fresh coffee bloom and you will see the bed swell, dome, and bubble like rising dough. It is the most visually satisfying moment in pour over, and one of the most functional.

Roasted coffee is full of carbon dioxide produced during Coffee Roasting Explained. When hot water hits the grounds, that CO₂ rushes out as gas. If you skip the bloom and pour all at once, those bubbles physically push water away from the coffee, creating dry pockets and uneven, often Under Extraction and Over Extraction cup. Blooming degasses the bed first so the main pours can saturate it evenly. The chemistry is covered in CO2, Degassing, and the Bloom Science. [!note] Fresher coffee blooms harder A vigorous, foamy bloom signals fresh, well gassed beans. Stale coffee barely reacts. Bloom is an informal Coffee Freshness and Degassing test you run every morning.

The standard guidance is 2 to 3 times the coffee weight in bloom water. Coffee dose Typical bloom water 15 g 30–45 g 20 g 40–60 g 30 g 60–90 g Use just enough to wet every grain. Too little leaves dry clumps; too much and the bloom drains away before degassing finishes. A gentle swirl or a careful stir helps reach any stubborn dry pockets — a mild form of Agitation and Turbulence.

Most recipes bloom for 30 to 45 seconds . Light, Light Roast and Specialty Coffee hold more gas and can reward a longer bloom (up to 60 seconds); older or Roast Levels for Pour Over coffee degasses faster and needs less. Watch the bed: when the doming relaxes and bubbling slows, the bloom has done its job. [!tip] Bloom by sight, not just the clock The timer is a starting point. The bed itself tells you when degassing is done — that is the real cue to begin your next pour.

Too little water — dry patches survive into the main brew, causing uneven Bypass and Channeling. Channeling the bloom pour — dumping water in one spot drills a hole; pour gently to wet the whole surface. Endless bloom — past 60 s the grounds cool and you lose Water Temperature for Brewing for little gain. The bloom is stage three in The Anatomy of a Pour Over and the foundation for everything that follows. A good bloom rarely makes a cup by itself, but a bad one almost always shows up later.

How you pour water is the most hands on lever in the whole brew. The same coffee, grind, and ratio can yield a clean, sweet cup or a hollow, sour one depending purely on where you pour, how high from, how fast , and how steadily you control the Gooseneck Kettles. This note covers the mechanics; how you sequence those pours is Pulse vs Continuous Pouring.

Variable Lever Effect Pattern Center vs spiral Evenness across the bed Height Close vs high Agitation and Turbulence\ energy Flow rate Slow vs fast Bed level & turbulence Placement Where on the bed Avoiding the walls

A center pour keeps the stream in one spot. It is gentle and stirs the bed less — useful when you want low agitation or are working with delicate grounds. A spiral pour moves outward in concentric circles, then back to the center, distributing water across the whole bed for a more even saturation. [!warning] Mind the walls Pouring directly onto the Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth wall washes coffee up the sides, where water can slip past the bed — classic Bypass and Channeling. Keep your spiral inside the coffee, stopping short of the paper.

Pour from low (a few centimeters above the bed) for a soft, calm entry; pour from high for a thin, energetic stream that drills into the grounds and adds Agitation and Turbulence. Height is your agitation dial: higher means more extraction energy, but also more risk of digging a crater. Most balanced recipes pour from a low to moderate height.

A Gooseneck Kettles exists for exactly this — its narrow spout gives you a precise, controllable stream that an ordinary kettle cannot. Aim for a steady flow, typically 5 to 10 g per second for a single cup. Keep the Scales, Timers, and Servers running so you pour to a target weight, not a guess. [!tip] Slow the stream to keep the bed calm If your bed keeps craters or you taste harshness, lower your kettle and slow your flow. A gentle pour is the easiest fix for over agitated brews.

Many brewers keep the slurry level fairly constant during continuous pours, replacing water as it drains. Others let it draw down between Pulse vs Continuous Pouring. Either works — what matters is consistency from brew to brew so you can Dialing In Grind Size deliberately. Pour technique interacts with Grind Size for Pour Over and Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers: a flat bed forgives sloppy pouring more than a steep cone.

Once the The Bloom is done, you face a choice that defines the rest of the brew: do you add the remaining water in several discrete pulses , or in one steady continuous stream? These are the two great pouring philosophies, and each shapes the cup differently.

[!example] Two ways to fill the brewer Pulse: several separate pours with pauses between, letting the bed draw down each time. Continuous: one (or few) long uninterrupted pours that keep the slurry level fairly high. Pulse pouring Continuous pouring Pours 3–5 small adds 1–2 long adds Contact pattern Bed drains between pours Bed stays flooded Agitation Renewed each pulse Steadier, often gentler Control More checkpoints Fewer variables to manage Feel Methodical Faster, flowing

Pulsing breaks the brew into stages you can monitor. Each new pour re saturates the bed, refreshes the Water Temperature for Brewing, and adds a burst of Agitation and Turbulence that lifts Extraction Yield and Strength. Because the bed draws down between pours, you get multiple chances to read the brew and adjust. The famous Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method is pure pulse pouring — five pours, each a deliberate lever on strength and flavor. The trade off is a longer, more involved brew and more total agitation, which can over extract a fine grind.

A continuous pour keeps the slurry level high and the extraction steady. With less stop start turbulence, it tends to be gentler and very repeatable — fewer moments to get wrong. Many modern recipes, including James Hoffmann V60 Technique in its later phase, blend the two: a couple of pulses early, then a more continuous top up. The risk is that a single long pour gives you fewer chances to correct course mid brew. [!tip] Match the style to your grind Pulse pouring adds agitation, so it pairs well with a slightly coarser grind. Continuous pouring is gentler, so it tolerates a finer grind. If you switch styles, expect to re Dialing In Grind Size.

Neither is "correct." Pulse gives control and a brighter, more layered cup; continuous gives simplicity and a rounder, repeatable one. Beginners often find a simple bloom + two pulses the easiest reliable structure. As you learn to read The Drawdown and Brew Time and Total Contact Time, you can move fluidly between styles. The point of both is the same: deliver your target water evenly without Bypass and Channeling.

Agitation is the physical energy you add to the coffee bed — through pour force, stirring, or swirling. It is one of the most powerful and least understood levers in pour over. More agitation means more extraction ; too much means harshness and Bypass and Channeling. Learning to dose agitation deliberately is what separates a flat cup from a vibrant one.

Extraction is a transfer of soluble compounds from grounds into water. Agitation speeds that transfer in two ways: it keeps fresh, less saturated water moving against the particle surfaces, and it dislodges Fines and Boulders and breaks up clumps so every grain is exposed. The result is a higher Extraction Yield and Strength for the same grind and time. See The Science of Extraction for the underlying mechanism.

Source How to add How to reduce Pour energy Higher kettle, faster flow Pour low and slow Pour count More Pulse vs Continuous Pouring\ Fewer, gentler pours Stirring Stir bloom or slurry Skip stirring Swirling Swirl the The Dripper Explained\ Let it sit still The The Rao Spin — a gentle swirl after the final pour — is a celebrated agitation tool: it settles grounds off the Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth walls and flattens the bed for an even The Drawdown.

[!warning] Agitation cuts both ways Aggressive agitation can over extract, drag bitterness into the cup, and — worst of all — carve channels in the bed that let water bypass the coffee entirely. Excess turbulence also kicks fines down to clog the filter, slowing drawdown and dragging out Brew Time and Total Contact Time.

Think of agitation as a dial you balance against Grind Size for Pour Over and The Brew Ratio: Cup tastes weak / sour / Under Extraction and Over Extraction? Add agitation — pour with more energy, add a stir, or swirl. Cup tastes harsh / bitter / drying? Reduce agitation — lower the kettle, pour gently, stop stirring. Bed has craters or coffee on the walls? You are over agitating in one spot; spread and soften the pour. [!tip] Change one thing at a time Agitation interacts with everything. When dialing in, hold grind and ratio fixed and vary agitation alone so you can feel its effect cleanly — then lock it in. Agitation is the hidden variable behind why two people following the same recipe get different cups. Control it on purpose and your brews become repeatable.

The drawdown is the final phase of a brew: after your last pour, the remaining water drains down through the coffee bed and out of the The Dripper Explained. It is the most diagnostic moment in pour over — how the bed drains tells you almost everything about whether your grind, pour, and agitation were balanced. Learn to read it and you barely need a recipe.

As water exits, the coffee bed settles and compacts . Fines and Boulders migrate downward and collect against the Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth, where they can throttle the flow. The slurry level drops, the surface flattens (or craters), and the last water passes through the most extracted grounds. When dripping stops, the brew is finished and your total Brew Time and Total Contact Time is set.

[!example] Drawdown is a gauge, not a target You do not "aim" for a drawdown time directly — it is an output that reveals what your inputs did. A surprisingly fast or slow drawdown is a clue, not a verdict. Drawdown Likely cause Likely cup Too fast Grind Size for Pour Over\ too coarse, Bypass and Channeling\ Weak, sour, Under Extraction and Over Extraction\ On target Balanced grind & pour Sweet, balanced Too slow / stalled Grind too fine, fines clogging, over agitation Bitter, Under Extraction and Over Extraction\ muddy

[!tip] Read the spent grounds Lift the dripper and look. A flat, level bed means even flow — well done. A deep crater or a "donut" with raised edges means you poured unevenly. Coffee stranded high on the Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth walls is a sign of Bypass and Channeling.

A stalled drawdown — water sitting and refusing to drain — usually means too many Fines and Boulders have sealed the filter, often from too fine a Grind Size for Pour Over or too much Agitation and Turbulence. Fixes: grind coarser, agitate less, or switch to a Particle Distribution and Uniformity grinder. A Kalita Wave or other Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers brewer drains more steadily and is more forgiving of fines than a steep Hario V60 cone.

sections.h: Speeding It Up or Slowing It Down
sections.t: Your grind is the master control on drawdown speed: coarser drains faster, finer slower. Pour style matters too — keeping the bed flooded (continuous) slows things, while Pulse vs Continuous Pouring lets it drain and reset. The The Rao Spin flattens the bed for a cleaner, more uniform final drain.

Two related faults explain most disappointing pour overs: bypass , where water flows around the coffee instead of through it, and channeling , where water carves a preferred path through the bed and floods down it. Both mean some water under extracts while it races past, leaving you a cup that is simultaneously weak and harsh — the hallmark of uneven extraction.

Bypass happens when water reaches the filter or the dripper wall and drains without passing through much coffee. Common culprits: Pouring onto the Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth wall, washing grounds upward and opening a gap. Coffee stranded high on the walls during The Drawdown, left dry. A bed that is too shallow for its volume — see Single Cup vs Batch Pour Over. Some bypass is designed in: Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers drippers like the Hario V60 allow a little wall flow by nature, which is part of their clean character. The goal is to control it, not eliminate it.

sections.h: Channeling: A Path of Least Resistance
sections.t: [!warning] One channel ruins the whole brew A channel is a low resistance crack in the bed. Once it forms, water rushes through it preferentially, over extracting that narrow column while the rest of the bed barely brews. The cup tastes thin, sour, and bitter at once. Channeling is the percolation cousin of the same fault in espresso, and the science is laid out in Channeling and Uneven Extraction.

Cause Why it channels Fix Uneven bed Water finds the thin spot Level the bed before brewing Aggressive pour Agitation and Turbulence\ drills a hole Pour gentler, lower Dry pockets Unwet clumps divert flow Better The Bloom\, stir gently Too many Fines and Boulders\ Migrate and crack the bed Particle Distribution and Uniformity\ Pouring on the wall Opens a wall channel Keep pours inside the bed

[!tip] An even bed is the whole game Start level: dose, tap the dripper, and consider wetting evenly during the The Bloom. Pour in calm spirals that stay off the walls. Finish with a gentle swirl — the The Rao Spin — to settle grounds back into a flat bed for an even drawdown. A Particle Distribution and Uniformity from a quality Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders is the deepest defense: fewer fines means fewer migrating particles to crack the bed. When a cup is off and you suspect channeling, confirm with the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide.

Pre wetting — or rinsing — the paper Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth is the very first step of most pour over recipes: you pour hot water through the empty filter and discard it before adding coffee. It is nearly universal practice, yet quietly one of the more contested rituals in brewing. This note covers what it does, how to do it, and where the evidence is genuinely murky.

[!note] Why people rinse 1. Remove papery taste — flush out the dry, cardboard like flavor of unbleached (and some bleached) paper. 2. Preheat the gear — warm the The Dripper Explained and Scales, Timers, and Servers so the brew doesn't lose Water Temperature for Brewing on contact.

1. Seat the filter in the dripper over your server. 2. Pour enough hot water to soak the whole paper, inside and along the seams. 3. For Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers filters, wet right up to (but not over) the rim so it clings to the wall. 4. Discard the rinse water from the server before dosing coffee. A thorough rinse also seals folded filters — like the Chemex's thick paper or Kalita Wave wavy filters — against the wall, reducing wall Bypass and Channeling.

This is where belief outruns evidence. Claim Verdict Removes paper taste Likely true , especially for cheap/unbleached paper; blind tastings sometimes detect it, sometimes don't. Preheats the brewer True and measurable — a cold ceramic dripper noticeably drops slurry temperature. Hugely changes the cup Contested — many blind tests find the flavor difference small with good modern paper. [!warning] A genuinely contested ritual Some respected brewers argue that with clean, high quality filters and a preheated setup, the flavor benefit of rinsing is marginal. Others insist they can taste unrinsed paper every time. The thermal benefit is the less disputable one. Treat rinsing as cheap insurance rather than dogma.

Rinse if your filters are unbleached or budget, if your dripper is cold ceramic or glass that steals heat (see Dripper Materials and Heat Retention), or if you simply want one fewer variable. Skip it only if you have tested your paper and gear and found no difference. Either way, be consistent — switching rinse habits mid dial in muddies your results when Dialing In Grind Size. Rinsing is stage one of The Anatomy of a Pour Over, so whatever you decide, do it the same way every time.

title: Brew Time and Total Contact Time

sections.t: Total contact time is the span from your first drop of water to the last drip out of the The Dripper Explained — how long water and coffee are in contact. It is one of three big extraction levers alongside Grind Size for Pour Over and Water Temperature for Brewing: longer contact, more Extraction Yield and Strength. But unlike ratio, time is partly an output — it emerges from your grind and pour as much as you set it.

[!note] These are guidelines, not gospel Times vary with dose, grind, and recipe. Use them as a sanity check, not a rule. Brewer Typical total time Notes Hario V60 (single) 2:30–3:30 Fast, steep cone Kalita Wave 3:00–4:00 Flat bed, steadier Chemex 4:00–5:00+ Thick filter, slow Origami Dripper 2:30–3:30 Filter dependent Single Cup vs Batch Pour Over\ 5:00–8:00 Deeper bed, more water

sections.h: Time Is Both Cause and Symptom
sections.t: You influence contact time directly — pour faster or slower, Pulse vs Continuous Pouring — but the bed's drainage sets the rest. That makes a surprising total time a diagnostic , not just a target: Running long Running short Grind Size for Pour Over\ too fine Grind too coarse Fines clogging the The Drawdown\ Bypass and Channeling\ / bypass Over agitation packed the bed Too little Agitation and Turbulence\ Risk: Under Extraction and Over Extraction\, bitter Risk: under extraction, sour

[!tip] Fix time with grind, not the clock If your brew finishes too fast and tastes weak, grind finer to slow flow and raise extraction. If it drags long and tastes bitter, grind coarser . Chasing a number by pouring frantically just trades one fault for another. Time also trades off against the other levers. A finer grind hits target extraction in less time; a hotter Water Temperature for Brewing extracts faster too. This is why two valid recipes can post very different total times and both taste great — they balance the levers differently. The The Coffee Brewing Control Chart shows that what matters is landing the right Extraction Yield and Strength, by whatever combination of grind, temperature, and time gets you there. [!warning] Don't worship the timer A "perfect" 2:30 means nothing if the cup is sour. Taste first; use time to explain why it tastes that way and which lever to move.

title: Single Cup vs Batch Pour Over

sections.t: Brewing one cup and brewing for a table are not the same act scaled up — they are different problems. As you increase the dose and water, the bed gets deeper , the water column gets taller , and the physics of The Drawdown and Extraction Yield and Strength shift. Technique that nails a 15 g Hario V60 can ruin a 50 g batch. This note covers what changes and how to adjust.

sections.h: What Changes When You Scale Up 📈
sections.t: [!note] Bed depth is the hidden variable Doubling the coffee does not double the brewer's width — it mostly deepens the bed. Water now travels through more coffee, so it spends longer in contact and extracts more aggressively per pass. Factor Single cup Batch Bed depth Shallow Deep Brew Time and Total Contact Time\ 2:30–3:30 5:00–8:00 Extraction tendency Baseline Runs higher Bypass and Channeling\ risk Moderate Higher Pour management Easy Demanding

Because a deep bed extracts more and drains slower, you typically: Grind coarser. A deeper bed lengthens contact time on its own; coarsening compensates to avoid Under Extraction and Over Extraction and a stalled drawdown. Keep the The Brew Ratio fixed. Strength is set by ratio regardless of size — scale both numbers together. Pour patiently in stages. Big water volumes are easy to dump; deliberate Pulse vs Continuous Pouring keep the deep bed even and reduce Bypass and Channeling. Mind temperature. A larger brew loses heat over its longer run; preheat the The Dripper Explained and consider the role of Water Temperature for Brewing. [!tip] Use a brewer built to scale Flat bottom and large brewers — the Kalita Wave 185, the Chemex, big Flat Bottom Drippers — April and Orea — keep a shallower, more even bed than a steep cone as volume grows. They drain more uniformly and forgive deeper beds. A full Batch Pour Over Recipe lives in the recipes domain.

The single cup is where you learn. It is fast, cheap on coffee, and gives quick feedback for Dialing In Grind Size — brew, taste, adjust, repeat in minutes. Most technique notes here assume a single cup precisely because the feedback loop is tight.

For guests, for the office, or for cold storage, batch is simply efficient — one brew, many cups. The trade is a longer, less forgiving process where small errors compound across the deeper bed. Get your single cup fundamentals solid first; batch rewards a brewer who already reads The Drawdown and Brew Time and Total Contact Time fluently.

Most bad cups come from a short list of recurring errors — and nearly all have quick fixes once you can name them. This note is the field guide to the frequent stumbles. For a symptom first diagnostic walkthrough, pair it with the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide.

[!warning] Nine mistakes behind most disappointing brews Work down this list before blaming the beans. Each links to the technique that fixes it. Mistake Result Quick fix 1 Guessing weights Inconsistent strength Use a Scales, Timers, and Servers\; set a The Brew Ratio\ 2 Stale or wrong Coffee Freshness and Degassing\ Flat, lifeless cup Buy fresh; rest 4–14 days 3 Bad Grind Size for Pour Over\ Sour or bitter Dialing In Grind Size\ grind 4 Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders\ Uneven, Fines and Boulders\ heavy Use a burr grinder 5 Skipping the The Bloom\ Uneven, Under Extraction and Over Extraction\ Bloom 30–45 s 6 Wrong Water Temperature for Brewing\ Sour (cold) / bitter (hot) 90–96 °C target 7 Pouring on the walls Bypass and Channeling\ Keep pours inside the bed 8 Over or under Agitation and Turbulence\ Harsh or weak Match agitation to grind 9 Ignoring Why Water Matters\ quality Dull, scaly Use good Water for Coffee\

Grind is the master dial. A sour, thin cup usually means too coarse; a bitter, draggy one means too fine. Before you touch anything else, get the Grind Size for Pour Over right and confirm your grinder gives a Particle Distribution and Uniformity particle spread. Skipping the bloom is the most common beginner error. Without it, escaping CO2, Degassing, and the Bloom Science pushes water away from dry grounds and you get patchy extraction. Thirty seconds fixes it — see The Bloom. Wrong water temperature sabotages good technique silently. Brew with off the boil water that's too cool and the cup sours; brew too hot with a Light Roast and Specialty Coffee you may scorch it. Aim for the Water Temperature for Brewing for your roast.

[!tip] Once the basics are clean, look here Inconsistent pours between brews — you can't Dialing In Grind Size a moving target. Ignoring The Drawdown — a fast or stalled drain is telling you something. Chasing a timer instead of taste — see Brew Time and Total Contact Time. Reusing or not rinsing filters inconsistently — see Pre Wetting the Filter. The thread running through all of these is consistency : change one variable at a time, keep everything else fixed, and taste deliberately. That discipline turns mistakes into a feedback loop instead of a mystery.

This is the home of every brewing recipe in the knowledge base — the part built to feed a future recipe app, so each note is precise, structured, and machine readable. A recipe is simply a documented set of choices about The Brew Ratio, Grind Size for Pour Over, Water Temperature for Brewing, and Pouring Technique that someone found reliably delicious. Learn to read one and you can reproduce a champion's cup in your own kitchen. Before diving in, read How to Read a Coffee Recipe — it explains the Recipe Card schema and every field you'll see repeated at the top of each note. Then Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes teaches you how to bend any recipe to your taste by changing one variable at a time. Together they turn a list of numbers into something you can actually tune. ⚙️ We sort recipes into three serious categories , each tagged in frontmatter so the app can filter them: official (the maker's own method), competition (World Brewers Cup routines), and community (popular creator and roaster recipes) — plus one off label category just for fun: soup brewed in coffee gear. Browse the category indexes — Official Brewer Recipes, Competition Recipes, Community Recipes, and Brewing Soup with Coffee Gear — or use the tables below.

Not coffee at all — the one novelty in the base. These repurpose immersion coffee brewers to make soup, written to the same Recipe Card schema so the app reads them like any other recipe. See Brewing Soup with Coffee Gear for why it works. Recipe Brewer "Ratio" Temp AeroPress Soup Recipe AeroPress 1:8 base to water 80 90°C OXO Rapid Brewer Soup Recipe OXO Rapid Brewer 1:10 base to water 90 96°C Every recipe here connects back to the fundamentals — when a number puzzles you, follow the link to the chapter that explains why . Start simple, brew often, and change one thing at a time.

title: How to Read a Coffee Recipe

sections.t: Every recipe in this knowledge base follows the same structure, deliberately, so that a human can scan it in five seconds and a machine can parse it without guessing. This note is the contract : it defines each field you'll see in the frontmatter and in the Recipe Card callout near the top of every recipe note. Once you know the schema, every recipe reads the same way. 🧩

Near the top of each recipe you'll find a callout — a key/value table that mirrors the structured frontmatter and adds a plain language Resulting cup row. It's the at a glance spec sheet. The same numbers live in the YAML frontmatter above the H1, which is what an app actually reads.

[!note] The recipe contract Field Meaning Always for an actual recipe note (false/absent on index notes). , , , or (off label). The device, e.g. , , . Grams of dry coffee. Total grams of brew water (including bloom). Coffee to water as — see The Brew Ratio. Brew temperature in Celsius; a range like is allowed. Relative size plus a reference point, e.g. medium fine. See Grind Size for Pour Over. Grams of water used in the The Bloom\. Bloom duration in seconds. Target total time as — see Brew Time and Total Contact Time. The Roast Levels for Pour Over\ the recipe flatters. Originating person or brand, plus year.

The numbers are not independent. and together imply the ; a 15 g dose with 250 g water is a 1:16.7 ratio. The is conventionally two to three times the dose — see CO2, Degassing, and the Bloom Science for why. The , , and form an extraction triangle: finer grind, hotter water, and longer time each push Extraction Yield and Strength up, so recipes balance them. Change one and you usually compensate elsewhere, which is the whole subject of Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes.

Below the card, each recipe lists a numbered, timed pour schedule . Every step gives three things: the clock time (from when the bloom water hits), the cumulative water in grams your scale should read at that moment, and the action (pour, swirl, wait, drain). Cumulative — not per pour — weight is used because that's what you watch on the Scales, Timers, and Servers as you brew. Hit each number on time and the recipe reproduces. [!tip] A recipe is a starting point, not a law Treat published numbers as a calibrated baseline. Your Coffee Beans and Roast, Grinding, Water for Coffee, and Dripper Materials and Heat Retention all differ from the originator's, so expect to dial in. The Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide tells you which way to nudge.

A pour over has only a handful of knobs, and learning what each one does to the cup is the difference between following recipes and writing them. This note is a tuning guide: change one variable at a time, taste, and move deliberately. Almost everything below comes back to one idea — pushing Extraction Yield and Strength up or down — explained fully in The Science of Extraction. ⚖️ [!tip] The golden rule of dialing in Change one variable at a time . If you adjust grind and temperature together and the cup improves, you won't know which fixed it — and the next bag will send you back to square one. See Dialing In Grind Size.

sections.h: The Knobs, and Which Way They Push
sections.t: Variable Turn it up → Turn it down → The Brew Ratio\ (more water) weaker, thinner, often brighter stronger, heavier, more intense Grind Size for Pour Over\ (finer) more extraction; sweeter then bitter; slower flow less extraction; sour/sharp if too coarse; faster flow Water Temperature for Brewing\ (hotter) more extraction; more body and bitterness less extraction; more acidity, cleaner Agitation and Turbulence\ (more) more extraction; more body; risk of Bypass and Channeling\ gentler, cleaner, more delineated The Bloom\ (longer/bigger) better degassing, more even later pours risk of stalling if grounds dry out Brew Time and Total Contact Time\ (longer) more extraction overall less extraction overall

If a brew tastes sour, sharp, thin, or salty , it is usually under extracted — see Under Extraction and Over Extraction. Grind finer, brew hotter, add a little agitation, or extend contact time. If it tastes bitter, hollow, drying, or ashy , it is usually over extracted — back off: grind coarser, cool the water, pour more gently. The clean middle, where sweetness and acidity balance, is the target the The Coffee Brewing Control Chart maps. Strength and extraction are separate axes, and confusing them is the most common tuning mistake. Ratio sets strength — how much coffee is dissolved into how much water. Grind, temperature, time, and agitation set extraction — what fraction of the available flavor you pulled. A cup can be strong and under extracted (sour and intense) or weak and over extracted (thin and bitter). Read Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) and Extraction Yield and Strength to keep them straight.

A recipe tuned for a Light Roast and Specialty Coffee will over extract a dark one, because dark roasts are more porous and soluble. As a rule of thumb: lighter roast wants finer grind, hotter water, and more agitation ; darker roast wants coarser grind, cooler water, and a gentler hand . Roast Levels for Pour Over covers the spectrum, and Coffee Freshness and Degassing explains why a fresh bag blooms vigorously and needs a longer, calmer bloom. ☕

Official recipes are the methods published by the people who make the brewer — Hario for the V60, Kalita for the Wave, the Chemex Corporation, AeroPress, and so on. They matter because the device was designed around them : the geometry of the cone, the number and size of the drainage holes, the ribs, and the filter thickness all assume a particular grind, ratio, and pour. Starting with the maker's recipe means starting where the engineering expects you to be. 🔧 These recipes tend to be deliberately approachable — forgiving ratios, simple pours, round numbers — because the manufacturer wants a first time buyer to succeed. They are an excellent baseline before you graduate to the more opinionated Competition Recipes and Community Recipes. Where a brand publishes several variations over the years, the note here documents a representative current method and flags where specifics are approximate. Recipe Brewer Dose Ratio Temp Grind Hario V60 Official Recipe Hario V60 15 g 1:15 92 96°C medium fine Kalita Wave Official Recipe Kalita Wave 21 g 1:16 93 96°C medium Chemex Official Recipe Chemex 42 g 1:15 94 96°C medium coarse AeroPress Official Recipe AeroPress 17 g concentrate, then diluted 80 85°C fine Clever Dripper Official Recipe The Clever Dripper 18 g 1:16 93 96°C medium OXO Rapid Brewer Recipe OXO Rapid Brewer 22 g 1:16 93 96°C medium Origami Dripper Recipe Origami Dripper 15 g 1:16 92 94°C medium fine Notice how the conical brewers (V60, Origami) lean finer and the flat bottom brewers (Kalita, OXO) and immersion brewers (Clever) sit a touch coarser — geometry driving grind. Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers explains why. To understand any number in these tables, follow the link, and consult How to Read a Coffee Recipe for the full field schema.

Hario's own method for the Hario V60 is the natural starting point for the world's most popular pour over cone. It is intentionally simple — a single bloom and two or three steady pours at a 1:15 The Brew Ratio — and it shows off exactly what the cone was engineered to do: a fast, clean, gravity driven The Drawdown through tall spiral ribs and a single large hole. 🌀 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario V60 (01 or 02) Dose 15 g Water 225 g total Ratio 1:15 Temp 92 96°C Grind medium fine, like table salt — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 30 g for 30 s Brew time 3:00 Roast light to medium Source Hario, published method (2010s) Resulting cup Clean and bright with light to medium body; clarity forward, the V60 signature

1. 0:00 — Add 30 g (cumulative 30 g) to wet all grounds; let it The Bloom 30 s. 2. 0:30 — Pour in slow concentric circles to 150 g (cumulative 150 g). 3. 1:15 — Pour again to 225 g (cumulative 225 g), keeping the bed level. 4. 2:00 3:00 — Let it draw down fully; the bed should leave a flat, even wall.

The V60's open cone and single large aperture mean the brewer controls flow with the pour and the grind, not the dripper — water leaves almost as fast as you add it. That demands a slightly finer grind than a flat bottom brewer and rewards a controlled Pouring Technique from a Gooseneck Kettles. Keep the slurry moving in gentle circles and avoid pouring straight down the side, which causes Bypass and Channeling. Hario scales the recipe linearly: many shops brew 20 g to 300 g or 30 g to 450 g with the same rhythm. The official numbers are a baseline, not gospel — Hario itself prints slightly different figures across markets and years, so treat the temperature and counts as approximate. From here, the more developed James Hoffmann V60 Technique and Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method are the natural next steps, both built on this same cone.

The Kalita Wave is the friendly, forgiving cousin of the V60. Its flat bottom and three small drainage holes restrict and even out the flow, so the brewer's pour matters less and consistency comes easier — which is exactly what makes it a favorite for cafés serving cup after identical cup. The official method leans on pulse pouring to keep the bed submerged and level. 🧱 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Kalita Wave 185 (stainless or glass) Dose 21 g Water 340 g total Ratio 1:16 Temp 93 96°C Grind medium, a touch coarser than Grind Size for Pour Over\ Bloom 50 g for 30 s Brew time 3:30 Roast medium, also flatters lighter Source Kalita, published method Resulting cup Sweet, rounded, even; more body and forgiveness than a V60, still clean

1. 0:00 — Add 50 g (cumulative 50 g); The Bloom 30 s. 2. 0:30 — Pulse to 150 g (cumulative 150 g) with small circular pours. 3. 1:15 — Pulse to 250 g (cumulative 250 g). 4. 1:50 — Pulse to 340 g (cumulative 340 g), keeping water above the bed. 5. 3:00 3:30 — Drawdown completes; the flat wave filter leaves a level bed.

The Wave's geometry does the regulating that you'd otherwise do by hand in a Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers. The fluted wave paper filter touches the dripper wall only at its ridges, insulating the slurry and preventing the filter from sealing against the side — so flow stays predictable. Because the three holes meter the outflow, the recipe uses multiple small pulses rather than long continuous pours: each pulse tops the water back up and gently stirs, building even Extraction Yield and Strength without aggressive Agitation and Turbulence. The flat bed makes the Wave unusually tolerant of a sloppy pour. Scale it linearly for the larger 185 size or down for the 155. Many brewers cool the water slightly for darker roasts; the published temperature band is approximate and worth dialing to your beans.

The Chemex is as much design object as brewer — a single piece of hourglass glass with a wooden collar, sold since 1941 and held in the Museum of Modern Art. Its defining feature is the proprietary bonded filter , up to 30% heavier than ordinary paper, which produces the cleanest, most sediment free cup in pour over. That thick filter also slows things down, so Chemex recipes run coarser and longer than a V60. 🍷 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Chemex (6 cup shown) Dose 42 g Water 630 g total Ratio 1:15 Temp 94 96°C Grind medium coarse, like kosher salt — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 90 g for 45 s Brew time 4:00 5:00 Roast medium, also light Source Chemex Corporation, published method Resulting cup Exceptionally clean and tea like; light body, bright, sediment free

1. 0:00 — Add 90 g (cumulative 90 g) to saturate evenly; The Bloom 45 s. 2. 0:45 — Pour in slow circles to 300 g (cumulative 300 g). 3. 1:45 — Pour to 480 g (cumulative 480 g). 4. 2:45 — Pour to 630 g (cumulative 630 g), keeping the bed level. 5. 4:00 5:00 — Drawdown finishes; remove the filter promptly.

The thick bonded filter traps fines and oils that thinner papers let through, which is why the Chemex cup is so strikingly clear and low in body — and why it benefits from a coarser grind to keep the long drawdown from stalling. Always seat the filter with the triple folded layer over the pouring spout and pre wet it generously (see Pre Wetting the Filter) — unrinsed Chemex paper imparts a notable papery taste. The big thermal mass of the glass and the slow flow make this a method that rewards a hotter pour and a Gooseneck Kettles for control. Chemex scales beautifully for entertaining — see Batch Pour Over Recipe for larger volumes. The 1:15 to 1:17 band and the exact pour counts are approximate; the constant is the coarse grind and the patient, level pour.

The AeroPress was invented in 2005 by Aerobie's Alan Adler — the same engineer behind the record setting flying ring — to make a single, smooth, low bitterness cup fast. The original instructions are unlike any pour over: relatively cool water, a quick stir, then air pressure plunging through a thin paper disc within about a minute, producing a strong concentrate you then dilute to taste , Americano style. Adler deliberately keeps contact time short — he considers a long steep contrary to the device's point. It's an immersion and pressure hybrid, included here as a contrast to gravity brewing. 💨 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer AeroPress (standard, upright) Dose 17 g (one rounded official scoop) Water 100 g over the grounds, then dilute Ratio concentrate 1:6, diluted to 1:13 1:16 in the cup Temp 80°C / 175°F (cooler than most pour over) Grind fine, finer than Grind Size for Pour Over\ Bloom none in the classic method Brew time 10 s stir, then 20 40 s press ( 1:00 total) Roast medium to dark Source Aerobie / Alan Adler, 2005 Resulting cup Smooth, round, low acidity and low bitterness; adjustable strength via dilution

1. Setup — Insert a rinsed paper filter in the cap, screw on, place upright on a sturdy mug; add 1 rounded scoop ( 17 g) and shake to level the bed. 2. 0:00 — Pour 80°C water to about 100 g (Adler's original fills to chamber level 1 1.5 for a single scoop). 3. 0:00 0:10 — Stir 10 seconds with the paddle. 4. 0:10 — Insert the plunger and press gently and steadily for 20 40 seconds ; stop at the hiss. 5. Finish — You now have a concentrate; top up with hot water to your preferred strength, Americano style.

Two things make the AeroPress forgiving: the short contact time and the cool water , which together hold bitterness and Under Extraction and Over Extraction in check even with a fine grind. The plunge isn't espresso pressure — it's gentle, just enough to push water through the puck in seconds. Because you brew a concentrate and dilute, strength is decoupled from extraction : you can pull a clean, well extracted shot and then set its intensity in the cup, which is why the device is so beginner proof. The AeroPress is famously hackable — countless variations exist, including the popular inverted method and the more developed James Hoffmann Ultimate AeroPress. Official guidance has shifted over the years (newer cards suggest hotter water), so treat the temperature as approximate and tune to your roast.

The The Clever Dripper solves immersion's one inconvenience with a clever valve: a silicone stopper in the base that stays shut until you set the dripper on a cup , which opens it and drains the brew through a paper filter. So you get the full immersion sweetness and near foolproof consistency of a French Press, but the clean cup of a paper filter. It's the most forgiving brewer in the book. ✅ [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer The Clever Dripper Dose 18 g Water 290 g total Ratio 1:16 Temp 93 96°C Grind medium, drip filter size — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 40 g for 30 s (optional) Brew time 3:30 4:00 steep, then drain Roast medium; flatters light and dark too Source Clever Coffee Dripper, published method Resulting cup Sweet, full, rounded; clean paper filtered clarity with immersion body

1. Setup — Rinse a paper filter in the closed Clever (off the cup so the valve stays shut). Add 18 g coffee. 2. 0:00 — Pour 40 g (cumulative 40 g) and stir gently; The Bloom 30 s. 3. 0:30 — Pour to 290 g (cumulative 290 g); give one gentle stir to wet all grounds. 4. 0:30 3:30 — Steep undisturbed (lid on to hold heat). 5. 3:30 — Optional gentle stir, then place on the mug to open the valve and drain ( 30 45 s).

Because the coffee steeps fully submerged , extraction is even and time driven rather than pour driven — there is no Pouring Technique to master and almost no Bypass and Channeling to worry about. That makes the Clever ideal for beginners, for batch testing beans, and for tea like delicate coffees. Total steep time is your main lever: shorter for a brighter, lighter cup, longer for more body and sweetness, with grind as the secondary adjustment. Keep the lid on to protect Water Temperature for Brewing during the steep. The Clever sits at the heart of the immersion family and pairs naturally with the switch valve The Hario Switch Hybrid Method, which adds percolation pours on top of the same idea. The bloom is optional and the exact steep time is approximate — many brewers simply pour all the water at once and steep 4 minutes.

The OXO Rapid Brewer is OXO's take on the The Clever Dripper idea: a flat bottom dripper with a shut off valve in the base so coffee steeps in full immersion, then drains through a flat paper filter when you press a lever or set it on the carafe. The flat bed and immersion steep make it very repeatable and friendly to anyone who finds free pour intimidating. ⏱️ [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer OXO Rapid Brewer Dose 22 g Water 350 g total Ratio 1:16 Temp 93 96°C Grind medium, drip filter size — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 45 g for 30 s Brew time 3:30 steep, then drain Roast medium Source OXO Brew, published method Resulting cup Balanced and sweet with a clean finish; flat bed evenness, immersion body

1. Setup — Seat and rinse a flat paper filter with the valve closed ; add 22 g coffee, level the bed. 2. 0:00 — Pour 45 g (cumulative 45 g) and stir to wet all grounds; The Bloom 30 s. 3. 0:30 — Pour to 350 g (cumulative 350 g); one gentle stir. 4. 0:30 3:30 — Steep undisturbed. 5. 3:30 — Open the valve / set on the carafe to drain ( 45 60 s).

Like the Clever, the OXO is an immersion brewer that finishes through paper , so it combines a full, sweet, even extraction with a clean cup and almost no technique requirement. The flat bottom keeps the bed level as it drains, which helps even Extraction Yield and Strength versus a steep cone. Your two main levers are steep time (longer for body and sweetness) and grind (finer for more extraction); the pour itself barely matters, which is the point. OXO also sells a fully automatic pour over machine — this note covers the manual immersion dripper . The bloom step is optional and the steep time is approximate; many users simply add all the water, steep four minutes, and drain. Compare with the Clever Dripper Official Recipe to see how close the two designs are.

The Origami Dripper is the photogenic Japanese cone with twenty fluted ridges that gained fame after Tetsu Kasuya was seen using one. Its trick is versatility : the ridges hold the paper off the wall for fast, V60 like flow, and it accepts both conical V60 filters and flat bottom Kalita Wave filters , so a single dripper can behave like either family depending on the paper you choose. This recipe documents a representative standard method using a conical filter. 🎨 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Origami Dripper (conical filter) Dose 15 g Water 240 g total Ratio 1:16 Temp 92 94°C Grind medium fine — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 45 g for 30 s Brew time 2:30 3:00 Roast light to medium Source Origami / representative standard method Resulting cup Bright, clean, and fast flowing; V60 like clarity, slightly more body

1. 0:00 — Add 45 g (cumulative 45 g); The Bloom 30 s, with a gentle swirl. 2. 0:30 — Pour in circles to 120 g (cumulative 120 g). 3. 1:10 — Pour to 180 g (cumulative 180 g). 4. 1:45 — Pour to 240 g (cumulative 240 g), keeping the bed level. 5. 2:30 3:00 — Drawdown completes; the fluted ridges keep flow brisk.

The Origami's air channels behind every ridge mean the filter never seals to the wall, so flow stays fast and even — closer to a Hario V60 than a Kalita Wave when fitted with a conical paper. Swap in a Wave style flat filter and the same dripper slows down and rounds out, behaving like a flat bottom brewer; many owners keep both papers on hand. Because it drains quickly, it likes a medium fine grind and a controlled Pouring Technique, and it sits happily on a matching wooden or resin holder. This is a representative recipe rather than a single "official" Origami spec — the brand and its many ambassadors publish a range of methods, so treat the exact pour counts and temperature as approximate and dial to your beans. Because Kasuya popularized it, the Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method is a natural partner recipe on this dripper.

Competition recipes come from the The World Brewers Cup, the global stage where baristas brew filter coffee to a panel of judges and explain every choice. These routines are obsessively optimized — every gram, degree, and second justified out loud — and several have escaped the competition floor to reshape how the rest of us brew at home. The Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method in particular became one of the most influential pour over frameworks of the last decade. 🥇 A caveat worth stating plainly: competition recipes are tuned to one extraordinary coffee, one grinder, one water, and one performer . They are fascinating and often brilliant, but they are not always the most practical everyday recipe — the competitors usually brew a rare, expensive, immaculately roasted lot. Read them for the ideas (the 40/60 split, equal pulse pours, temperature staging) as much as the exact numbers, several of which are reconstructed from interviews and are approximate. Recipe Year Brewer Ratio Temp Signature idea Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method 2016 Hario V60 1:15 92°C 40% taste / 60% strength split Matt Winton Triangulation Method 2021 Hario V60 1:15 88 93°C five equal pours, staged temps Stefanos Domatiotis Brewers Cup Recipe 2014 Hario V60 1:15 90 92°C precision and bean selection Emi Fukahori Brewers Cup Recipe 2018 Hario V60 1:13 80 95°C temperature staged hybrid pours To understand why these choices work, lean on the fundamentals: The Brew Ratio, Water Temperature for Brewing, Agitation and Turbulence, and Extraction Yield and Strength. Then see how home creators distilled these ideas into everyday recipes in Community Recipes.

When Tetsu Kasuya won the 2016 World Brewers Cup, he introduced a framework so clear and adjustable that it became the default modern V60 method for serious home brewers worldwide. The 4:6 method divides the brew water into a first 40% that controls taste (sweetness vs. acidity) and a last 60% that controls strength — and crucially, it makes both dialable by changing how you split each portion , not by changing the recipe's bones. 🔢 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario V60 Dose 20 g Water 300 g total Ratio 1:15 Temp 93°C (light roast; 88°C medium, 83°C dark) Grind coarse, coarser than standard Grind Size for Pour Over\ Bloom first pour (50 g here), 45 s rest Brew time 3:30 Roast light to medium Source Tetsu Kasuya, 2016 World Brewers Cup Resulting cup Clean, sweet, adjustable; bright or balanced and light or strong to taste The water is split into two phases . The first 40% (120 g for a 300 g brew) is given in two pours whose balance sets flavor: equal pours (60 g + 60 g) for balance, a smaller then larger split (e.g. 50 g + 70 g, or Kasuya's own 40 g + 80 g) for more sweetness, the reverse for more acidity. The last 60% (180 g) is split into more pours for a stronger cup, fewer for a milder one — typically three equal 60 g pours. Pours are spaced at a steady 45 s interval , each begun once the bed has nearly drained, which is what lets a coarse grind extract efficiently. Kasuya sets temperature by roast : 93°C for light, 88°C for medium, 83°C for dark.

This is the balanced, sweetness leaning version (50/70 split, then three pours): 1. 0:00 — Pour 50 g (cumulative 50 g); rest until 0:45 (The Bloom). 2. 0:45 — Pour 70 g (cumulative 120 g); let it drain. 3. 1:30 — Pour 60 g (cumulative 180 g); let it drain. 4. 2:15 — Pour 60 g (cumulative 240 g); let it drain. 5. 3:00 — Pour 60 g (cumulative 300 g); let it drain. 6. 3:30 — Remove the dripper as the last water clears.

The genius is decoupling the two things drinkers care about . The early water, when the grounds are freshest and most soluble, disproportionately shapes the acid/sweet balance — so adjusting that first 40% tunes flavor. The later water, added once the bed is established, mostly adds strength — so adjusting the number of those pours tunes intensity. Letting each pour drain completely (a "pour and wait" rhythm) keeps the relatively coarse grind from under extracting and yields a clean cup with minimal Bypass and Channeling. The 4:6 flatters bright, Light Roast and Specialty Coffee single origins where you want clarity and control. Kasuya popularized it on the Hario V60 and the Origami Dripper; the temperature and exact timings are his stated baseline and can be nudged for your beans.

Matt Winton won the 2021 World Brewers Cup with a deceptively plain looking V60 routine — the "five pour method" : five equal pours of 60 g each , every one with the same circular motion, spaced 30 s apart, and crucially no stirring . He replaces the agitation of a stir with the turbulence of many aggressive pours, landing the brew on target in even, predictable increments. 🧮 [!note] About the name Winton himself calls this the Five Pour Method ; he does not market it as "triangulation." That label is a community nickname for his methodical, repeatable, target the same cup approach. The recipe below follows his documented, Hario published numbers. [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario V60 (metal) Dose 20 g Water 300 g total (5 × 60 g) Ratio 1:15 Temp 93°C (constant; or slightly off the boil) Grind coarse — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom first 60 g pour, 30 s Brew time 3:30 total Roast light Source Matt Winton, 2021 World Brewers Cup Resulting cup Very clean, layered, and aromatic; high clarity, light body

Five equal 60 g pours at a constant 93°C , each followed by a 30 s wait — no stir; pour a little aggressively to build extraction: 1. 0:00 — Pour to 60 g (cumulative 60 g); saturate all grounds, The Bloom 30 s. 2. 0:30 — Pour to 120 g (cumulative 120 g), circular motion centre to edge. 3. 1:00 — Pour to 180 g (cumulative 180 g). 4. 1:30 — Pour to 240 g (cumulative 240 g). 5. 2:00 — Pour to 300 g (cumulative 300 g). 6. 2:00 3:30 — Final drawdown completes; dial grind so it finishes near 3:30.

By making every pour identical in size and motion , Winton removes pour to pour variability — the biggest source of inconsistency in free pour V60. Each 60 g addition agitates the bed and tops the water back up, building extraction in equal steps without ever reaching for a spoon. His no stir stance is deliberate: stirring costs time and adds inconsistency on stage, so he gets the same agitation by pouring near the limit of his kettle's flow rate. Five pours of a single, The Role of Temperature in Extraction (93°C, just off the boil) on a Light Roast and Specialty Coffee coffee build complete, even extraction while keeping the cup clean. This method rewards a clean, well developed light roast and a Gooseneck Kettles you can pour consistently and assertively. The load bearing idea is five equal 60 g pours, 30 s apart, no stir — dial the grind so total time lands near 3:30.

Stefanos Domatiotis of Taf Coffee in Athens won the 2014 World Brewers Cup for Greece, and his routine is remembered less for a single gimmick than for uncompromising precision and bean selection — an immaculate light roasted Panama Geisha from Ninety Plus (he was the first champion to use a Ninety Plus coffee) brewed on a Hario V60. He used two kettles to hold temperature exactly where he wanted it and poured calm and even. He is one of the brewers who helped cement the idea that a great cup is won at the farm and the roaster , then merely not ruined at the brew bar. 🏛️ [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario V60 Dose 30 g (approx.) Water 450 g total (approx.) Ratio 1:15 Temp 90 92°C Grind medium fine — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 60 g for 45 s Brew time 3:30 Roast light, single origin Panama Geisha (Ninety Plus) Source Stefanos Domatiotis (Taf), 2014 World Brewers Cup Resulting cup Floral, tea like, exceptionally clean; delicate body, vivid aromatics

A representative reconstruction of a gentle, multi pour V60 in his style (exact competition figures are not fully public — treat as approximate): 1. 0:00 — Pour to 60 g (cumulative 60 g); The Bloom 45 s. 2. 0:45 — Pour gently to 200 g (cumulative 200 g). 3. 1:40 — Pour to 325 g (cumulative 325 g). 4. 2:30 — Pour to 450 g (cumulative 450 g), keeping the bed level. 5. 3:30 — Drawdown completes. [!warning] Specifics are approximate What is well documented: he brewed a Ninety Plus Panama Geisha on a V60 using two kettles for temperature control. His exact 2014 dose, pour weights, and timings are not published in a single authoritative source the way Kasuya's or Fukahori's are. The numbers above are a sensible reconstruction in his documented style; trust the approach (light Geisha, two kettle temperature control, gentle even pours) over the precise figures.

The lesson of this routine is restraint . With a coffee this special, the job is to extract its delicate florals and sweetness cleanly and completely without dragging out anything harsh — so the water is kept on the cooler side, the grind moderate, the pour gentle, and Agitation and Turbulence minimal. Everything serves Extraction Yield and Strength. Domatiotis is also a roaster, and his competitions underline how much the cup depends on Coffee Processing Methods, Coffee Varietals, and roast before a single drop of water is poured. This is an aspirational, bean led recipe rather than a daily driver. To chase the same clarity at home, start from a clean The Standard V60 Recipe and a Light Roast and Specialty Coffee, then refine.

Emi Fukahori of MAME in Zurich won the 2018 World Brewers Cup — the first woman to do so — with two memorable departures from the crowd. She brewed an experimental Laurina (a naturally low caffeine variety from Daterra in Brazil, processed by semi carbonic maceration) rather than the near ubiquitous gesha, and she brewed it on a GINA — a valve brewer that toggles between full immersion and drip — while staging her water temperature cool, then hot, then cool to shape exactly which flavors each phase pulled. 🌡️ [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer GINA (valve immersion/drip; a Hario V60–The Clever Dripper\ hybrid) Dose 17 g Water 220 g total Ratio 1:13 Temp staged 80°C → 95°C → 80°C Grind coarse — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom first 50 g pour (immersion), 45 s Brew time 2:55 Roast light (Laurina, semi carbonic maceration) Source Emi Fukahori (MAME), 2018 World Brewers Cup Resulting cup Sweet, fruity, and round; gentle winey acidity, clean finish

Her documented three phase routine alternates the GINA valve between immersion (closed) and drip (open): 1. 0:00 — Immersion: valve closed; pour 50 g at 80°C (cumulative 50 g); steep 45 s for sweetness (The Bloom). 2. 0:45 — Drip: valve open; pour 100 g at 95°C (cumulative 150 g) until 1:45 to open up complex acidity. 3. 1:45 — Immersion: valve closed; pour 70 g at 80°C (cumulative 220 g) for 45 s to build juicy body. 4. 2:30 — Drip: valve open; final drawdown 25 s for a clean finish, completing 2:55. [!note] On a single dripper Without a GINA you can approximate this with The Hario Switch Hybrid Method (which has the same immersion/drip valve) or a Hario V60 with two kettles, holding the valve or pour to match the 80/95/80 temperature staging. The weights and temperatures above are her published figures.

Temperature is one of the strongest levers on Extraction Yield and Strength, and Fukahori used it like a dimmer. The cool immersion first pour coaxes out delicate sweetness and fruit without harshness while the grounds are most soluble; the hot middle drip drives the bulk of the extraction and develops complex acidity; the cool final immersion gives juicy body and rinses through cleanly without dragging out bitterness. Pulling this off requires the GINA's valve plus either two kettles or a precise variable temperature kettle — see The Role of Temperature in Extraction and Water Temperature for Brewing. Her win is also a reminder that bean choice is a competitive weapon : a fragile, low caffeine Coffee Varietals like Laurina rewards exactly this gentle, staged handling. At home, you can borrow the idea by simply letting your kettle cool a few degrees for the final pour.

Community recipes are the methods that spread through YouTube, blogs, forums, and roaster brew guides — refined by educators and professionals, then road tested by thousands of home brewers. They sit between the friendly Official Brewer Recipes and the rarefied Competition Recipes: more developed than the box instructions, but designed for everyday beans, ordinary water, and a normal kitchen. This is where most people find their daily driver. ▶️ The defining feature of this category is repeatability for real homes . James Hoffmann's swirl, Scott Rao's spin, and Lance Hedrick's pour from height all exist to remove a specific source of inconsistency, so that an amateur with a decent grinder can hit the same cup twice. Where a recipe targets a particular brewer or roast, the note says so. Recipe Creator Brewer Ratio Temp James Hoffmann V60 Technique James Hoffmann Hario V60 1:16.7 95°C James Hoffmann Ultimate AeroPress James Hoffmann AeroPress 1:18 100°C The Rao Spin Scott Rao Hario V60 1:16 94 96°C Lance Hedrick Pour Over Technique Lance Hedrick Hario V60 1:16 90 99°C The Hario Switch Hybrid Method Various / Kasuya Hario Switch 1:15 90 95°C Onyx Coffee Lab V60 Recipe Onyx Coffee Lab Hario V60 1:16.7 91 96°C The Standard V60 Recipe Composite Hario V60 1:16 94°C Iced and Cold Pour Over Composite V60 over ice 1:15 93 96°C Batch Pour Over Recipe Composite flat bottom / Chemex 1:16 94 96°C If you're new, start with The Standard V60 Recipe or the James Hoffmann V60 Technique, then explore the others as you build a Building Your Palate. Everything here assumes the fundamentals in Brewing Technique; follow the links when a step puzzles you.

James Hoffmann's "Ultimate V60 Technique" is probably the single most watched pour over tutorial in the world, and for good reason: it takes the Hario V60 and makes it consistent for normal people by replacing fiddly stirring with gentle swirling and by structuring the pours around clean percentages. Built for one cup at 15 g to 250 g , it's the recipe most home brewers cut their teeth on. 🌀 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario V60 (size 02) Dose 15 g Water 250 g total Ratio 1:16.7 (60 g/L) Temp 95 99°C (off the boil for light roasts) Grind medium fine — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 45 g for 45 s Brew time 3:30 Roast light to medium Source James Hoffmann, 2019 Resulting cup Clean, sweet, balanced; high clarity, very repeatable

1. 0:00 — Add 45 g (cumulative 45 g; Hoffmann's bloom rule is 2 3× dose); swirl the slurry until evenly mixed; The Bloom 45 s. 2. 0:45 — Pour steadily to 150 g (cumulative 150 g, 60% of total) over 30 s. 3. 1:15 — Pour to 250 g (cumulative 250 g, 100%) over 30 s. 4. 1:45 — Give one or two gentle stirs, then a final swirl to flatten the bed. 5. 3:30 — Drawdown completes with a flat, even coffee bed.

The key innovation is swirling instead of stirring . A swirl mixes the bloom evenly and later flattens the bed for uniform The Drawdown, but it's gentler and more repeatable than a spoon stir, which varies wildly between people — so two brewers get closer to the same cup. Pouring to clean 60% then 100% marks keeps the math trivial, and finishing with a swirl prevents the high and dry grounds on the wall that cause uneven Extraction Yield and Strength. A flat bed at the end is the visual signal you did it right. Hoffmann recommends water just off the boil for most coffees and soft, low mineral water for clarity (see Water for Coffee). The method scales — 30 g to 500 g for two cups with the same rhythm — though larger brews flow slower. It's the natural upgrade from the Hario V60 Official Recipe and a great launchpad before the Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method.

After testing the AeroPress's many myths on camera, James Hoffmann landed on a recipe that's almost provocatively simple — and it works. It throws out the inverted gymnastics and the cool water dogma in favor of an upright brew, a long gentle steep, boiling water, and a slow press , producing a clean, sweet cup with none of the bitterness people fear from the device. 💧 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer AeroPress (standard, upright) Dose 11 g Water 200 g Ratio 1:18 Temp 100°C (boiling) Grind medium fine — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom none Brew time 2:30 steep, then slow press Roast light to medium Source James Hoffmann, 2020 Resulting cup Sweet, clean, low bitterness; light to medium body, well rounded

1. Setup — Rinse a paper filter in the cap, screw on, set the AeroPress upright on a sturdy mug; add 11 g coffee. 2. 0:00 — Pour to 200 g of boiling water; insert the plunger 1 cm to create a seal (prevents dripping). Do not stir yet. 3. 0:00 2:00 — Steep undisturbed. 4. 2:00 — Swirl the whole brewer (don't stir) to settle the grounds, 30 s. 5. 2:30 — Press gently and slowly; stop before the hiss .

Hoffmann's testing found that two beloved variables — agitation and water temperature — mattered far less than people assume, so he simplified ruthlessly. The long, calm steep does the extraction with minimal fuss; the swirl instead of stir settles the bed without over agitating; and stopping before the hiss avoids pushing air through a tightening puck, the moment that drips bitter, Under Extraction and Over Extraction liquid into the cup. Boiling water is fine here precisely because the contact time and immersion are so gentle. It's an unfussy daily recipe that flatters Light Roast and Specialty Coffee and travels well — no kettle finesse, no inversion flip, no pour pattern. Compare it with the more concentrate and dilute AeroPress Official Recipe to see how far the community took Adler's invention.

Scott Rao is one of coffee's most influential technical writers, and his V60 method is built around a single famous move: the spin . After the final pour, you give the dripper a firm rotational swirl to flatten the coffee bed and wash every last ground off the filter wall , driving the most even The Drawdown possible. It looks like a flourish; it's actually extraction engineering. ⚙️ [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario V60 Dose 22 g Water 352 g total Ratio 1:16 Temp 94 96°C Grind medium fine — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 66 g for 30 45 s, with a spin Brew time 3:30 Roast light to medium Source Scott Rao, 2010s Resulting cup Even, sweet, high extraction; clean with good body

1. 0:00 — Add 66 g (cumulative 66 g, 3× dose); spin the dripper to wet all grounds evenly; The Bloom 30 45 s. 2. 0:30 0:45 — Pour in a single steady stream to 352 g (cumulative 352 g), keeping the slurry deep and the bed agitated. 3. 1:30 — As the water level drops, give the dripper a firm spin to flatten the bed and rinse the walls. 4. 3:30 — Drawdown completes over a flat, even bed.

Rao's two priorities are even extraction and avoiding bypass . The spin after the bloom guarantees full saturation; the deep, continuous pour keeps the whole bed agitated so fines aren't left under extracted; and the final spin levels the bed and pulls down the high grounds that would otherwise sit dry on the wall, raising overall Extraction Yield and Strength and uniformity. Done well, the spin gives you the consistency competitive brewers chase, with less fuss than counting many small pours. A word of nuance: the spin does add agitation , so on very fine grinds or fragile coffees it can tip toward over extraction or stalling — see Agitation and Turbulence. Rao's broader work also champions fresh, well developed roasts and careful grinding (he favors flat burrs); the temperature and exact weights are a representative baseline to dial to your beans.

Lance Hedrick — barista, former US AeroPress champion, and prolific educator — built a pour over approach for accessibility and repeatability on imperfect home gear . His signature moves are a coarser grind (to dodge the clogs and astringency that fines cause on cheap grinders) paired with aggressive, high pour turbulence to make up the lost extraction, plus a habit of adjusting grind and temperature together by roast . It's a method that meets ordinary kitchens where they are. 🛠️ [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario V60 Dose 18 g Water 288 g total Ratio 1:16 Temp 90 99°C (hotter for light, cooler for dark) Grind medium coarse — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 54 g for 45 s, vigorous Brew time 2:30 3:30 Roast any — adjust grind and temp to suit Source Lance Hedrick, early 2020s Resulting cup Sweet, clear, even; forgiving of fines and modest grinders

1. 0:00 — Add 54 g (cumulative 54 g, 3× dose); stir or swirl vigorously to fully wet and agitate; The Bloom 45 s. 2. 0:45 — Pour from height in the centre to 170 g (cumulative 170 g), creating turbulent droplet impact. 3. 1:30 — Pour again from height to 288 g (cumulative 288 g). 4. 1:45 — A gentle swirl to settle and flatten the bed. 5. 2:30 3:30 — Drawdown completes.

Hedrick's insight is that most home grinders make too many fines , which clog the Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth, stall flow, and add astringency. Going coarser sidesteps that, and the lost surface area is recovered by pouring from height — water breaking into turbulent droplets at the "stream breakup point" agitates the bed hard, boosting Extraction Yield and Strength without a finer grind (see Agitation and Turbulence).
