He also adjusts by Roast Levels for Pour Over: light roasts finer and hotter (dense beans need more energy), dark roasts coarser and cooler (porous beans extract fast). Above all he preaches that grinder quality is the highest leverage upgrade a home brewer can make. This is a robust, low drama daily recipe that forgives modest equipment — ideal if your cups come out muddy or astringent. Pair it with Dialing In Grind Size to find your grinder's sweet spot.

The Hario Switch is a V60 cone with a little spring loaded shut off valve in the base. Closed, it steeps coffee in full immersion like a The Clever Dripper; flicked open, it drains by percolation like an ordinary Hario V60. That one switch lets you blend the two worlds in a single brew — immersion sweetness and evenness, then a percolation rinse for clarity — and Tetsu Kasuya among others has published acclaimed switch recipes. 🎚️ [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario Switch (uses Hario V60 filters) Dose 20 g Water 300 g total Ratio 1:15 Temp 90 95°C (stage if you like) Grind medium to medium fine — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 50 g for 45 s (valve open or closed) Brew time 3:30 Roast light to medium Source Hario / Tetsu Kasuya, 2020s Resulting cup Sweet and full like immersion, but clean like a V60

A representative "immersion then drain" hybrid (Kasuya style); valve states matter: 1. Setup — Rinse a V60 paper; close the valve. 2. 0:00 — Pour 50 g (cumulative 50 g); swirl; The Bloom 45 s with the valve closed. 3. 0:45 — Pour to 300 g (cumulative 300 g); steep immersion with the valve still closed. 4. 1:45 — Open the valve to begin percolation drawdown. 5. 3:30 — Drains clean; an optional swirl just before opening flattens the bed. [!tip] Switch the order for a different cup Open early for a more percolation led, clarity forward brew; keep it closed longer for more immersion body and sweetness. Some recipes alternate — drain partway, close again, top up — to layer the two styles.

The valve hands you time as a direct lever . While closed, every ground sits in the same water for the same duration — the hallmark even, forgiving extraction of French Press. When you open it, the brew finishes as The Drawdown, the paper filter polishing out fines for a clean finish. You get immersion's roundness and paper's clarity, and you can bias toward either by when you flip the switch . It's the most flexible single dripper in the book, which is why it became a competition and café favorite. This sits alongside the Clever Dripper Official Recipe and OXO Rapid Brewer Recipe in the immersion family, but with the added percolation phase. Treat the timings as a baseline; the switch invites experimentation.

Onyx Coffee Lab is one of the most decorated roasters in the United States, and its published V60 brew guide is a clean, professional template aimed at getting the best from its Light Roast and Specialty Coffee. The house style favors a bloom heavy start and several measured pours for high clarity and sweetness — a roaster's recipe, designed to flatter the beans they sell. 📋 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario V60 Dose 22 g Water 350 g total Ratio 1:16 Temp 200 205°F ( 93 96°C) Grind medium fine — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 50 g for 45 s Brew time 2:45 3:15 Roast light to medium Source Onyx Coffee Lab, published brew guide Resulting cup Bright, sweet, exceptionally clean; pronounced origin character

A representative pulse pour version of the Onyx guide (figures approximate; Onyx publishes several variants): 1. 0:00 — Add 50 g (cumulative 50 g, 2 3× dose); The Bloom 45 s. 2. 0:45 — Pour to 150 g (cumulative 150 g) in slow circles. 3. 1:15 — Pour to 250 g (cumulative 250 g). 4. 1:45 — Pour to 350 g (cumulative 350 g), keeping the bed level. 5. 2:45 3:15 — Drawdown completes.

A roaster's brew guide exists to show off its own coffee , so the choices skew toward Extraction Yield and Strength: a generous bloom to degas fresh beans, a moderate medium fine grind, hot but not boiling water, and several even pulses that build extraction without harshness. Onyx also publishes detailed grind size and dialing in guidance for its lots, reflecting a roaster's conviction that the recipe should adapt to each coffee, not the other way around. The pulses keep the The Drawdown even and the cup clean. Because roaster guides evolve and Onyx offers more than one version (including faster, higher agitation methods), treat the exact pour weights and temperature as approximate and lean on the Reading a Coffee Bag Label for a specific lot. It pairs naturally with bright, Washed Process single origins.

This is the recipe to learn first — a clean, forgiving, beginner friendly default that strips pour over down to its essentials: one bloom, two simple pours, round numbers, and a 1:16 The Brew Ratio. There's nothing clever to get wrong, which is exactly the point. Brew this a dozen times until the motions feel natural, then graduate to the more developed methods. 🌱 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario V60 (size 01 or 02) Dose 15 g Water 240 g total Ratio 1:16 Temp 94°C (just off the boil) Grind medium fine, like table salt — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 45 g for 45 s Brew time 3:00 Roast medium; also flatters light Source Composite beginner default Resulting cup Balanced, sweet, clean; an honest, repeatable everyday cup

1. Setup — Rinse the paper filter (Pre Wetting the Filter), discard the rinse water, add 15 g coffee, level the bed. 2. 0:00 — Add 45 g (cumulative 45 g, 3× dose); The Bloom 45 s, with a small swirl. 3. 0:45 — Pour in slow circles to 140 g (cumulative 140 g). 4. 1:30 — Pour to 240 g (cumulative 240 g), keeping the bed level and avoiding the wall. 5. 3:00 — Drawdown completes; aim for a flat, even bed.

Everything here is chosen to be hard to mess up . A 1:16 ratio sits in the sweet spot between weak and overpowering; a medium fine grind flows reliably; water just off the boil suits most Roast Levels for Pour Over; and two even pours keep the bed submerged without demanding pro level control. If the cup is sour , grind finer or brew hotter; if it's bitter , grind coarser or cooler — the simple diagnostic loop in Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes and the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide. This is your reference point. Once it's automatic, the James Hoffmann V60 Technique adds the swirl, the Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method adds tunable splits, and the Kalita Wave Official Recipe shows the flat bottom contrast. The numbers are a sensible composite of common practice — adjust freely as your Building Your Palate develops.

Japanese style iced coffee — also called flash brewing — is pour over's best summer trick: you brew hot, directly onto ice , so the coffee is chilled the instant it's extracted. This is not cold brew. The hot water still extracts the bright acidity and floral aromatics that cold brew mutes, and the ice flash cools and dilutes the concentrate to drinking strength in one move, locking in a vivid, clean iced cup. ❄️ [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer Hario V60 over a server of ice Dose 20 g Water 300 g total (ice + brew water) Ratio 1:15 (incl. melted ice) Temp 93 96°C (hot brew) Grind medium fine, a touch finer than hot — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 50 g for 45 s Brew time 3:00 Roast light to medium Source Japanese iced method / composite Resulting cup Bright, aromatic, crisp; clean acidity, lighter body, refreshing

The trick is dividing your total water into ice in the server and hot brew water through the cone . A common split for 300 g total is 120 g ice in the server and 180 g hot water poured through. The ice melts into the brew, so it counts toward your ratio — skip it and the coffee comes out thin and over diluted.

1. Setup — Put 120 g ice in the Scales, Timers, and Servers under the dripper; rinse the filter; add 20 g coffee. 2. 0:00 — Pour 50 g hot water (cumulative 50 g brew water); The Bloom 45 s. 3. 0:45 — Pour to 120 g (cumulative 120 g brew water) in circles. 4. 1:30 — Pour to 180 g (cumulative 180 g brew water). 5. 2:30 3:00 — Drawdown completes onto the ice; swirl the server to melt remaining ice, then serve over fresh ice.

Brewing hot captures the volatile aromatics and clean acidity that only dissolve well at high temperature — the very compounds slow cold brew leaves behind — while the ice arrests the brew before it warms and dulls , preserving them. Because roughly 40% of your water is ice , you brew a stronger than usual concentrate (effectively a tighter ratio through the cone), then the melt brings it to balance. Grind slightly finer than your hot recipe to compensate for the smaller volume of liquid actually passing through the bed. This flatters bright, fruity, Washed Process and Light Roast and Specialty Coffee coffees spectacularly. For a chilled batch instead, scale up via the Batch Pour Over Recipe with the same ice in the server principle.

Brewing for a table is its own skill. Scaling a single cup recipe straight up doesn't quite work: a deep bed of coffee resists The Drawdown, holds more heat, and over extracts the bottom while the top runs fast. This recipe adapts the fundamentals for larger flat bottom brewers and the Chemex — roughly 60 g to 960 g (about four cups) — with the tweaks that keep a big brew even. 👥 [!example] ☕ Recipe Card Field Value Brewer large flat bottom dripper or Chemex (6 8 cup) Dose 60 g Water 960 g total Ratio 1:16 Temp 94 96°C Grind medium, slightly coarser than single cup — see Grind Size for Pour Over Bloom 120 g for 45 s Brew time 5:00 6:00 Roast medium; also light Source Composite batch method Resulting cup Even, sweet, balanced; consistent from first cup to last

1. Setup — Rinse the filter generously (Pre Wetting the Filter), add 60 g coffee, level the deep bed. 2. 0:00 — Add 120 g (cumulative 120 g, 2× dose); The Bloom 45 s, stirring or swirling to wet the deep bed fully. 3. 0:45 — Pour to 400 g (cumulative 400 g) in wide circles. 4. 1:45 — Pour to 680 g (cumulative 680 g). 5. 2:45 — Pour to 960 g (cumulative 960 g), keeping the bed level. 6. 5:00 6:00 — Drawdown completes; stir or swirl the server before pouring so the cup is uniform.

sections.h: Why It Works — and What Changes at Scale
sections.t: Three things shift when you go big. First, the deeper bed extracts unevenly , so you grind a touch coarser and stir the bloom to saturate it fully — see Single Cup vs Batch Pour Over. Second, the larger thermal mass holds heat , pushing extraction up, which the coarser grind offsets. Third, the drawdown is slow , so pours are bigger and fewer, and the Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth choice matters — the thick Chemex paper is naturally suited to volume. A final swirl of the carafe before serving evens out strength stratification between the first and last cup poured. For café scale volumes most shops switch to automatic Single Cup vs Batch Pour Over, but a Chemex or large flat bottom dripper handles a dinner party beautifully. The numbers scale linearly from there; just expect longer total times as the bed deepens.

This is the knowledge base's one off label brew type : using a coffee brewer to make soup . It sounds like a joke, but it follows directly from what these devices actually are — and the internet is full of people doing it. An AeroPress is a sealed immersion chamber with a plunger that forces liquid through a filter; an OXO Rapid Brewer is a flat bottom immersion vessel with a shut off valve that drains a clean liquid off a bed of solids. Swap the coffee grounds for savory solids — miso, bouillon, dried mushrooms, kombu, aromatics — and the very same machine that "brews" coffee will brew a clean, single serve bowl of soup. 🥄 Treat this page like the Official Brewer Recipes hub: it collects the soup recipes, each written to the same machine readable How to Read a Coffee Recipe as every coffee recipe, with in the frontmatter so a future recipe app can filter the novelty out — or in.

sections.h: Why a Coffee Brewer Makes Good Soup
sections.t: Two mechanics carry over from coffee one to one. Immersion steeping — the move behind the The Clever Dripper and the OXO — pulls flavor from solids sitting in hot water, which is exactly how you build a broth from dried mushrooms, kombu dashi, or a bouillon cube. Filtered separation — paper, or the AeroPress plunge — then strains the spent solids out, giving a clean, sediment free bowl in a way a pot on the stove never quite does. The AeroPress press goes one step further: forced through a filter, cooked tomatoes or roasted vegetables come out the bottom as a smooth, skin and seed free bisque, the plunger acting like a tiny food mill. [!warning] 🧼 Protect your coffee gear first Oils and fats are the enemy of paper filters — and of your next coffee. They blind the filter bed and leave rancid, savory ghosts behind. Stick to low fat ingredients (miso, dashi, bouillon, dried veg), rinse everything immediately, and consider keeping a dedicated set of filters and seals for soup so your morning Hario V60 never tastes of onion.

Recipe Brewer Style "Ratio" Temp AeroPress Soup Recipe AeroPress Pressed single serve soup / smooth bisque 1:8 base to water 80 90°C OXO Rapid Brewer Soup Recipe OXO Rapid Brewer Steeped, filter clean broth 1:10 base to water 90 96°C The AeroPress is the pressure and strain tool — best when you want to push solids through into a smooth, emulsified bowl. The OXO is the steep and drain tool — best when you want a crystal clear broth poured off a bed of aromatics. Both simply repurpose the immersion mechanics mapped in the Brew Method Family Tree. [!tip] Read the card the same way The fields in these soup cards mean what they always mean — is your dry/solid soup base, is the liquid, is the steep — so How to Read a Coffee Recipe and Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes still apply. Want it stronger? Same lever as coffee: more base, longer steep.

The AeroPress is a sealed immersion chamber with a plunger that forces liquid through a thin filter — which makes it, off label, a superb single serve soup press . Two modes work: a steeped soup, where you hydrate miso or bouillon plus dried add ins and press a clean bowl; and a smooth bisque , where you press cooked tomatoes or roasted vegetables through the filter so skins and seeds stay behind. Either way the plunge does the straining a stovetop can't. This is the headline method of the Brewing Soup with Coffee Gear brew type. 💨 [!example] 🥣 Recipe Card Field Value Brewer AeroPress (standard, upright) Soup base (dose) 30 g — e.g. 1 tbsp miso + ½ bouillon cube, or a dried soup mix Water 250 g hot Ratio 1:8 base to water, adjusted to taste Temp 80 90°C (keep miso below a boil) Filter metal disc, or two paper filters — see Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth Steep 2 4 min Press slow and steady, 30 45 s Add ins dried wakame, scallion, cubed tofu, chili crisp Source AeroPress home cook community (folk method) Resulting bowl Smooth, clean, sediment free single serve soup; body without grit

1. Setup — Seat a metal disc (or two paper filters) in the cap, screw it on, and place the AeroPress upright on a sturdy mug or bowl; add 30 g soup base and any quick hydrating dried add ins (wakame, dried veg). 2. 0:00 — Pour 80 90°C water to 250 g ; stir 10 15 s to dissolve the base. 3. 0:15 3:00 — Steep 2 4 minutes to hydrate the add ins and build flavor. 4. 3:00 — Insert the plunger and press slowly for 30 45 s; stop at the hiss. 5. Finish — Top with fresh garnish (scallion, chili crisp, sesame); taste and loosen with a splash more hot water if needed. [!tip] 🍅 Smooth bisque variation Load the chamber with 120 g cooked or canned tomatoes (or roasted vegetables) plus 120 g hot stock, steep 1 2 minutes, then press. The plunge strains skins and seeds, leaving a silky bisque. Use the metal disc — pulp will blind paper fast.

Immersion plus pressure does the two soup jobs at once. The hot steep extracts savory flavor by the same physics as a coffee steep — see The Science of Extraction — and the plunge then strains the solids like a food mill, so you get body without sediment. The cooler water mirrors why the AeroPress Official Recipe runs cool: it protects miso's delicate aroma, which a rolling boil would flatten. And strength is a dilution game exactly as it is with coffee — press a concentrated base, then loosen to taste, the core move of Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes. Keep it low fat. Oily broths (rich ramen tare, coconut, cream) clog filters and taint the chamber for your next cup, so save those for a pot and reserve the AeroPress for clean, light soups.

The OXO Rapid Brewer is an immersion vessel with a shut off valve and a flat paper filter — a "steep, then drain clean" machine. Off label, that makes it an excellent broth steeper : load aromatics, steep with the valve closed, then open it and a clear, sediment free broth drains into your bowl, like an oversized tea bag. It shines for dashi, dried mushroom broth, herbal or vegetable broth, and clarified instant miso — the steep and drain half of the Brewing Soup with Coffee Gear brew type. ⏱️ [!example] 🍲 Recipe Card Field Value Brewer OXO Rapid Brewer Aromatics (dose) 40 g — dried shiitake, kombu, bouillon, dried veg, ginger, garlic Water 400 g, just off the boil Ratio 1:10 aromatics to water Temp 90 96°C Filter flat paper — dedicate one to soup Steep 5 8 min, valve closed Drain open the valve / set on the bowl, 60 s Source OXO Rapid Brewer off label use (folk method) Resulting bowl Clear, clean, sediment free savory broth

1. Setup — Seat and rinse a flat paper filter with the valve closed ; add 40 g aromatics and level the bed. 2. 0:00 — Pour 90 96°C water to 400 g ; stir once to wet everything. 3. 0:00 6:00 — Steep undisturbed 5 8 minutes (longer for kombu and dried mushroom depth). 4. 6:00 — Open the valve / set on your bowl to drain a clear broth ( 60 s). 5. Finish — Season the drained broth (soy, salt, a spoon of miso stirred in off heat) and add cooked noodles or tofu in the bowl. [!tip] 🥄 Add miso after, not during Stir miso into the drained broth rather than steeping it — boiling dulls its aroma, and its paste would clog the paper. Same goes for any thickener: keep the bed clean, finish in the bowl.

This is the OXO Rapid Brewer Recipe with savory solids: a full immersion steep for even extraction, then a flat bed drain through paper for a clean cup. Steep time is the main flavor lever, exactly as in coffee — see Brew Time and Total Contact Time — and the bed drains evenly thanks to its Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers. Because the paper traps fines and sediment, the broth pours clearer than a stovetop simmer, the same clarity that makes the OXO's coffee so clean. As always with off label brewing, keep ingredients low fat so oils don't blind the filter, and rinse the brewer well so your next coffee doesn't taste of mushroom.

Every cup of pour over is a chemistry experiment you can drink. Hot water meets ground coffee, dissolves a fraction of its mass, and carries that fraction into your cup. This domain explains the science underneath the ritual — what actually dissolves, how much of it, how we measure the result, and why two brewers with the same coffee can land in wildly different places. Understanding it turns guesswork into control, and turns "this tastes off" into "this is under extracted, so I'll grind finer." 🎯

Brewing is extraction : water pulls soluble compounds out of roasted coffee, and the art is taking the right amount of the right compounds. Take too little and the cup is sour and thin; take too much and it turns bitter and hollow. Two independent numbers describe where you landed — how much coffee dissolved (Extraction Yield and Strength) and how concentrated the result is (Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)). Almost everything else in this knowledge base — Grinding, Water for Coffee, Water Temperature for Brewing, Brewing Technique — is a lever that moves those two numbers. [!note] Science serves taste, not the other way around Numbers are a map, not the territory. A refractometer tells you where you are; your palate decides whether you like it. Use the science to repeat good cups, not to chase a target you can't taste.

Topic Note What extraction is The Science of Extraction What dissolves, in what order Solubility and What Dissolves The two independent axes Extraction Yield and Strength Measuring concentration Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) The classic chart The Coffee Brewing Control Chart Measuring extraction Refractometers and Measuring Extraction Reading the cup Under Extraction and Over Extraction When beds extract unevenly Channeling and Uneven Extraction Heat and solubility The Role of Temperature in Extraction Surface area Grind Size and Surface Area Why fresh coffee bubbles CO2, Degassing, and the Bloom Science

The science here is the why behind every practical choice elsewhere. When you adjust Grind Size for Pour Over you are changing surface area and extraction rate; when you follow The Bloom you are managing CO2; when a cup disappoints, the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide reasons in this domain's language. Sensory work in Tasting and Sensory is the human counterpart — extraction theory predicts the flavor, tasting confirms it.

Extraction is the process of dissolving soluble compounds out of roasted, ground coffee and into water. A coffee bean is mostly insoluble plant matter — cellulose, fiber, structural carbohydrate — but roughly 28–30% of its mass can, in principle, dissolve. Brewing is the controlled act of taking some of that soluble fraction. How much you take, and which compounds, decides whether the cup is sour, sweet, balanced, or bitter. Everything else in pour over is in service of steering this one process.

sections.h: The Physics: Three Things Happening at Once
sections.t: When water contacts a coffee particle, three mechanisms drive flavor out: 1. Dissolution — soluble solids (acids, sugars, Aroma and Flavor Compounds) leave the particle surface and enter the water. 2. Diffusion — compounds deeper inside the particle migrate outward to the surface to be dissolved, the slow rate limiting step. 3. Erosion — agitation and flow physically wash material off the surface. Extraction is driven by a concentration gradient : water grabs compounds fastest when it is fresh and "empty," and slows as it loads up. This is exactly why percolation brewing like What Is Pour Over Coffee — where fresh water continually passes through the bed — extracts efficiently, and why immersion methods like the French Press, sitting in increasingly saturated water, extract more gently.

Lever Effect on extraction Note Grind size Finer = more surface area = faster Grind Size and Surface Area Temperature Hotter water dissolves faster The Role of Temperature in Extraction Time Longer contact = more extracted Brew Time and Total Contact Time Agitation Stirring/turbulence speeds erosion Agitation and Turbulence Water chemistry Minerals help bind flavor Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity [!tip] Extraction is a rate , not a switch You don't "fully extract" coffee — you stop somewhere along a curve. Pull too little and you get Under Extraction and Over Extraction; push too far and you get over extraction. The goal is to land in the tasty middle.

[!warning] "Bitter coffee was over brewed; sour coffee was under brewed" Half true at best. Sourness and harsh astringency can both come from the same brew if extraction is uneven — some particles over extracted while others under extracted via Channeling and Uneven Extraction. Average extraction can look fine while the cup tastes wrong. Read average and local extraction as different things. The numbers that describe the outcome — how much dissolved and how strong the cup is — are covered in Extraction Yield and Strength, and the order in which compounds come out is in Solubility and What Dissolves.

Not everything in coffee dissolves at the same speed. Brewing extracts compounds in a rough sequence , and that sequence is the secret behind why timing, grind, and temperature matter so much. The early arrivals taste bright and acidic; the middle of the run brings sweetness and balance; the late arrivals turn bitter and drying. A good brew rides this curve and gets off at the right stop. ⏱️

Stage What comes out How it tastes First Fruit acids, salts, some caffeine Sour, sharp, bright Middle Sugars, Aroma and Flavor Compounds\, browning products Sweet, balanced, complex Last Bitter Acidity in Coffee\, heavier melanoidins, dry compounds Bitter, astringent, hollow This is approximate , not a strict timeline — extraction is continuous and overlapping. But the trend is real: acids and small, highly soluble molecules leave first, while larger and less soluble bitter compounds need more energy and time. It is why a brew cut short tastes Under Extraction and Over Extraction and one pushed too long tastes bitter.

Acids — chlorogenic, citric, malic, and others. Highly soluble, extract early, drive Acidity in Coffee. Chlorogenic acids also break down on roasting into bitter compounds. Sugars and caramelized carbohydrates — sources of Sweetness and Body; less soluble than acids, so they need adequate extraction to appear. Melanoidins — large brown polymers formed by the Coffee Roasting Explained during roasting. They contribute Sweetness and Body, color, and some bitterness, and extract late and slowly. Lipids (oils) — barely water soluble; mostly trapped by Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth, which is why paper filtered pour over tastes "cleaner" than a French Press. Caffeine — fairly soluble and largely extracted in most brews; a minor flavor contributor (mildly bitter), not the main source of bitterness. [!warning] Myth: "Dark roast and long brews have more caffeine" Caffeine is remarkably stable through roasting and extracts early. Bitterness and caffeine are not the same thing — most bitterness comes from late extracting phenolics and roast compounds, not caffeine.

Because the tasty compounds (sweetness, balanced acidity) sit in the middle of the run, the brewer's job is to extract far enough to reach them but not so far as to drag in the bitter tail. That balance is set by Grind Size and Surface Area, The Role of Temperature in Extraction, and Brew Time and Total Contact Time — and scored by Extraction Yield and Strength.

Two numbers describe almost everything about a brew, and the key insight is that they are independent . Extraction yield (EY%) is how much of the coffee dissolved. Strength (TDS) is how concentrated the resulting liquid is. You can have a strong cup that is under extracted, or a weak cup that is over extracted — they are separate axes, and confusing them is the most common conceptual mistake in brewing. 🧭

Extraction yield is the percentage of the dry coffee's mass that ended up dissolved in the cup. Brew 20 g of coffee, dissolve 4 g of it, and you have a 20% extraction yield . Of coffee's 28–30% soluble maximum, most filter brews aim for roughly 18–22% : Below 18% → Under Extraction and Over Extraction: sour, salty, thin. 18–22% → the commonly cited "sweet spot" for balance. Above 22% → over extracted: bitter, drying, hollow. [!note] These bands are guidelines, not gospel The "18–22%" window came from mid century research on average American palates and roasts. Modern light roasts and great coffees often taste best above 22%. Let your palate, not the number, draw the lines.

Strength is measured as Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) — the percentage of the brewed liquid that is dissolved coffee, typically 1.15–1.45% for pour over. Higher TDS tastes more intense and syrupy; lower TDS tastes more delicate and tea like. Strength is set mainly by the The Brew Ratio (water per gram of coffee), while extraction yield is set mainly by grind, time, and temperature.

These three quantities are linked by a simple formula: [!example] The brewing equation Extraction Yield = (Brew Mass × TDS%) ÷ Dose where Brew Mass is the grams of liquid coffee in the cup and Dose is the grams of dry coffee. Measure TDS with a Refractometers and Measuring Extraction, weigh your output, and EY falls out. You change... EY moves TDS moves Finer grind ↑ ↑ More water (looser ratio) slight ↑ ↓ Hotter water ↑ ↑ Longer brew time ↑ ↑ The two axes plotted together create the famous map of brewing space — the The Coffee Brewing Control Chart.

Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) is the share of a brewed coffee's mass that is dissolved coffee rather than water. If a cup is 1.35% TDS , then 1.35% of the liquid's weight is dissolved solids and the remaining 98.65% is water. TDS is the scientific name for what tasters call strength or concentration — and it is one of the two numbers, alongside Extraction Yield and Strength, that define any brew. 🔬 [!note] Two TDS, don't confuse them Brew TDS (this note) measures dissolved coffee in your cup. TDS and Mineral Content measures minerals in your brewing water before any coffee touches it. Same acronym, completely different quantity.

Brew style Typical TDS Character Delicate pour over 1.15–1.30% Light, tea like, articulate Standard pour over 1.30–1.45% Balanced, the common target Strong / immersion 1.45–1.60% Heavy, syrupy, intense Espresso (for contrast) 8–12% Concentrated shot Pour over generally lives around 1.2–1.45% . The The Specialty Coffee Movement centered a "good" cup near 1.15–1.35% , but, as with extraction bands, modern preference often runs higher.

Strength is dominated by the The Brew Ratio — how much water you use per gram of coffee. A tight ratio (say 1:15) gives a stronger, higher TDS cup; a loose one (1:17) a weaker, lower TDS cup. Because Bypass and Channeling (water that skirts the bed without extracting) dilutes the brew, dripper geometry and pour style nudge TDS too. Extraction yield also raises TDS — dissolving more coffee puts more solids in the same water — which is why the two numbers, though independent, move together when you change grind or temperature.

A digital Refractometers and Measuring Extraction shines light through a drop of filtered, cooled brew and reads how much the dissolved solids bend it, reporting TDS as a percentage (or °Brix, converted). It is the only practical way to put a real number on strength; everything else is calibrated tongue. [!warning] Perceived strength ≠ measured TDS Your palate's sense of "strong" is also shaped by bitterness, body, and acidity. A high EY brew can taste intense at a modest TDS, while a clean high TDS brew can taste surprisingly easy. TDS is the measured strength, not the perceived one.

Knowing TDS lets you separate two questions that feel like one: "is this too strong?" (adjust The Brew Ratio) versus "is this extracted right?" (adjust Grind Size and Surface Area or The Role of Temperature in Extraction). That separation is the whole point of the The Coffee Brewing Control Chart.

The Coffee Brewing Control Chart is the classic map of brewing space: a graph with Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) on the vertical axis and Extraction Yield and Strength on the horizontal axis. Any brew you make plots as a single dot somewhere on it. A central rectangle marks the historically "ideal" zone, and the regions around it are labelled with how brews drift wrong — too sour, too bitter, too strong, too weak. It is the single most reproduced diagram in coffee science. 📈

Region EY TDS Verdict Bottom left Low Low Under developed & weak Bottom right High Low Bitter but thin Top left Low High Sour & strong Top right High High Bitter & strong Center box 18–22% 1.15–1.45% The classic "ideal" The two axes move semi independently: slide left/right by changing Grind Size and Surface Area, The Role of Temperature in Extraction, or Brew Time and Total Contact Time (these change extraction); slide up/down by changing the The Brew Ratio (this changes strength). Diagonal lines on the chart represent constant brew ratios — a handy way to see that grinding finer pushes you up and to the right at once.

The chart descends from work by Dr. Ernest Earl Lockhart at MIT in the 1950s , funded by the Coffee Brewing Institute. Lockhart surveyed American drinkers, found a preferred extraction band around 18–22% and a strength near 1.15–1.35% , and the result became the SCA "Golden Cup" standard. The familiar grid form is often called the Brewing Control Chart or, in newer interactive versions, the "coffee compass."

[!warning] The "ideal box" is dated and culturally narrow Lockhart's data reflected 1950s American palates and the dark, robusta heavy roasts of the era. Modern light, Light Roast and Specialty Coffee roasts frequently taste sweetest at 22–24% extraction — well outside the original box. Treat the chart as a coordinate system , not a verdict. A second limitation: the chart plots a single average EY. It cannot see Channeling and Uneven Extraction, so a brew that lands dead center can still taste flawed if some grounds over extracted while others under extracted. Average is not the same as good. [!tip] Use it as a logbook, not a law Plot your favourite cups over time. The cluster you love is your personal target — which may sit well outside the printed rectangle.

A refractometer is the instrument that turns brewing from intuition into measurement. It reads the Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) of your cup, and from TDS plus a little arithmetic you can calculate the Extraction Yield and Strength — the two coordinates that place your brew on the The Coffee Brewing Control Chart. For anyone chasing repeatability, it is the difference between "I think that was better" and "that was 21.2%, half a point higher than yesterday." 📐

Dissolved solids change how much light bends (refracts) as it passes through a liquid — more solids, more bending. A refractometer shines an LED through a small drop of brew and measures the refractive index , which it reports as a percentage of TDS (or as °Brix , a sugar scale reading it converts internally). Coffee refractometers like the well known VST units pair the optics with software calibrated specifically to brewed coffee, since coffee solids refract slightly differently from pure sugar. [!warning] Brix is not TDS A cheap winemaker's Brix refractometer reads sugar, not coffee solids. The conversion factor differs, so generic Brix readings overstate coffee TDS unless corrected. Coffee specific tools (or apps) apply the right factor.

Garbage in, garbage out — small errors swing the result: 1. Filter the sample. Undissolved fines scatter light and inflate the reading; draw your drop through a small syringe filter. 2. Cool it down. Temperature shifts refractive index; let the sample reach near room temperature or use a temperature compensating unit. 3. Wipe and zero. Calibrate to distilled water, and clean the lens between reads. 4. Sample representatively. Stir the brew first; the first drops from the The Drawdown differ from the average cup.

[!example] The formula EY% = (Beverage Mass × TDS%) ÷ Dry Coffee Dose Example: 250 g of brewed coffee at 1.40% TDS from a 15 g dose → (250 × 0.014) ÷ 15 = 3.5 g dissolved ÷ 15 g = 23.3% extraction yield. You need How to get it Beverage mass Weigh the brewed liquid in the cup/server TDS% Read from the refractometer Dose Weigh dry coffee before grinding on your Scales, Timers, and Servers\ Note beverage mass , not water poured: some water is retained by the wet grounds and never reaches the cup, so using the poured weight inflates EY.

[!tip] A tool for repeatability, not a flavor judge A refractometer can't tell you a cup is delicious — only where it is on the map. Its value is letting you reproduce a great brew and reason about adjustments precisely. Many superb brewers never own one; many dialed in Dialing In Grind Size go faster with one.

If a brew tastes off, the first question is almost always: did I take too little or too much from the coffee? Under extraction stops too early on the Solubility and What Dissolves, leaving the cup dominated by the first, sour leaning compounds. Over extraction runs too far and drags in the late, bitter tail. Learning their taste signatures is the fastest diagnostic skill in pour over — and each has a clean set of fixes. 👅

Under extracted Over extracted Extraction yield Too low (≲18%) Too high (≳22–24%) Acidity Sharp, sour, "lemony" aggressive Dull, flat Sweetness Lacking, salty Lost, replaced by bitter Body Thin, watery, empty Can feel heavy but harsh Finish Quick, hollow, drying sour Lingering bitter, Identifying Defects and Off Flavors\ Classic word Sour Bitter / hollow [!tip] Sour vs. bitter is the quickest fork A bright, sour , salty cup that makes you wince is usually under extracted. A bitter , dry, ashy cup that lingers unpleasantly is usually over extracted. This single distinction guides most corrections.

To raise extraction (fix under extraction), do any of: Grind finer — more Grind Size and Surface Area; the strongest single lever. Brew hotter — see The Role of Temperature in Extraction. Extend contact time — slower or more Pulse vs Continuous Pouring, or a finer grind that slows The Drawdown. Add agitation — gentle Agitation and Turbulence. To lower extraction (fix over extraction), reverse them: grind coarser , cool the water, shorten contact time, and reduce agitation. [!note] Change one variable at a time Adjust grind alone first — it is the cleanest lever. If you move grind, temperature, and pour together you won't know which fixed (or broke) the cup. This is the heart of Dialing In Grind Size.

[!warning] A cup can taste under AND over extracted at once Astringent sour and bitter together is the fingerprint of uneven extraction, not a single wrong target. Channeling and Uneven Extraction over extracts some grounds while under extracting others, and the average can sit in the ideal zone while the cup tastes broken. Before reaching for grind, check that your bed extracted evenly — flat top, no cracks, no Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide. Strength is a separate question: a cup can be correctly extracted yet too strong or weak, which you fix with the The Brew Ratio, not the grind.

The The Coffee Brewing Control Chart reports a single average extraction yield — but a brew is millions of particles, and water does not treat them equally. Channeling is when water carves preferential paths through the coffee bed, racing through some zones while barely touching others. The result is uneven extraction : part of the bed is over extracted, part under extracted, and the cup carries both flaws at once even when the average looks perfect. It is the hidden villain behind many "I followed the recipe but it tastes wrong" brews. 🕳️

[!note] The number lies when the bed is uneven Imagine half your grounds extract at 26% and half at 16%. The average is a textbook perfect 21% — but you taste the harsh bitterness of the 26% half and the sour thinness of the 16% half. Even, mediocre extraction often beats uneven, "ideal on average" extraction. Channeling widens the gap between average extraction (what a Refractometers and Measuring Extraction sees) and local extraction (what each particle actually experiences). The goal of good technique is to shrink that gap so the whole bed extracts as uniformly as possible.

Cause What happens Note Fines migration Tiny particles wash down and clog spots, forcing water elsewhere Fines and Boulders Uneven particle size A wide grind spread extracts at many rates at once Particle Distribution and Uniformity Poor pouring Aggressive or off center pours dig holes and crack the bed Pouring Technique Insufficient bloom Trapped CO2, Degassing, and the Bloom Science\ pockets repel water The Bloom Dry pockets / clumps Un wetted grounds never extract Agitation and Turbulence Bed geometry Steep walls and deep beds invite side channels Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers

Channeling leaves clues: visible cracks or fissures in the surface during The Drawdown, fast spouts of water shooting through one spot, a crater or hole in the spent bed, or grounds stranded high and dry on the dripper walls. A flat, evenly settled bed with grounds sitting level is the sign of an even extraction.

Grind uniformly — better Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders narrow the spread of particle sizes. Pre wet evenly and run a proper The Bloom to degas before the main pours. Pour gently and centrally , building height with controlled circles rather than digging. Keep the bed level — a final swirl or gentle The Rao Spin settles grounds flat. Wet all the grounds — no dry clumps clinging to the walls. Flat bottom brewers like the Kalita Wave resist channeling partly because their geometry keeps the bed shallow and even.

title: The Role of Temperature in Extraction

sections.t: Heat is energy, and extraction is an energy driven process — so water temperature is one of the most powerful levers on how fast and how completely coffee dissolves. Hotter water dissolves more solids, faster; cooler water extracts gently and selectively. Sitting alongside Grind Size and Surface Area and Brew Time and Total Contact Time, temperature lets you steer Extraction Yield and Strength without touching your grinder. ♨️

Higher temperature raises the solubility of many compounds and speeds the diffusion of flavor out of each particle. Practically, every degree hotter nudges your brew toward higher Extraction Yield and Strength — useful for dense, hard to extract Light Roast and Specialty Coffee, risky for fragile Roast Levels for Pour Over that turn bitter easily. Temperature also affects which compounds you favor: cooler water extracts acids relatively more and bitter phenolics relatively less, which is the principle behind some Iced and Cold Pour Over techniques.

Water temp Effect Best for 100°C / 212°F (boiling) Maximal extraction, scalding risk Very dense light roasts, high altitude 92–96°C The common pour over sweet spot Most coffees 88–92°C Gentler, more forgiving Medium/dark roasts, lessening bitterness < 85°C Selective, under extraction risk Deliberate experiments The widely cited target is 90–96°C (195–205°F) , the The SCA Water Standard range. See Water Temperature for Brewing for the practical kettle side guidance. [!tip] Use temperature to fine tune, not overhaul Grind is your coarse adjustment; temperature is the fine one. If a cup is slightly sour, a few degrees hotter can fix it without re dialing the grinder.

[!warning] "You must never pour boiling water on coffee" Boiling water won't "burn" coffee — coffee already survived roasting at 200°C+. Off boil water simply gives a touch more headroom against bitterness. Plenty of skilled brewers (and most French Press users) pour at or near boiling on light roasts deliberately. [!warning] "Hotter is always stronger" Hotter raises extraction , not necessarily perceived strength . Strength (TDS) is set mainly by the The Brew Ratio. Temperature changes how much of the coffee dissolves, which is a different axis — see Extraction Yield and Strength.

Water also cools as it sits in the kettle, travels through the air, and contacts a cold dripper and grounds. This is why Pre Wetting the Filter matters, why Dripper Materials and Heat Retention changes the cup, and why later pours in a long brew run cooler than the first.

Grind size is the master lever of extraction, and the reason is pure geometry: smaller particles expose vastly more surface area to the water, and surface area is where dissolution happens. Halve a particle's diameter and you roughly double the surface area for the same mass of coffee — which is why a small twist of the grinder can swing a cup from sour to bitter. This note is the science behind the practical Grind Size for Pour Over in the grinding domain. 🔍

Extraction happens at the interface between coffee and water. A whole bean has almost no usable surface relative to its mass; grinding shatters it into thousands of fragments, multiplying the exposed area enormously. More surface means: More dissolution sites — compounds leave the surface faster. Shorter diffusion paths — flavor deep inside a particle has less distance to travel out. Both effects accelerate The Science of Extraction. So at a fixed time and temperature, finer grind → higher Extraction Yield and Strength , and coarser grind → lower. Grind Surface area Extraction rate Flow / drawdown Coarse Low Slow Fast Medium Moderate Balanced Moderate Fine High Fast Slow (can stall)

Grind changes extraction through two channels at once, which is why it is so potent: 1. Surface area — finer grounds extract faster per unit time. 2. Flow resistance — finer grounds pack tighter, slowing The Drawdown and lengthening Brew Time and Total Contact Time. Both push extraction the same direction, compounding the effect — and at the extreme, an over fine grind can stall the bed entirely, paradoxically risking over extraction from the long contact time. See the practical Dialing In Grind Size.

sections.h: Uniformity Matters as Much as Size
sections.t: [!warning] Surface area isn't the whole story A grinder doesn't make one size — it makes a distribution . Fines and Boulders have huge surface area and over extract; boulders have little and under extract. A wide spread guarantees Channeling and Uneven Extraction no matter where you set the dial, which is why a good Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders often matters more than the exact setting. See Particle Distribution and Uniformity. [!tip] Grind is your single cleanest variable When dialing in, hold ratio, water, and pour steady and move grind alone. Sour cup → finer. Bitter, slow, stalling cup → coarser. It is the most direct route onto the The Coffee Brewing Control Chart sweet spot.
Roast Levels for Pour Over are more brittle and porous, so they grind into more fines and extract faster — meaning a dark roast usually wants a coarser setting than a light roast at the same target.

title: CO2, Degassing, and the Bloom Science

sections.t: Pour fresh coffee its first splash of water and the bed swells, foams, and exhales — the famous bloom . That eruption is carbon dioxide escaping. Roasting fills coffee with CO2, the gas slowly leaks out over days ( degassing ), and how much remains determines how dramatically a coffee blooms and how cleanly it brews. Understanding this chemistry explains both why we bloom and why bean age changes everything. ✨

During roasting, the Maillard reaction and the later caramelization and pyrolysis of sugars generate large volumes of gas — mostly CO2 — that get trapped inside the bean's porous structure. A freshly roasted bean can hold several milliliters of CO2 per gram. From the moment roasting ends, that gas begins to seep out: this is degassing , covered from the bean side in Coffee Freshness and Degassing.

Trapped CO2 actively interferes with brewing: It is hydrophobic acting at the surface — gas pockets repel water, creating dry zones the brew never reaches. Escaping bubbles push water away from the grounds and lift the bed, shortening real contact time. Uneven gassing builds uneven channels, feeding Channeling and Uneven Extraction. Dissolved CO2 also adds a faint carbonic tang and can mute sweetness. [!note] This is why the bloom exists The The Bloom — wetting the grounds with a small pour and pausing 30–45 s — lets the bulk of the CO2 escape before the main pours, so water can then saturate the bed evenly. No bloom on fresh coffee means gassy, uneven, under extracted cups.

Days off roast CO2 state Brewing behavior 0–3 days Very high Violent bloom, gassy, hard to extract evenly 4–14 days Settling The sweet spot — lively bloom, even extraction 2–4 weeks Low Gentle bloom, still good, fading aromatics 1 month+ Mostly gone Flat, stale, Identifying Defects and Off Flavors\ [!tip] Rest, then brew Most filter roasts brew best 4–14 days off roast. Brewing day of roast is often harder , not better — too much gas to manage. Let it rest.

[!warning] "A big bloom means good coffee" A vigorous bloom signals freshness and CO2 content , not quality. A stale but fine coffee blooms weakly; a fresh mediocre one foams impressively. Bloom size measures gas, not deliciousness. [!warning] "Degassing only matters for espresso" It matters for pour over too. Very fresh coffee channels and under extracts in a Hario V60 just as it misbehaves under pressure — the mechanism (gas blocking water) is the same.

Everything else in this knowledge base — the The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta, the Coffee Processing Methods, the Grind Size for Pour Over, the Water for Coffee, the The Brew Ratio, the The Science of Extraction — exists to serve one moment: the cup arriving in your mouth. Tasting and sensory is the domain where the chemistry becomes experience. It is the skill of perceiving what is in a brew, deciding whether you like it, and describing it precisely enough that the description survives the trip from your palate to someone else's ear. This folder teaches you to do all three. Tasting is often mistaken for a gift — something a lucky few are born with. It is not. 🧠 The physiology of smell and taste is remarkably even across healthy adults; what separates a novice from a professional is attention, vocabulary, and reference experience , all of which are trainable. The notes here build that capacity from the ground up, then connect it back to the brewing decisions that produced the flavors you are now naming.

[!note] Read in order, or jump to what you need Start with mechanics, move to standards, then to the building blocks of flavor, and finish with language and practice. Note What it covers How to Taste Coffee Slurping, retronasal aroma, and the attributes to assess in any cup Cupping The standardized industry protocol for evaluating coffee side by side The Coffee Flavor Wheel The SCA \ World Coffee Research lexicon and how to navigate it Acidity in Coffee The acids that make coffee bright — a virtue, not a flaw Sweetness and Body Perceived sweetness and the mouthfeel we call body Aroma and Flavor Compounds The volatile chemistry behind coffee's smell and taste Identifying Defects and Off Flavors Sour, bitter, astringent, baggy, ferment — and their fixes Building Your Palate Comparative tasting, reference flavors, and journaling Describing Coffee — The Vocabulary Talking about a cup precisely and usefully

Sensory skill closes the feedback loop. When you can tell that a cup is hollow and sour rather than balanced, you know to grind finer or pour hotter; when it is harsh and drying, you know to back off. This is the same diagnostic logic that powers Under Extraction and Over Extraction and the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide — but those notes describe causes, while your palate detects effects. The two only work together. 🔁 [!tip] Taste everything, every time The fastest way to improve is to taste your own brews deliberately, even the bad ones — especially the bad ones. A cup you cannot describe is a lesson you cannot repeat.

Flavor perception is partly personal — genetics, culture, and memory all shape what you notice and prefer. Professionals manage this not by pretending to be objective but by anchoring to shared references : the Cupping, the The Coffee Flavor Wheel, and a common Describing Coffee — The Vocabulary. Calibration, not innate superiority, is what lets two tasters agree.

Tasting coffee well is a deliberate act, not a sip. The goal is to gather as much sensory information as possible from each mouthful and to do it the same way every time, so that differences you notice come from the coffee rather than from how you drank it. This note covers the mechanics professionals use — many borrowed from the formal Cupping protocol — and the specific attributes worth assessing in any cup, whether it came from a Hario V60 or a French Press.

Most of what we call flavor is actually aroma, detected by the nose. Before tasting, smell the dry grounds , then the wet crust after water hits them, then the liquid itself. Aroma fatigues quickly, so take short sniffs rather than long inhalations. The chemistry behind these scents lives in Aroma and Flavor Compounds; the language for them lives on The Coffee Flavor Wheel.

[!tip] Why professionals slurp loudly Slurping aerates the coffee into a fine spray that coats the whole tongue and forces aromatic vapor up the back of the throat to the olfactory receptors — this is retronasal aroma , and it is where the majority of flavor perception happens. The noise is a side effect of doing it right, not bad manners. Draw the coffee in sharply over a relaxed tongue, let it spread across the entire mouth, and breathe out gently through your nose as you do. The same liquid tasted by sipping politely will read flatter and less complex than the same liquid slurped. 🔊

Evaluate these attributes in roughly this order, because earlier ones influence how you read later ones: Attribute Question to ask Deep dive Fragrance / Aroma What does it smell like, dry and wet? Aroma and Flavor Compounds Acidity Is it bright, lively, sharp, or flat? Acidity in Coffee Sweetness Does it taste of sugar, fruit, caramel? Sweetness and Body Body How heavy or light is the mouthfeel? Sweetness and Body Flavor What specific notes appear? Describing Coffee — The Vocabulary Aftertaste What lingers, and is it pleasant? The Coffee Flavor Wheel Balance Do the parts add up to a whole? Cupping

Coffee changes dramatically as it cools, and cooling reveals truth . Heat suppresses sweetness and masks defects; many flavors — and many Identifying Defects and Off Flavors — only emerge once a cup drops below 50 °C. Taste the same brew hot, warm, and near room temperature, and note how the impression shifts. A coffee that is only good piping hot is usually hiding something.

[!warning] Tongue zones are a myth The old diagram showing sweet at the tip and bitter at the back is false — every taste receptor type is distributed across the whole tongue. Coat your entire palate when you slurp; do not chase flavors to specific spots. Avoid toothpaste, strong food, and smoking before a serious tasting, and reset with water between cups. Tasting in Building Your Palate — two or more coffees side by side — sharpens perception far faster than tasting one cup alone, because contrast makes subtle differences obvious.

Cupping is the coffee industry's standardized method for tasting and scoring coffee. Roasters, buyers, graders, and competitors all use it because it strips away nearly every brewing variable — no The Dripper Explained, no Pouring Technique, no Grind Size for Pour Over — leaving the coffee itself as the only thing that differs from cup to cup. When a green buyer in one country and a roaster in another need to agree on what a lot tastes like, cupping is the common language. ☕

A Hario V60 brew reflects the brewer as much as the bean. Cupping removes that confound by fixing every parameter: a set The Brew Ratio, a single Grind Size for Pour Over, one Water Temperature for Brewing, and an identical steeping time across every sample. Differences you taste must therefore come from the coffees — different Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir, Coffee Processing Methods, or Roast Levels for Pour Over — making cupping a fair comparison and a reliable diagnostic.

[!example] The standard SCA cupping setup Ratio: 8.25 g coffee per 150 mL water (about 1:18), weighed per cup Grind: coarse, like sea salt — ground fresh, one sample at a time Water: clean, 93 °C, poured to saturate all grounds Cups: typically 5 per sample, to catch a single tainted bean Method: immersion — no filter, grounds steep in the cup The sequence is fixed: 1. Smell the dry grounds and record fragrance. 2. Pour water over each cup and let it steep for 4 minutes; a crust of grounds forms on top. 3. Break the crust by pushing through it three times with a spoon while leaning in to inhale the released aroma — a signature, almost ceremonial step. 👃 4. Skim the floating grounds and foam off the surface. 5. Slurp from a spoon as the coffee cools, evaluating across temperatures.

The SCA cupping form rates each attribute on a 6–10 scale; the sum, after deductions for defective cups, yields the cup's overall score. A coffee scoring 80 points or above is "specialty" grade — the threshold that defines the entire The Specialty Coffee Movement category. Attribute What it captures Fragrance / Aroma Smell of dry grounds and wet crust Flavor & Aftertaste The core taste and what lingers Acidity Quality of brightness — see Acidity in Coffee Body Weight and texture — see Sweetness and Body Balance How the attributes integrate Sweetness, Clean Cup, Uniformity Often scored as present/absent across the 5 cups Tasters describe what they find using The Coffee Flavor Wheel and the shared Describing Coffee — The Vocabulary, and flag any Identifying Defects and Off Flavors that pull the score down. [!note] Cupping is for evaluation, not enjoyment An immersion cup at 1:18 is not how you would drink a coffee — it is a microscope. The flavors it reveals still predict how a coffee will behave in your Brewing Technique, which is why even home brewers benefit from learning to cup.

The Coffee Taster's Flavor Wheel is a circular diagram that organizes the flavors found in coffee from broad, general categories at its center to precise, specific descriptors at its rim. First published by the Specialty Coffee Association in 1995 and completely rebuilt in 2016 by the SCA together with World Coffee Research , it gives tasters a shared map for naming what they perceive — so that "fruity" can be sharpened to "berry," and "berry" to "blackberry," with everyone meaning the same thing. 🫐

The 2016 wheel was not invented by a committee guessing at words. It is the visible tip of the World Coffee Research Sensory Lexicon , a rigorous reference in which trained panels defined and calibrated each attribute against physical samples — actual foods and aromas you could buy and smell. That grounding is what separates the wheel from a poetic word cloud: every descriptor traces back to a reproducible reference, which is exactly what Building Your Palate relies on. [!note] Read it from the inside out The center holds nine broad families — fruity, floral, sweet, nutty/cocoa, spices, roasted, green/vegetative, sour/fermented, and other. Each spoke fans outward into mid level groups and then to specific terms. You move outward only as far as your confidence allows.

The wheel is a tool for converging on language , used in three steps: 1. Start broad. Decide which inner family fits — is this coffee fundamentally fruity , nutty/cocoa , or roasted ? 2. Move outward. Within fruity, is it berry , citrus , stone fruit , or dried fruit ? 3. Land on a leaf. Citrus → lemon ? Berry → raspberry ? Stop at the level you can honestly defend. If the cup reads… Look in this family Specifics you might land on Bright and tart Fruity / Sour citrus, berry, malic acid — see Acidity in Coffee Rich and toasty Nutty / Cocoa almond, hazelnut, dark chocolate Burnt or ashy Roasted acrid, smoky — often a Roast Levels for Pour Over\ effect Funky or boozy Sour / Fermented winey, overripe — check Identifying Defects and Off Flavors

The 2016 wheel uses color drawn from the real foods (yellow lemons, purple grapes) and gaps between wedges to show how related two flavors are — terms placed near each other are often confused, terms far apart rarely co occur. This makes the wheel a reasoning aid, not just a dictionary: if you are torn between "blueberry" and "blackberry," their adjacency tells you the distinction is genuinely subtle. [!tip] The wheel describes, it does not rank No flavor on the wheel is inherently "good" or "bad." A jasmine note and a tobacco note are simply different ; whether either is desirable depends on the coffee and your preference. Quality is judged on the Cupping form, not by where a descriptor sits on the wheel. The wheel pairs naturally with the broader Describing Coffee — The Vocabulary, which adds the structural words — clean , balanced , juicy — that the wheel's flavor only focus leaves out.

In coffee tasting, acidity is a virtue, not a fault. It is the bright, lively, mouth watering quality that makes a great light roast Kenyan taste like blackcurrant or a Colombian taste like green apple. Newcomers often hear "acidic" as a warning, confusing it with the sourness of an under brewed cup — but the two are not the same. Desirable acidity is crisp and refreshing ; unpleasant sourness is sharp and empty . Learning to tell them apart is one of the biggest leaps in Building Your Palate. ✨

Coffee genuinely contains acids — it sits around pH 4.85 to 5.10, comparable to a tomato. The pleasant brightness we taste comes from a handful of organic acids created in the plant and transformed during Coffee Roasting Explained: Acid Tastes like Notes Citric Lemon, orange Highest in high grown Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir\ Malic Green apple, pear Crisp, clean brightness Phosphoric Sparkling, cola like Gives African coffees their zing Chlorogenic (precursor) Breaks down in the roast into the below Quinic & Caffeic Sour, astringent Rise in dark roasts and stale coffee [!note] Bright vs. sour — the key distinction Brightness is structured and sweet backed: it makes your mouth water and resolves cleanly. Sourness is the thin, harsh acidity of Under Extraction and Over Extraction\ — acids dissolve early in a brew, so a cup pulled too short tastes aggressively sour because the balancing sweetness never extracted. The fix is usually a finer Grind Size for Pour Over\ or hotter Water Temperature for Brewing\, not less acidic coffee.

🌱 Origin and altitude. Coffee grown high and cool develops more acidity. This is why washed Ethiopian and Kenyan coffees are prized for their sparkling, fruit forward cups, while low grown coffees taste rounder and softer. See Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir. 🔥 Roast level. Acidity falls as roast deepens. Light roasts preserve the delicate citric and malic acids; dark roasts burn them off and build heavier, bitter notes instead — a core reason Light Roast and Specialty Coffee dominate pour over. See Roast Levels for Pour Over. 💧 Brewing. Higher temperatures, finer grinds, and longer contact all raise extraction and can either reveal balanced brightness or, pushed too far, flatten it. Water Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity also matters: high alkalinity water buffers acids and mutes brightness, which is why The SCA Water Standard keeps bicarbonate moderate.

Acidity has both intensity (how much) and quality (how pleasant). On the Cupping form it is scored on quality, not quantity — a delicate but exquisite acidity can outscore a loud, harsh one. Use the fruit and sour wedges of The Coffee Flavor Wheel to name it: malic reads as apple, citric as lemon, and a coffee with little acid simply tastes flat. [!tip] Train the difference fast Brew the same light roast coffee twice — once correctly, once ground far too coarse so it under extracts. Taste them side by side. The harsh, hollow sourness of the second cup against the juicy brightness of the first will teach you the distinction in one sitting.

If Acidity in Coffee is coffee's brightness, sweetness is its reward and body is its weight. Together they form the backbone of a satisfying cup: sweetness makes flavors pleasant and balanced, while body gives the coffee presence and texture on the tongue. Both are widely misunderstood — sweetness in black coffee surprises people, and "body" gets used as a vague compliment when it actually describes something specific and measurable. This note pins down what each is and what you can do to maximize it.

Coffee can taste genuinely sweet without any sugar added. Green coffee contains sugars that, during Coffee Roasting Explained, partly caramelize and partly drive the Maillard reaction , generating caramel, honey, chocolate, and toasted notes. Some sweetness is true taste; much is aroma read as sweetness by the brain — a vanilla or caramel smell makes a cup seem sweeter even though sugar levels are low. [!note] Sweetness signals good extraction Sweet compounds dissolve in the middle of a brew, after the early sour acids and before the late bitter ones. A balanced, fully sweet cup is therefore evidence of even The Science of Extraction\. Cups that finish sour are usually Under Extraction and Over Extraction\; cups that turn harsh and dry are over extracted, and in both cases the sweetness goes missing. What drives sweetness: 🌱 Ripe, well processed cherry — sugar starts in the fruit. Natural Process and Honey and Experimental Processing often read sweeter than Washed Process. 🔥 Roast development — under developed roasts taste grassy and sour; well developed ones unlock caramelized sweetness. 💧 Even extraction — a clean Pouring Technique without Bypass and Channeling lets the sweet middle of the brew come through.

sections.h: Body — The Texture of the Cup
sections.t: Body (or mouthfeel ) is the tactile weight and texture of coffee, sensed by touch rather than taste — from thin and tea like to thick, syrupy, and creamy. It comes from physical material suspended in the brew: insoluble oils, fine colloids, and tiny coffee particles that survive the Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth. Factor More body Less body Filter Metal / cloth — see French Press Paper — see Chemex Dripper Kalita Wave\, slower flow Fast Hario V60\ Grind Finer (more Fines and Boulders\) Coarser, cleaner Roast Darker roasts Light roasts [!tip] Body and clarity trade off Paper filters trap oils and fines, producing a clean, light bodied, transparent cup that shows off Acidity in Coffee\ and delicate flavors. Metal and cloth let more through, giving a heavier, rounder, muddier cup. Neither is better — it is a stylistic choice you control with equipment, explained in Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers.

Assess sweetness by what lingers pleasantly after you swallow, and body by pressing the liquid against your palate and noting its weight — the way you would distinguish skim milk from cream. Both are scored on the Cupping form, and both are named through The Coffee Flavor Wheel (caramel, honey) and the Describing Coffee — The Vocabulary (silky, syrupy, watery).

When you describe a coffee as smelling of jasmine, blueberry, or roasted hazelnut, you are reading the work of volatile aromatic compounds — molecules light enough to evaporate into the air and reach the smell receptors in your nose. Coffee is one of the most aromatically complex foods on earth: over 800 to 1,000 volatile compounds have been identified in roasted beans, though only a few dozen contribute most of the character we actually perceive. This note explains where those molecules come from and why aroma dominates the experience of How to Taste Coffee. 🧪

A foundational fact of sensory science: the tongue detects only five basic tastes — sweet, sour, salty, bitter, umami — while the nose distinguishes thousands of smells . What we casually call flavor is taste and aroma fused together by the brain. Pinch your nose while sipping coffee and it collapses to mere bitterness and acidity; release it and the cup blooms into fruit, flowers, and caramel via retronasal aroma — the reason professionals How to Taste Coffee. [!note] Orthonasal vs. retronasal Orthonasal smell is sniffing the cup from outside; retronasal is the aroma rising from inside your mouth up the back of the throat as you swallow. The same molecules can read differently by each route, which is why a coffee can smell of one thing and taste of another.

Aromatics are built across the coffee's whole life, then largely created in the roaster: Source Process Resulting aromas The green bean Genetics & Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir\ Floral, fruity precursors Processing Coffee Processing Methods\ Berry, winey, tropical (esp. Natural Process\) Roasting Maillard & caramelization Nutty, chocolate, toasty Roasting (hot) Pyrolysis Smoky, spicy, roasty The Maillard reaction (amino acids + sugars) and caramelization (sugars alone) during Coffee Roasting Explained generate the bulk of coffee's aromatic library — pyrazines for nutty/roasted notes, furans for caramel, and aldehydes and esters for fruit and flowers. Key players include β damascenone (fruity floral), guaiacol (smoky spicy), and 2 furfurylthiol, a sulfur compound responsible for the unmistakable "fresh brewed coffee" smell.

Volatile means fleeting . ⏳ Aromatics are exactly the molecules that escape fastest, which is why coffee's character fades with time and exposure: Staling. Ground coffee loses its most delicate aromatics within minutes. See Coffee Freshness and Degassing — grind immediately before brewing. CO₂'s role. Fresh roasted beans trap aromatic gases that escape during The Bloom; this is why a fresh coffee smells so much louder. See CO2, Degassing, and the Bloom Science. Temperature. Hot coffee releases aroma vigorously then quiets as it cools — another reason to taste How to Taste Coffee. [!tip] Smell more than you sip Because aroma carries most of the information, deliberately smelling the dry grounds, the wet Cupping, and the cooling cup will teach your palate faster than tasting alone. Map what you find onto The Coffee Flavor Wheel, whose descriptors are themselves organized by these compound families.

Learning what good coffee tastes like is only half of palate training; the other half is recognizing what is wrong and tracing it to a cause. Off flavors come from two very different places: defects baked into the coffee before you ever touched it — at the farm, the mill, or the roaster — and brewing faults you create at the Brewing Technique stage. The first you can only avoid by buying better; the second you can fix this morning. Telling them apart is the diagnostic skill this note builds. 🔍

sections.h: Brewing Faults — You Can Fix These
sections.t: These are the most common complaints from home brewers, and almost all trace back to Under Extraction and Over Extraction: Off flavor Tastes like Likely cause Fix Sour Sharp, hollow, harsh Under extraction Grind finer, hotter Water Temperature for Brewing\, longer Brew Time and Total Contact Time\ Bitter Acrid, lingering, dry Over extraction Grind coarser, cooler water, less Agitation and Turbulence\ Weak / watery Thin, flavorless Wrong The Brew Ratio\ or under extraction Less water per gram, finer grind Astringent Mouth puckering, drying Channeling / Fines and Boulders\ Even Pouring Technique\, better grinder Ashy / burnt Smoky, charred Dark roast or scorched roast Lighter Roast Levels for Pour Over\ [!warning] Sour and bitter are opposite problems The single most useful diagnostic in coffee: sour means too little extraction, bitter means too much. They sit at opposite ends of the same dial. If a cup is both — sour and harsh — you usually have uneven extraction from Bypass and Channeling, where some grounds over extract while others barely brew. The Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide walks through each case.

A frequent confusion: astringency is a tactile drying, puckering sensation (like over steeped tea), not a taste. It comes from certain chlorogenic acid breakdown products and excess Fines and Boulders, and it signals uneven or excessive extraction. Naming it correctly points you to grinder quality and pour evenness rather than to the dial of grind size alone.

sections.h: Green Coffee and Roast Defects — You Cannot Brew These Out
sections.t: Some faults are intrinsic to the bean and will haunt every cup no matter how well you brew: 🛢️ Baggy / faded / woody — stale green coffee held too long; flat, papery, cardboard notes. 🍷 Ferment / fermented — over fermentation during Coffee Processing Methods; boozy, rotten, or onion like. A little funk is fashionable in modern naturals; too much is a defect — the line is contested and divides tasters. 🥔 Potato defect — a raw potato taint linked to a bug, notably in some East African coffees. 🌫️ Phenolic / medicinal — harsh, iodine like, often a processing or storage fault. Quakers — under ripe beans that fail to roast properly; pale, peanutty, papery. [!note] Fresh ≠ flawless, and old ≠ broken A defect is a quality problem, not just an age problem. Conversely, a coffee can be perfectly clean yet taste dull simply because it is past peak Coffee Freshness and Degassing — that is staling, not a defect. Use Building Your Palate to learn the difference.

A trained palate is not a gift you are born with — it is a skill you build through deliberate practice, exactly like learning an instrument or a language. The good news is that the underlying hardware is roughly equal across healthy people; what professionals possess is organized memory and vocabulary , not magic taste buds. This note lays out a practical training program: how to taste comparatively, how to anchor flavors to physical references, and how to record what you learn so it compounds over time. 📈

You cannot reliably judge a flavor in isolation, because your palate has no fixed ruler. Place two cups side by side, however, and differences leap out. Always taste against a reference. [!tip] Build a tasting flight Brew two or three coffees — or the same coffee brewed differently — at once, and slurp back and forth. Effective contrasts: One coffee at the right Grind Size for Pour Over\ vs. ground too coarse → learn Acidity in Coffee\ A Washed Process\ vs. a Natural Process\ of the same origin → learn processing character A Light Roast and Specialty Coffee\ vs. a dark roast → learn what roast does Paper filtered vs. French Press\ → learn Sweetness and Body\ and clarity Contrast turns a vague impression ("this is nice") into a precise observation ("this one is brighter and cleaner, that one heavier and sweeter").

The The Coffee Flavor Wheel and the World Coffee Research Sensory Lexicon work because their descriptors point to physical samples . You can build your own library cheaply: smell and taste actual blueberries, lemons, jasmine tea, dark chocolate, almonds, brown sugar, and tomato (for Acidity in Coffee). Once you have consciously registered what blackcurrant smells like, your brain can match it in a cup. 🫐 Sense to train Cheap reference exercise Fruity acidity Smell lemon, apple, blackcurrant before tasting Sweetness Compare honey, caramel, brown sugar aromas Body Feel skim milk vs. cream in the mouth Defects Smell wet cardboard (baggy), over ripe fruit (ferment) Aroma kit products (like Le Nez du Café ) formalize this, but a kitchen and a grocery run accomplish most of it.

Memory is the bottleneck, and writing defeats it. Keep a simple tasting journal — paper or app — and for every coffee record the same fields, so entries become comparable over months: [!example] A minimal tasting journal entry Coffee: origin, Coffee Processing Methods\, Roast Levels for Pour Over\, roast date Brew: The Brew Ratio\, Grind Size for Pour Over\, temp, time, method Aroma / Acidity / Sweetness / Body / Flavor / Aftertaste Score & one line verdict — would I buy it again? Logging the brew alongside the taste is what links sensory training back to technique: over time your journal reveals which variables move which flavors, turning guesswork into the directed adjustments of the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide.

sections.h: Practice Habits That Speed It Up
sections.t: Cup regularly. The Cupping protocol is the gym of palate training — same setup, many coffees. Taste blind sometimes. Hiding the label stops expectation from biasing perception. Protect your instrument. Skip toothpaste, smoking, and strong food before serious tasting. Taste your mistakes. A failed brew you can describe is the most valuable cup of the day.

Once you can perceive what is in a cup, the final skill is describing it — putting words to flavor precisely enough that another person can picture the coffee they have never tasted. This is harder than it sounds, because flavor language is slippery and often pretentious. The goal is not to sound impressive; it is to be useful, specific, and honest . A good description tells someone whether they will like a coffee and helps you remember it later. This note organizes the vocabulary into the categories tasters actually use. ☕

sections.h: Two Kinds of Words: Flavor and Structure
sections.t: A complete description needs both. Flavor words name what the coffee tastes of — blueberry, jasmine, milk chocolate, lemon. These come straight from The Coffee Flavor Wheel, moving from broad ("fruity") to specific ("raspberry") only as far as you can honestly defend. Structure words describe how the cup is built — its acidity, body, sweetness, balance, and finish. The wheel leaves these out, so tasters borrow a separate vocabulary, much of it adapted from wine. [!note] Specific beats poetic "Tastes like ripe strawberry and brown sugar, with bright apple acidity and a clean finish" tells a reader something real. "An ode to a summer meadow" tells them nothing. Reach for the most specific term you genuinely perceive and stop there.

Term Means Related note Bright / lively Pleasant, mouth watering acidity Acidity in Coffee Flat / dull Lacking acidity Acidity in Coffee Clean No off flavors; transparent Identifying Defects and Off Flavors Juicy / vibrant Sweet acidity, fruit forward The Coffee Flavor Wheel Body: silky / syrupy / watery Mouthfeel weight Sweetness and Body Balanced No single attribute dominates Cupping Complex Many distinct, evolving notes Aroma and Flavor Compounds Finish / aftertaste What lingers after swallowing How to Taste Coffee

A subtle but professional habit: separate how much of something there is from how good it is. A coffee can have high acidity that is harsh , or delicate acidity that is exquisite . The Cupping scores quality, not quantity, and good descriptions reflect that — say "gentle but elegant sweetness," not just "sweet." [!warning] Beware borrowed and meaningless words Much coffee marketing language is vague ("smooth," "robust," "bold") or copied from wine without grounding. "Smooth" can mean low acid, low bitter, or simply inoffensive — it tells the reader little. When you use a word, be ready to say what you tasted that justifies it. Honesty also means admitting when a cup is simply "pleasant but unremarkable."

[!tip] The four part description 1. Lead flavor — the most obvious note ("blackcurrant") 2. Acidity — type and quality ("bright, malic apple acidity") 3. Body & sweetness — texture and reward ("medium body, caramel sweetness") 4. Finish & verdict — what lingers, overall ("clean, lingering finish — excellent") Build your fluency through Building Your Palate and journaling: the more cups you describe out loud and on paper, the more your words converge with the shared language of Cupping tables and roaster tasting notes — and the more reliably someone else can trust your description.

Pour over is more than a brewing technique — it is a culture , an industry , and a community that together turned a niche manual method into a global craft. This is the map of the human side of coffee: the people who grow, roast, sell, brew, compete, film, and argue about it. Where the brewing notes explain how to make a cup, these notes explain who makes the cup possible, where the beans come from, and why the world started caring so much about a slow drip of water through ground coffee. This folder is the hub for the Culture & Industry domain. Use it as a table of contents, then follow the links outward into history, equipment brands, and the recipes named after the people below.
