Note What it covers The Specialty Coffee Industry The SCA, the 80 point grading line, and how the modern market is structured Notable Roasters Influential specialty roasters who shaped the filter coffee aesthetic Coffee Shops and Cafe Culture Third wave cafés, the pour over bar, and café ritual The World Brewers Cup The competition that pushes home brewing technique forward Coffee Gear Brands Hario, Kalita, Fellow, Acaia and the gear ecosystem Baristas and Home Brewers The professional craft and the hobbyist community Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing Commodity, Fair Trade, direct trade — and their real limits Sustainability in Coffee Climate, farmer economics, and coffee's footprint Coffee Online — Communities and Content YouTube, forums, and the online driven renaissance

The story runs in a loop. Farmers and sourcing models (see Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing) feed green coffee to Notable Roasters, who shaped the Light Roast and Specialty Coffee that filter brewing flatters. Those roasters supply Coffee Shops and Cafe Culture, where Baristas and Home Brewers turn brewing into performance — most visibly at The World Brewers Cup. The gear they use comes from Coffee Gear Brands like Hario and Acaia, and the whole scene is documented, debated, and amplified by Coffee Online — Communities and Content. Threaded through every link is the question of Sustainability in Coffee — whether this culture can endure. [!note] A culture built on a movement Pour over's cultural moment grew directly out of the The Specialty Coffee Movement and the First, Second, and Third Wave Coffee. The history notes tell that origin story; these notes pick it up in the present. [!warning] Romance vs. reality The coffee industry tells beautiful stories about itself — "direct trade," "sustainable," "single origin," "ethically sourced." Many are partly true and partly marketing. These notes flag where the popular narrative outruns the evidence, especially around Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing and Sustainability in Coffee.

The specialty coffee industry is the slice of the global coffee trade built around quality rather than commodity volume — coffees evaluated, scored, and sold on the basis of how good they taste, not merely how cheaply they can be produced. It is the economic and institutional ground that pour over grew out of, and the reason a bag of beans can carry a farm name, a varietal, a process, and a cupping score on its label.

The defining technical fact of specialty coffee is a grading scale . Trained tasters, called Q graders, evaluate a coffee on a 100 point scale during a structured Cupping — judging aroma, flavor, acidity, body, balance, and the absence of defects. A score of 80 points or above is the conventional threshold for "specialty." Below it, coffee is graded as commercial or commodity. [!note] A score, not a guarantee The 80 point line is meaningful but imperfect. Scores depend on the taster, the roast, and the day; a "85 point" coffee from one cupper may cup lower elsewhere. Treat scores as informed signals, not absolute truth — a theme echoed in How to Taste Coffee. Score band Grade Typical use 90+ Outstanding / "presentation" Competition, micro lots, auction coffees 85–89 Excellent Premium single origin filter coffee 80–84 Very good / specialty entry Most quality Single Origin vs Blends\ and blends Below 80 Commercial / commodity Supermarket and instant coffee

The Specialty Coffee Association (SCA) — formed in 2017 from the merger of the American and European specialty associations — is the field's central body. It sets standards (including the The SCA Water Standard and the The Coffee Brewing Control Chart), runs education and certification, publishes research, and organizes the World Coffee Championships, including The World Brewers Cup. Its standards quietly shape how cafés brew and how Notable Roasters talk about quality.

The industry sits atop the commodity market but tries to escape it. Most of the world's coffee trades as a fungible commodity whose price is set on the futures market (the "C price"). Specialty coffee carves out a premium tier through differentiation — origin, varietal, process, score — and through alternative Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing that promise to pay farmers above the commodity rate. [!warning] The value capture problem Most of the money in a premium cup is captured downstream — by roasters, cafés, and retailers — not by the farmer. Specialty pricing helps, but the structural imbalance is real and persistent. See Sustainability in Coffee for the farmer economics side. The industry is also defined by its wave narrative — commodity convenience, then branded café chains, then the quality obsessed First, Second, and Third Wave Coffee that made Light Roast and Specialty Coffee, origin forward, manually brewed coffee the aspirational standard.

A handful of specialty roasters did more than sell beans — they defined the aesthetic of modern filter coffee: lighter roasts, transparent sourcing, named producers, and recipes published for the world to copy. This note profiles a few of the most influential. It is deliberately non exhaustive and non ranked : hundreds of excellent roasters exist worldwide, and the ones below are illustrative landmarks, not a definitive "best" list. [!note] Why these names recur Influential roasters tend to share three habits: they champion Light Roast and Specialty Coffee suited to pour over, they publish technique openly, and they invest in Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing. That combination is what turns a roastery into a cultural reference point.

Roaster Base Why it matters Tim Wendelboe Oslo, Norway World Barista Champion (2004) turned micro roaster; a global benchmark for ultra light Nordic roasting and farm level sourcing Square Mile London, UK Co founded by Coffee Online — Communities and Content\ and Anette Moldvaer; helped set the UK third wave standard Onyx Coffee Lab Arkansas, USA Competition driven roaster known for meticulous published recipes — see Onyx Coffee Lab V60 Recipe Counter Culture North Carolina, USA An early US specialty pioneer, influential in training and transparency reporting Blue Bottle California, USA Bridged third wave craft and the mainstream; later majority acquired by Nestlé

The Nordic school — Tim Wendelboe , Coffee Collective, and peers — pushed roasts so light they reframed what "done" meant, prioritizing origin clarity and acidity over roast character. In the UK, Square Mile paired competition pedigree with retail. In the US, Counter Culture , Intelligentsia , and Stumptown formed an early triumvirate, while Blue Bottle carried the look and feel of specialty into airports and offices. Onyx later became the data forward face of competition style brewing. [!warning] Marketing vs. substance "Direct trade," "single farm," and "relationship coffee" appear on many roaster bags. Some are rigorously sourced; some are looser stories. The terms are mostly unregulated — read Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing before taking any sourcing claim at face value.

Independent roasting is a thin margin craft. Many beloved roasters stay small by choice; others scale, and a few are acquired — Blue Bottle by Nestlé, Stumptown and Intelligentsia by JAB linked owners. Acquisition is divisive in the community: it brings capital and reach but raises questions about whether the First, Second, and Third Wave Coffee ethos survives corporate ownership. These roasters supply the Coffee Shops and Cafe Culture where their coffee is brewed, set the Roast Levels for Pour Over that filter brewing flatters, and shape what home brewers buy.

The café is where pour over became public ritual. Long before most people owned a Hario V60 at home, they encountered manual brewing across a counter — watching a barista weigh beans, bloom the grounds, and pour in slow concentric circles. The third wave café turned brewing into theatre, education, and a quietly persuasive sales pitch for the whole craft.

The signature fixture of a third wave café is the pour over bar (or "brew bar"): a dedicated station, often with a row of The Dripper Explained on stands, a Gooseneck Kettles, a Scales, Timers, and Servers, and a menu of Single Origin vs Blends brewed to order. Ordering a pour over there is slow on purpose — three to four minutes of The Anatomy of a Pour Over in front of you, sold as an experience rather than a quick caffeine fix. [!example] The brew bar ritual Bean chosen → dose weighed → Coffee Roasting Explained\ mentioned → grind dialed → The Bloom\ poured → measured pours → cup presented with tasting notes. Each step is both craft and quiet teaching.

sections.h: 🌊 What "Third Wave" Means in a Café
sections.t: The café embodies the First, Second, and Third Wave Coffee in physical form. Where second wave chains sold comfort, consistency, and dark roasted espresso drinks, third wave cafés foreground origin transparency , Light Roast and Specialty Coffee, and the producer's story. Common signals include: Feature What it signals Visible scales and timers Brewing treated as measurable craft Roast dates on bags Emphasis on Coffee Freshness and Degassing\ Single origin filter menu Coffee as terroir, not just a drink Minimalist, light filled design The third wave visual language Baristas who discuss flavor Education as part of service

sections.h: 🌍 Café Culture as a Social Form
sections.t: Cafés are also "third places" — social spaces that are neither home nor work. Specialty cafés inherited that role from older traditions: the Viennese coffeehouse, the Italian espresso bar, and especially the Japanese Japanese Coffee Culture and the Kissaten, whose patient, hand brewed The Siphon and Nel Drip Tradition service is a direct ancestor of the modern brew bar. [!warning] The aesthetics trap Minimalist tile, exposed bulbs, and oat milk flat whites are now a global café template — sometimes copied as a look without the substance. A beautiful café is not automatically a good one; judge it by the coffee and the people, not the lighting. The café is the bridge between Notable Roasters and the public, the natural habitat of the professional Baristas and Home Brewers, and the gateway through which most home brewers first fall in love with the method.

The World Brewers Cup (WBrC) is the competition that put manual filter brewing on a global stage. Organized under the Specialty Coffee Association as one of the World Coffee Championships, it asks a deceptively simple question: who can brew the best cup of filter coffee, by hand, and explain why? Its annual routines have a measurable downstream effect — the techniques, recipes, and gear that win tend to ripple straight into home brewing.

Competitors face two services, judged by sensory and technical panels. Service What happens Coffee used Compulsory Brew using coffee supplied by the organizers, unseen until shortly before Same beans for everyone — levels the field Open Brew a coffee of the competitor's choice while delivering a presentation Free choice — often a rare, high scoring lot In the open service , the competitor brews (usually three cups for the judges), talks through the coffee's origin, processing, and intended flavors, and is scored on the cup and the presentation. The compulsory round strips that away, testing raw brewing skill on unfamiliar beans. Most competitors use a Hario V60 or another Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers, a Gooseneck Kettles, and obsessively Building Water — Recipes and Remineralization. [!note] It rewards explanation, not just taste Unlike a blind cupping, the Brewers Cup judges the story as well as the cup. Clear reasoning about Extraction Yield and Strength and flavor is part of the score — which is why winners double as teachers.

Several champions translated their routines into recipes that home brewers still use: Stefanos Domatiotis (Greece, 2014 champion, of Taf Coffee) — see Stefanos Domatiotis Brewers Cup Recipe. Emi Fukahori (Switzerland, 2018 champion) — see Emi Fukahori Brewers Cup Recipe. Matt Winton (Switzerland, 2021 champion) — popularized a precise multi pour "triangulation" approach; see Matt Winton Triangulation Method. Tetsu Kasuya (Japan, 2016 champion) — whose adjustable Tetsu Kasuya 4:6 Method became one of the most widely taught home recipes in the world. More recent champions (such as Austria's Martin Wölfl in 2024) continue the pattern of competition technique flowing outward. [!warning] Competition ≠ everyday Winning routines use rare, expensive coffees, lab built water, and conditions tuned for three perfect cups — not a daily two cup morning brew. Borrow the principles (even pours, controlled Agitation and Turbulence, deliberate Pulse vs Continuous Pouring); don't expect a competition cup from supermarket beans. The Brewers Cup is the proving ground that links Baristas and Home Brewers to the Competition Recipes this knowledge base documents — a feedback loop between the stage and the kitchen counter.

Pour over culture runs on gear , and a surprisingly small set of brands defines the modern brewer's bench. Some are decades old manufacturers from Japan and Germany; others are startups born from the The Modern Pour Over Renaissance. Together they form an ecosystem where a dripper from one company, a kettle from another, and a scale from a third assemble into a single ritual.

Brand Origin Known for Hario Japan The Hario V60\ — the world's defining conical dripper — plus servers and kettles Kalita Japan The flat bottom Kalita Wave\, favored for forgiving, even extraction Chemex USA The iconic hourglass Chemex\ brewer and its thick bonded filters Origami Japan The colorful, ribbed Origami Dripper\ that takes both conical and flat filters Fellow USA Design forward kettles (the Stagg EKG) and grinders that defined the modern aesthetic Comandante Germany The Nitro Blade Hand Grinders vs Electric Grinders\, a benchmark for hand grinding Acaia USA/Taiwan The Pearl/Lunar Bluetooth brewing Scales, Timers, and Servers\ that made data driven brewing normal OXO USA Accessible, well designed gear including the OXO Rapid Brewer\

Two cultures meet on the brew bar. The heritage manufacturers — Hario (founded 1921 as a glass company), Kalita, and Germany's Melitta, whose founder Melitta Bentz and the Paper Filter in 1908 — make the drippers and Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth that brewing literally depends on. The renaissance startups — Fellow, Acaia, Timemore, Weber, and others — entered through design and electronics, turning kettles, scales, and grinders into objects of desire. [!note] The filter lock in Many drippers are tied to their maker's filters: a V60 wants a V60 cone, a Kalita wants a Wave filter, a Chemex wants Chemex bonded paper. Brand loyalty in pour over is often really filter compatibility . The Origami Dripper is unusual in accepting both shapes.

sections.h: 💡 Why Brands Matter to the Cup
sections.t: Gear is not neutral. A dripper's Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers, its Dripper Materials and Heat Retention, a kettle's spout, and a scale's responsiveness all change how a brew behaves. The brands above earned their place because their design choices produce repeatable results — which is why competition brewers at The World Brewers Cup reach for the same short list. [!warning] Gear is not skill A 300 dollar kettle and a Bluetooth scale will not fix bad Grind Size for Pour Over or Pouring Technique. The community's recurring lesson: spend first on a good grinder and fresh beans, not on the prettiest dripper.

Pour over culture is carried by two overlapping groups of people: the professional barista behind the café bar, and the home brewer chasing the same cup on a kitchen counter. The remarkable thing about specialty coffee is how porous that line is — professional technique flows freely to amateurs, and obsessive hobbyists sometimes out nerd the pros.

A barista is a skilled service professional, not just someone who pushes a button. In a serious café, the role spans espresso, milk, customer education, and increasingly manual filter brewing at the Coffee Shops and Cafe Culture. Doing it well means mastering Dialing In Grind Size, reading the The Drawdown, managing Agitation and Turbulence, and tasting critically enough to adjust — all under time pressure, repeatably, drink after drink. [!note] Brewing as performance The barista's craft peaks at competition. The World Brewers Cup turns filter brewing into a judged, scripted performance — and many competitors are working baristas refining technique they then teach to everyone else.

The home brewer is the engine of the The Modern Pour Over Renaissance. Pour over is cheap to start and deep enough to obsess over, which made it the perfect hobby for a generation that learned from the internet. Home brewers cluster into recognizable types: Brewer type Mindset The morning ritualist Wants one reliable, repeatable cup The tinkerer Chases variables — grind, water, The Brew Ratio\, pour The gear collector Owns more Coffee Gear Brands\ than they can use The data nerd Measures Refractometers and Measuring Extraction\ with a refractometer Most brewers drift between these as they learn.

sections.h: 🔁 How the Two Groups Feed Each Other
sections.t: The flow runs both ways. Professionals publish recipes and techniques; home brewers adopt, test, and remix them. Figures who straddle both worlds — like Coffee Online — Communities and Content, a working coffee professional and prolific educator — collapse the distance entirely, teaching café grade technique to anyone with a Hario V60 and a Scales, Timers, and Servers. [!tip] The community's core advice Almost every experienced brewer, pro or amateur, gives the same beginner guidance: buy fresh beans, grind right before brewing on a decent Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders, weigh everything, and change one variable at a time. The craft is less about secret tricks than disciplined repetition. [!warning] Gatekeeping and snobbery Coffee communities can tip into elitism — dismissing dark roasts, milk drinks, or affordable gear. The healthiest corners reject this: good coffee is whatever the drinker enjoys, brewed with a little care.

How a roaster buys green coffee is one of the most contested topics in the industry — partly because the stakes are real (farmer livelihoods) and partly because the language is loose. "Direct trade," "Fair Trade," "relationship coffee," and "single farm" appear constantly on Notable Roasters bags, but they mean very different things, and some are barely defined at all. This note maps the main sourcing models and their real world limits.

Model What it means Main limitation Commodity ("C price") Coffee bought as a fungible good at the futures market rate Price often below cost of production; no quality incentive Fair Trade Certified minimum price + premium, via co ops Certification fees; minimums can lag real costs; rewards co op membership, not quality Direct Trade Roaster buys directly from a producer, often above market Unregulated term — no shared standard; claims hard to verify Relationship Coffee Multi year partnerships emphasizing trust and feedback Depends entirely on the buyer's integrity

Direct trade is the most romanticized — and the most slippery — term in coffee. [!warning] An unregulated label Unlike Fair Trade, "direct trade" has no single governing body, audit, or legal definition . Each roaster defines it however they like. At its best it means transparent, well above market prices and genuine farm relationships; at its worst it is a marketing phrase attached to coffee bought through the same importers as everyone else. A claim is only as good as the roaster's published evidence. Strong direct trade programs publish what they pay (FOB and farm gate prices), name the producers, and return year after year. The community increasingly demands this price transparency precisely because the label alone proves nothing.

Even well intentioned buyers face structural friction. Coffee passes through many hands — producer, mill, exporter, importer, roaster — and the small scale farmers who grow most of the world's coffee rarely have the leverage to capture much of the final price. Real "direct" relationships are also logistically costly, which is why even ethical roasters often work through trusted importers rather than truly direct. [!note] Certification is not a moral hierarchy Fair Trade, organic, Rainforest Alliance, and direct trade each solve different problems and miss others. A coffee with no certification can be sourced more ethically than a certified one; the label is a signal, not a verdict. Sourcing connects directly to Sustainability in Coffee — because a farm that cannot earn a living wage cannot invest in quality or survive The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta — and to the The Specialty Coffee Industry core promise that quality should reward the people who grow it.

Coffee has a sustainability problem on every axis at once: the plant is threatened by a warming climate, the farmers who grow it are squeezed by economics, and brewing it generates real waste and emissions. For a culture that prizes the craft of pour over, these are not abstract concerns — they determine whether good coffee will still exist, and at what human cost.

The flavor rich The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta species that specialty coffee depends on is also the most climate sensitive. It thrives in a narrow band of temperature, altitude, and rainfall, and warming is squeezing that band. [!warning] The shrinking coffee belt Widely cited research suggests that a large share of today's suitable Arabica land could become unsuitable by 2050 as temperatures rise, rainfall grows erratic, and pests like the coffee borer beetle and the fungus coffee leaf rust spread to higher altitudes. Estimates vary and are debated, but the direction is not: the best Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir\ are under pressure. Adaptation strategies include moving to higher elevations, breeding resilient Coffee Varietals, shade growing, and — controversially — leaning on hardier but lower scoring The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta.

Sustainability is impossible without viable livelihoods. Many smallholders earn less than the cost of production at Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing, which drives an aging farming population, abandoned farms, and a generation reluctant to inherit the work. Pressure Consequence Volatile C price below cost Farmers exit coffee or cut quality Low value captured at origin Little to reinvest in farms Aging farmers, few successors Long term supply risk Climate driven yield loss Income and stability both fall The specialty premium and Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing help at the margins, but specialty is a small fraction of global volume.

The brewing end has its own footprint: Paper filters and pods — single use Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth are compostable but still waste; metal and cloth filters cut it. Coffee cherry and pulp — Coffee Processing Methods discards huge volumes of cherry; some is now reused (cascara, fertilizer). Water and energy — Washed Process is water intensive; roasting and shipping add emissions. Milk and disposables — in cafés, dairy and single use cups often dwarf the coffee's own footprint. [!tip] What a home brewer can actually do Buy from transparent Notable Roasters\, avoid waste by The Brew Ratio\, compost grounds and filters, prefer reusable filters where you like the cup, and don't over buy gear. Small, but real. [!note] Beware greenwashing "Sustainable" is unregulated, like "Direct Trade and Coffee Sourcing\." Look for specifics — published prices, named projects, measured outcomes — not vibes.

If the café introduced people to pour over, the internet taught them to do it at home . The modern coffee renaissance is, to an unusual degree, a media phenomenon: YouTube videos, forums, blogs, and social feeds carried café grade technique into millions of kitchens, collapsing the distance between professional and amateur and turning a slow ritual into a shared global hobby.

No single channel did more to popularize pour over than that of James Hoffmann — the 2007 World Barista Champion and co founder of Notable Roasters — whose calm, rigorous videos on grinders, water, and method became the default reference for a generation of brewers. See James Hoffmann V60 Technique and James Hoffmann Ultimate AeroPress. A wave of other creators built the rest of the ecosystem: Creator Known for James Hoffmann Authoritative, research driven brewing and gear deep dives Lance Hedrick Technique obsessed tutorials emphasizing the why ; see Lance Hedrick Pour Over Technique Morgan Eckroth Barista world and latte art content that reached a mainstream audience Scott Rao (author/consultant) Technical writing whose ideas — like the The Rao Spin\ — spread online [!note] Why video changed everything Pour over is a physical skill — the pour, the swirl, the The Drawdown\. Text and photos struggle to convey it; video shows it in real time. That format match is a big reason the method exploded online rather than through books alone.

sections.h: 💬 Forums, Reddit, and the Long Tail
sections.t: Before and alongside YouTube, written communities did the deep work. Home Barista.com, Coffee Geek, and later r/Coffee and r/espresso on Reddit became searchable archives where brewers troubleshooted Common Pour Over Mistakes, debated Coffee Gear Brands, and compared Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes. These forums are where much practical, crowd tested knowledge actually lives.

sections.h: 🌐 How Online Content Drove the Renaissance
sections.t: The internet supplied the three things a craft hobby needs to spread: instruction (anyone could learn café technique for free), community (brewers found each other across the world), and demand (viewers bought the Coffee Gear Brands and beans they saw, fueling the whole The Specialty Coffee Industry). It also democratized the The World Brewers Cup that once lived only on the stage. [!warning] The influencer caveat Coffee media runs on affiliate links and sponsorships. Even principled creators face pressure to review gear favorably. Treat any single recommendation — especially for expensive equipment — with healthy skepticism, and cross check across Baristas and Home Brewers before buying. Online content is the connective tissue of modern coffee culture: it links Notable Roasters, Baristas and Home Brewers, and Coffee Gear Brands into one continuous, very online conversation.

The Reference domain is the quick lookup toolkit of this knowledge base — the shelf you reach for mid brew when you need an answer fast rather than a deep read. Where the other eleven domains teach concepts, these notes condense them into glossaries, tables, indexes, and checklists you can scan in seconds. Bookmark this page; it is the fastest way back into the rest of the vault and home to the Master Index of every note. [!tip] How to use this domain Keep a Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide open while you dial in a new coffee, lean on the Coffee Measurement and Conversion Tables when you scale a recipe, and skim the Glossary of Coffee Terms whenever a piece of jargon trips you up.

Note Use it when… Glossary of Coffee Terms a term is unfamiliar — bloom, drawdown, EY, TDS, channeling Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide a cup tastes wrong and you need a fix fast Master Index you want the whole vault, organized and linked Brew Method Comparison Table choosing a brewer or comparing geometries Coffee Measurement and Conversion Tables scaling ratios, converting grams, ml, °C and °F Recommended Reading and Resources you want books, channels, and courses to go deeper Frequently Asked Questions a beginner question needs a one paragraph answer Pour Over Quick Start Checklist brewing your first good cup, step by step

Reference is a connective tissue domain, so it links outward more than any other. The Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide sends you to Grind Size for Pour Over, Water Temperature for Brewing, and Under Extraction and Over Extraction; the Brew Method Comparison Table feeds directly off Equipment and Drippers; and the Frequently Asked Questions note threads through nearly every fundamental, from The Brew Ratio to Coffee Freshness and Degassing. If you are brand new, start with What Is Pour Over Coffee and How to Use This Knowledge Base, then come back here. [!note] A reference shelf, not a reading order Unlike the book like flow of the earlier domains, these notes are meant to be jumped into out of order. Treat them as appendices to the main text — and let the Master Index be your table of contents for the entire vault.

A working brewer's A to Z. Each entry is a short, plain language definition; where a term has its own full note, the term links to it. For pronunciation and flavor vocabulary used at the cupping table, pair this with Describing Coffee — The Vocabulary and the The Coffee Flavor Wheel. [!tip] Reading this list Bold terms are the headword; a → points you to the note that goes deep. Skim once, then return whenever a recipe or note uses a word you do not recognize.

Acidity — the bright, tangy, often fruity quality of a coffee; a desirable trait in filter brewing, not a defect. → Acidity in Coffee Agitation — physical disturbance of the coffee bed by pouring, stirring, or swirling, which speeds extraction and evens it out. → Agitation and Turbulence Alkalinity (KH) — water's capacity to buffer (neutralize) acids; high alkalinity dulls perceived acidity. → Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity Arabica — the premium coffee species behind nearly all specialty coffee. → The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta Astringency — a drying, mouth puckering sensation, usually a sign of over extraction or excess fines.

Blade grinder — a grinder that chops beans with a spinning blade, producing uneven particles; avoid for pour over. → Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders Bloom — the initial wetting of the grounds that releases trapped CO₂ before the main pour. → The Bloom Body — the weight or texture of the coffee in the mouth, from tea like to syrupy. → Sweetness and Body Boulders — the largest particles in a grind, which under extract relative to the rest. → Fines and Boulders Burr grinder — a grinder that crushes beans between two abrasive surfaces for a uniform grind. → Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders Bypass — water that slips down the filter walls without passing through the coffee bed. → Bypass and Channeling

Channeling — water carving a fast path through the bed, causing uneven extraction. → Channeling and Uneven Extraction Conical dripper — a V shaped, single hole dripper such as the Hario V60. → Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers Contact time — the total time water is in contact with the coffee. → Brew Time and Total Contact Time Cupping — the standardized protocol for tasting and scoring coffee. → Cupping

Degassing — the slow release of CO₂ from roasted beans as they age. → Coffee Freshness and Degassing Dialing in — adjusting variables (usually grind) to optimize a given coffee. → Dialing In Grind Size Dose — the mass of dry coffee used, in grams. Drawdown — the final phase as the last water drains through the bed. → The Drawdown

Extraction — the dissolving of soluble compounds out of the grounds and into the brew. → The Science of Extraction Extraction yield (EY) — the percentage of the dry coffee mass dissolved into the cup; 18–22% is the classic target. → Extraction Yield and Strength

Fines — the smallest particles in a grind; they extract fast and can clog the filter. → Fines and Boulders Flat bottom dripper — a wave or basket dripper with a flat bed, such as the Kalita Wave. → Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers Flow rate — how quickly water drains through dripper and bed.

General hardness (GH) — the dissolved calcium and magnesium in water, key to extraction. → Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity Gooseneck kettle — a kettle with a swan neck spout for precise, slow pouring. → Gooseneck Kettles Grind size — the coarseness of the ground coffee, the master variable for flow and extraction. → Grind Size for Pour Over

Immersion — brewing with the coffee fully steeped in water, as in a French Press. Percolation — brewing with fresh water continuously passing through the bed, as in pour over. Pulse pouring — adding water in discrete bursts rather than one continuous stream. → Pulse vs Continuous Pouring

Ratio — the proportion of water to coffee, e.g. 1:16. → The Brew Ratio Refractometer — a device that reads TDS to calculate extraction. → Refractometers and Measuring Extraction Robusta — the hardier, more bitter coffee species. → The Coffee Plant — Arabica and Robusta Roast level — how far beans were roasted, from light to dark. → Roast Levels for Pour Over

Slurry — the mix of water and grounds during brewing. Solubility — how readily a compound dissolves; it changes through the brew. → Solubility and What Dissolves Strength — the concentration of dissolved solids, perceived as intensity. → Extraction Yield and Strength TDS (total dissolved solids) — the concentration of dissolved coffee in the cup, used to compute extraction. → Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) Terroir — the environmental signature a growing region imparts. → Coffee Growing Regions and Terroir Third wave — the movement treating coffee as an artisanal, origin driven product. → First, Second, and Third Wave Coffee Turbulence — agitation within the slurry that mixes and evens extraction. → Agitation and Turbulence

Under extraction — too little dissolved, giving sour, sharp, hollow cups. → Under Extraction and Over Extraction Over extraction — too much dissolved, giving bitter, astringent, drying cups. → Under Extraction and Over Extraction Varietal — a genetic variety of the coffee plant, e.g. Geisha, Bourbon. → Coffee Varietals Washed process — a processing method that ferments and rinses fruit off the seed. → Washed Process

Something tastes off. Before you blame the beans, work through the symptom tables below — most bad cups trace back to one or two variables. The golden rule: change one thing at a time so you learn what each adjustment does. For the theory behind why these fixes work, lean on Under Extraction and Over Extraction and The Science of Extraction. [!warning] Diagnose before you adjust Sour and bitter are the two anchor flavors. Sour usually means under extracted; bitter usually means over extracted. Almost every fix below nudges extraction up or down by changing grind, time, temperature, or agitation.

Symptom Likely cause Fix Sour / sharp / lemony Under extraction Grind finer, raise water temp, slow the pour, or increase contact time → Grind Size for Pour Over Bitter / harsh Over extraction Grind coarser, lower water temp, shorten contact time, reduce agitation → Water Temperature for Brewing Astringent / drying Over extraction or excess fines Grind coarser, reduce agitation, check for stalled drawdown → Fines and Boulders Weak / watery / thin Low strength Use less water per gram (tighter ratio) or grind slightly finer → The Brew Ratio Too intense / muddy High strength Use more water per gram, or scale the recipe up evenly → Recipe Variables and Cup Outcomes Flat / dull / lifeless Low extraction or poor water Check water hardness and alkalinity; raise temp → Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity Both sour AND bitter Uneven extraction Reduce channeling: even the bed, pour gently, level the grounds → Channeling and Uneven Extraction

Symptom Likely cause Fix Drawdown too fast Grind too coarse, or too much bypass Grind finer; pour in the center, not down the walls → Bypass and Channeling Drawdown too slow / stalled Grind too fine, too many fines, or clogged filter Grind coarser; reduce agitation; pre wet the filter → Pre Wetting the Filter Water pooling on top Bed clogged by fines Coarsen grind; consider a coarser grinding burr → Particle Distribution and Uniformity Coffee draining unevenly Channeling or an uneven bed Swirl or tap to settle the bed before drawdown → The Drawdown Big crater in the bed Pour too aggressive or off center Pour more gently in slow concentric circles → Pouring Technique

Symptom Likely cause Fix Little or no bloom Stale beans (degassed) Use fresher coffee; bloom is CO₂ escaping → Coffee Freshness and Degassing Huge, foamy bloom that overflows Very fresh, gassy beans Use a larger bloom water volume and a longer bloom → The Bloom Papery / cardboard taste Filter not rinsed Pre wet the paper before brewing → Pre Wetting the Filter Muted aroma Old or improperly stored coffee Buy smaller, fresher; store airtight → Reading a Coffee Bag Label [!tip] Still stuck? Reset to a known good baseline Brew The Standard V60 Recipe at 1:16, 92–94°C, with a medium grind, and change exactly one variable per brew. A consistent baseline turns guesswork into Dialing In Grind Size.

sections.h: When It Is Not the Brew
sections.t: Sometimes the cup is fine and the coffee simply is not for you — a dark, smoky roast will never taste like a bright washed Ethiopian no matter how you brew it. Check whether the issue is technique or Single Origin vs Blends and roast. And rule out the usual suspects from Common Pour Over Mistakes before going deeper.

Nine common brewers, side by side. Pour over (percolation) and immersion methods extract differently and reward different goals — clarity versus body, control versus convenience. Use this table to pick a brewer or to understand why two devices treat the same coffee so differently. For the underlying split, see What Is Pour Over Coffee and the Brew Method Family Tree. [!note] Percolation vs immersion Percolation (V60, Kalita, Chemex, Origami, Melitta, OXO) passes fresh water through the bed for a cleaner cup. Immersion (Clever, French Press, and AeroPress in steep mode) steeps the coffee for a heavier, more forgiving one.

Brewer Type Filter Grind Flow Body / Clarity Difficulty Best for Hario V60 Conical percolation Paper (cone) Medium fine Fast, pour controlled Light body, high clarity High Bright single origins, control Kalita Wave Flat bottom percolation Paper (wave) Medium Moderate, restricted Balanced body \ forgiving Medium Repeatable, even brews Chemex Conical percolation Thick paper bond Medium coarse Slow Very clean, light body Medium high Crystal clarity, carafe serving Origami Dripper Conical/flat percolation Cone or wave paper Medium Adjustable by filter Clarity, flexible Medium Versatility across filters Melitta and Wedge Drippers Wedge percolation Paper (wedge) Medium Restricted (1–3 holes) Fuller, forgiving Low Easy, classic home drip The Clever Dripper Immersion + draw Paper (cone) Medium coarse Valve released Full body, clean finish Very low Hands off, beginner friendly AeroPress Immersion / pressure Paper or metal disc Fine to medium Plunger forced Bold, low clarity (variable) Low Travel, single cups, experimentation OXO Rapid Brewer Flat bottom percolation Paper (flat) Medium Auto modulated Even, balanced Very low Set and forget consistency French Press Immersion Metal mesh Coarse Steep + plunge Heavy body, low clarity Low Rich, textured, full immersion [!tip] Reading "difficulty" Difficulty reflects how much pour technique affects the result. The Hario V60 punishes sloppy pouring; the The Clever Dripper, OXO Rapid Brewer, and French Press mostly forgive it. If you are new, start forgiving and graduate to the V60 once your Pouring Technique is steady.

If you chase clarity and brightness , reach for a conical percolator like the Hario V60 or Chemex; if you want consistency with less skill , a flat bottom or immersion brewer like the Kalita Wave, OXO Rapid Brewer, or The Clever Dripper is kinder. Body lovers gravitate to the French Press or a metal filtered AeroPress. Geometry drives much of this — see Conical vs Flat Bottom Drippers and Dripper Materials and Heat Retention — and the Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth note explains how filter choice shifts body independently of the dripper.

These columns describe typical behavior, not destiny. Grind, ratio, and pour can push any brewer toward a different cup — a coarsely ground, gently poured V60 can taste surprisingly soft, and a fine AeroPress recipe can taste remarkably clean. Treat the table as a starting map, then dial in with Dialing In Grind Size and the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide.

The numbers you need at the scale, in one place. Brew by weight , not volume — a tablespoon of coffee varies wildly with bean density and grind, but grams never lie. These tables turn ratios, doses, and temperatures into the exact figures you punch into a recipe. For the reasoning behind ratios, read The Brew Ratio; for why grams beat scoops, see Scales, Timers, and Servers. [!tip] The one rule that simplifies everything Water is 1 gram per millilitre at brewing temperature, so 1 ml of water ≈ 1 g . That means a scale and a kettle are all the measuring tools you ever need.

sections.h: Brew Ratios → Water for a Given Dose
sections.t: Water (g) = dose (g) × ratio. Common pour over ratios run 1:15 (stronger) to 1:17 (lighter). Dose (g) 1:15 1:16 1:17 12 g 180 g 192 g 204 g 15 g 225 g 240 g 255 g 18 g 270 g 288 g 306 g 20 g 300 g 320 g 340 g 22 g 330 g 352 g 374 g 25 g 375 g 400 g 425 g 30 g 450 g 480 g 510 g [!note] Yield is a little less than water in The coffee bed retains roughly 2 g of water per gram of dry coffee . An 18 g dose holds back 36 g, so a 288 g brew (1:16) yields 250 ml in the cup. Plan your serving size accordingly.

The filter coffee sweet spot is roughly 90–96°C . Convert with °F = (°C × 9 ⁄ 5) + 32. °C °F 85 185 88 190 90 194 92 198 93 199 94 201 96 205 100 212 (boiling at sea level) [!warning] Boiling point drops with altitude Water boils below 100°C at elevation (about 1°C lower per 300 m). At high altitude your "off boil" water is already cooler, so you may need to brew closer to boiling. See Water Temperature for Brewing and The Role of Temperature in Extraction.

A common bloom is 2–3× the dry dose in grams of water. For 18 g of coffee that is 36–54 g for the bloom pour. More gas means a bigger, longer bloom — see The Bloom and Coffee Freshness and Degassing. Dose Bloom (2×) Bloom (3×) 12 g 24 g 36 g 15 g 30 g 45 g 18 g 36 g 54 g 25 g 50 g 75 g

Where to go when this vault leaves you wanting more. The coffee world is rich with excellent books, channels, and communities; below are the ones most worth a brewer's time, lightly annotated. None of this replaces brewing daily — but good sources shorten the path from curious to competent. For the people and platforms behind much of it, see Coffee Online — Communities and Content and Baristas and Home Brewers.

The SCA Coffee Skills Program offers structured Brewing and Sensory modules for those who want formal grounding, while Barista Hustle runs accessible online courses on water and extraction. Neither is required to brew beautifully — but if you enjoy systematic learning, they pair well with the Science and Extraction and Tasting and Sensory domains here. [!tip] How to use these without overwhelm Pick one book and one channel, then brew alongside them. Theory sticks when you taste it the same day. Loop back to the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide whenever a new idea produces a worse cup — the fundamentals here still apply. [!note] On chasing the new Coffee content moves fast, and "the best new method" is often last year's method renamed. Anchor on principles — Extraction Yield and Strength, Water Chemistry — Hardness and Alkalinity, Grind Size for Pour Over — and treat every recipe as a variation on those.

The questions almost every new pour over brewer asks, answered concisely — with a link to go deeper. If your question is "why does my coffee taste sour/bitter/weak," skip straight to the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide. Otherwise, start here. [!tip] Short answers, deep links Each answer is a paragraph; the bracketed link takes you to the full note. Treat this as a fast index into the rest of the vault.

What gear do I actually need to start? A dripper, paper filters, a way to heat water, a scale, and — ideally — a burr grinder. A gooseneck kettle helps but is not mandatory at first. The Pour Over Quick Start Checklist walks the whole setup, and Equipment and Drippers covers each piece in depth. Which dripper should I buy first? For control and clarity, the Hario V60; for forgiving consistency, the Kalita Wave or OXO Rapid Brewer; for hands off ease, The Clever Dripper. The Brew Method Comparison Table lays out the trade offs so you can match a brewer to your temperament. Do I really need a scale? Yes — brewing by weight is the single biggest upgrade to consistency, because volume scoops vary with grind and bean density. See Scales, Timers, and Servers and the Coffee Measurement and Conversion Tables.

How fresh should my beans be? Best from about 4 days to 4 weeks off roast; too fresh and they gas wildly, too old and they go flat. Buy dated bags and use them within a few weeks → Coffee Freshness and Degassing and Reading a Coffee Bag Label. What roast is best for pour over? Light to medium roasts shine, showcasing origin character and acidity that filter brewing reveals. Dark roasts work but taste roastier and flatter → Roast Levels for Pour Over and Light Roast and Specialty Coffee. Should I buy a grinder before a fancy dripper? Almost always yes. A good burr grinder improves every brew more than a premium dripper does, because grind quality drives even extraction → Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders.

What ratio should I use? Start at 1:16 (e.g. 18 g coffee to 288 g water) and adjust to taste — tighter for strength, wider for a lighter cup → The Brew Ratio. How hot should the water be? Around 90–96°C. Lighter roasts like it hotter, darker roasts cooler. Off the boil after a short rest is a fine default → Water Temperature for Brewing. Does water quality matter that much? More than most beginners expect — minerals drive extraction and very hard or very soft water both hurt the cup. Filtered water is a safe start → Why Water Matters and TDS and Mineral Content. My coffee tastes sour. What do I do? Sour usually means under extracted: grind finer, brew hotter, or slow down. Bitter is the opposite → Under Extraction and Over Extraction and the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide. Why does my brew drain too slowly and stall? Usually too fine a grind or too many fines clogging the filter; coarsen up and reduce agitation → The Drawdown and Fines and Boulders. Is pour over stronger than other methods? No — strength is set by the ratio, not the method. Pour over tends to taste cleaner , not stronger → What Is Pour Over Coffee.

Everything you need to brew a genuinely good first cup, in order, with nothing skipped. Print it, tape it to your kettle, and follow it top to bottom. The recipe baked into this checklist is The Standard V60 Recipe — once it feels routine, branch out using the rest of the vault. New to the whole idea? Read What Is Pour Over Coffee first. [!example] The one line recipe 18 g coffee · 288 g water · 1:16 · 93°C · medium grind · 3:00 total. Everything below is just doing that well.

[ ] A dripper — a Hario V60 is the classic starting point → Equipment and Drippers [ ] The matching paper filters → Coffee Filters — Paper, Metal, and Cloth [ ] A burr grinder (skip the blade grinder) → Burr Grinders vs Blade Grinders [ ] A scale that reads to 0.1 g, ideally with a timer → Scales, Timers, and Servers [ ] A kettle — a gooseneck makes pouring easier but is optional → Gooseneck Kettles [ ] Fresh coffee, roasted within the last few weeks → Coffee Freshness and Degassing [ ] Good water — filtered is a safe default → Filtered, Bottled, and Tap Water

1. [ ] Heat water to about 93°C (just off the boil, rested 30 s) → Water Temperature for Brewing 2. [ ] Weigh 18 g of beans → The Brew Ratio 3. [ ] Grind to medium (like coarse table salt) → Grind Size for Pour Over 4. [ ] Put the filter in the dripper and rinse it with hot water; discard the rinse → Pre Wetting the Filter 5. [ ] Add the grounds, level the bed, and place the dripper on your cup or server on the scale 6. [ ] Tare the scale to zero

7. [ ] Bloom: pour 50 g of water to wet all the grounds, then wait 30–45 s → The Bloom 8. [ ] First pours: add water in slow concentric circles up to 150 g → Pouring Technique 9. [ ] Final pours: continue to 288 g total, keeping the bed gently submerged → Pulse vs Continuous Pouring 10. [ ] Drawdown: let it drain; aim to finish around 2:45–3:15 → The Drawdown 11. [ ] Give the dripper a gentle swirl or tap to settle the bed if needed

12. [ ] Let it cool slightly, then taste → How to Taste Coffee 13. [ ] Too sour? Grind finer or brew hotter next time 14. [ ] Too bitter? Grind coarser or brew cooler next time 15. [ ] Change one variable per brew and keep notes → Dialing In Grind Size [!tip] Your safety net If a cup goes wrong, the Pour Over Troubleshooting Guide turns the flavor into a fix. And the Frequently Asked Questions note answers the "wait, why?" moments that come up early. [!warning] The two most common beginner mistakes Grinding with a blade grinder and brewing by volume instead of weight. Fix those two and your coffee leaps forward — more in Common Pour Over Mistakes.
